Folding is too tight given the price (I only need to call 200 more), and a light raise to 500 with this raggy holding is unnecessary — it bloats the pot OOP against an aggressive opponent. I’ll call 200 more (300 total in the pot including my BB) to see a flop cheaply. Postflop plan: play cautiously OOP — check/fold to large continuation bets if I miss, but continue or probe if I pair an 8 or 5 or pick up a realistic draw. Avoid committing more chips without a clear improvement.

Heads-up on a three-diamond flop and I hold 8h5s — I have no diamonds and only an inside straight draw to any 7 (4 outs). That’s roughly 8.5% to hit the turn and ~16.5% by the river, so my raw equity is low. Flash opened pre and will c-bet this board a lot, and his range contains many diamonds (so he often has the nut/near-nut draw or a made hand). I’m OOP and don’t want to inflate the pot with a very marginal draw and no blockers, so I’ll check and realize my equity. Plan: check-fold to a large bet, call very small bets for pot odds/implied odds, and if I hit my straight (7) on the turn/river play for value or pot-control depending on action.

I have Ace-high with the Jack of Diamonds, giving me the Jack-high flush draw on a monotone diamond board. Since Minni checked to me as the pre-flop aggressor, I will put in a standard continuation bet of 300 (half pot). This allows me to take down the pot immediately if she doesn't have a piece of the board or a diamond, and builds the pot for when I potentially improve my hand on later streets.

Fold. On a three-diamond flop (9d-4d-6d) with 8h5s I have no diamonds and only a gutshot to a 7 (4 outs) — ~8.5% to hit the turn and ~16.5% to hit by the river. Flash's 300 into a 900 pot means I must call 300 to win 1,200 total (pot odds ≈ 25%). My realistic equity versus his c-bet range (which likely contains many diamonds, made pairs and draws) is well below the 25% needed, and many of my pair-outs (pairing the 8 or 5) would still be second-best. Raising as a bluff is unattractive too: I’m out of position, have no diamond blockers, and Flash shows a tendency to continue or call down. Preserve the 6k stack for better, more profitable spots — fold.

Heads‑up on the button (SB) with 4♦7♦ — a marginal but playable suited hand in position. Pot is 150 and I only need to put in 50 more (total committed 100) to see a flop; with deep stacks and the big blind historically defending and 3‑betting often, open‑raising here risks a costly 3‑bet or a difficult pot to play out of position. Calling preserves my position advantage, gives good implied odds for suited connectors, and keeps the pot controllable. Plan: see the flop in position, proceed with pairs/draws or take free equity; fold to heavy aggression with no equity. Action: call to 100 total.

I am in the Big Blind with J9o. Minni, on the button/small blind, has just completed the blind. J9o is a respectable hand in a heads-up situation, but since Minni will have position on me post-flop, I prefer to take the free flop and see how the board develops rather than building a pot out of position with a marginal holding.

I have Jack-high with a gutshot straight draw (needing a Queen to complete a 9-10-J-Q-K straight). The board is King-Ten-Three rainbow. Since Minni limped pre-flop and the pot is small, I will check to see how she reacts. Out of position, checking allows me to see a free turn card if she checks back or evaluate her bet size if she leads out.

Flop K♦ 3♠ T♣ — my 4♦7♦ has no pair and only a very weak backdoor diamond flush (need both turn and river). In heads‑up position this is a dry board and Flash checked, so a small continuation bet is the highest EV play: it leverages my position, applies fold equity against a wide checking range, and costs very little relative to the stacks. The table UI enforces a 100 minimum, so I’ll fire the min‑bet (100) to either take down the 200‑chip pot now or keep the pot manageable if called. Plan: if he folds I collect; if he calls I’ll check the turn with no improvement and re‑evaluate; if he raises I’ll fold — my hand has too little equity to continue.

I have Jack-high with a gutshot straight draw (any Queen gives me the straight K-Q-J-T-9). Minni's bet of 100 into a 200-chip pot gives me 3:1 pot odds. Calling 100 to win 300 is a reasonable price to pay with a gutshot, especially since her pre-flop limp suggests her range is quite wide and potentially weak. I will call to see the turn.

I have completed a King-high straight (9-10-J-Q-K) on the turn. Since I have been playing this hand passively (checking pre-flop and check-calling the flop), checking again is the best way to disguise my monster hand and induce Minni to continue betting her bluffs or marginal pairs. If she bets, I will have the option to either raise for value immediately or call to trap her for a larger bet on the river.

I c-bet the flop and was called; the turn Q♥ is a blank for my 4♦7♦ — no pair, no straight or flush draw (my backdoor diamond route is dead with only one card to come). Villain's flop-calling range is likely Kx, Qx, Tx and some broadways, and the queen on the turn only strengthens that range and cuts my fold equity. With essentially zero showdown value and only a few low pairing outs, betting here would be a thin bluff risking chips vs a deep-stacked, sticky opponent. I will check to control the pot and preserve my stack; plan is to fold to any significant bet on the turn and re-evaluate the river if checked to me (consider a river bluff only if the card and opponent behavior make it favorable).

On this river, the Ace completes a possible straight for anyone holding a Jack. I hold the Jack of Spades, giving me the Ace-high straight (10-J-Q-K-A), which is currently the nuts as there are no possible flushes or full houses. Minni checked back the turn after betting the flop, suggesting she might have a marginal made hand or was drawing. I will bet 300 into the 400-chip pot to extract value from any Ace, King, or two-pair she might have, or to split the pot if she also holds a Jack.

I checked the turn with no improvement and called the flop with a speculative 4♦7♦. On the river (Kd 3s Tc Qh Ad) Flash bets 300 into a 700 pot. My hand has no pair, no straight and no realistic flush (only two diamonds on board), so my absolute showdown equity is extremely low — only a few pairing outs at best. Pot odds: calling 300 into a 1000 pot (pre-call) means I need ~23% equity to break even, which I don’t have versus a reasonable value-heavy river-betting range (Kx, Qx, Ax, Tx and Jx straights) that fits his line (check-call flop, check turn, lead river). A bluff-raise or call here would be a thin, negative-EV play; preserving stack and folding to this river bet is the correct play.

I am on the button with 7d 6d, which is a strong suited connector hand in heads-up play. With a significant chip lead, I want to maintain aggression. I will raise to 300 to build the pot and exert pressure on Minni.

Heads-up to a button open to 300. I hold 9♠5♣ — a weak offsuit hand with limited 3‑bet/raise equity and poor postflop playability out of position. The pot is 400 and it’s 200 to call, giving ~2:1 pot odds (need ≈33% equity). Folding is too tight versus a wide heads‑up opening; 3‑betting light would bloat the pot and is suboptimal with this hand. I’ll defend the big blind and call to see a flop, keep the pot manageable, and reassess based on texture and opponent action.

Flop: 7♥ 8♣ 9♣ — I hold 9♠5♣: top pair but a very weak kicker and a backdoor club. Heads‑up to a button open, Flash’s range is wide (broadways, suited connectors, small pairs, and many hands with 6/10 that have straight potential here). The board is connected and two‑toned so there are straight and single‑card club draws to charge/protect against. Pot is 600 and I act first (out of position).

Objective: extract value from worse pairs/7s/8s and charge straight/club draws while keeping the pot reasonable with a marginal kicker. A c‑bet is standard; I want a size big enough to deny equity to draws but not so large I fold out worse hands or commit my stack unnecessarily. I choose ~2/3 pot.

Action plan: Bet 400 to charge draws and get value from worse hands. If Villain calls I’ll re‑evaluate on turn (continue vs blank turns, proceed cautiously vs completed straights or heavy aggression). If Villain check‑raises big I’ll give credit to straights/sets and fold (or fold large portions) because my kicker is poor and I’m OOP; I’ll only continue to small/medium raises with specific reads.

On a very connected flop of 7h-8c-9c, I have bottom pair and an open-ended straight draw (6-7-8-9). Minni has led out for 400 into a 600 pot. This 'donk bet' often represents a strong made hand or a significant draw on this texture. While I have good equity with my straight draw and a pair, raising into such a wet board against a lead is risky as it may induce a shove from her relatively short stack. Calling allows me to realize my equity and see a turn card while keeping the pot manageable. I will call and reassess on the turn.

I hold 9♠5♣ — top pair with a very weak kicker. The turn 3♣ makes the board 7♥‑8♣‑9♣‑3♣: there are now three clubs on board, so any opponent holding two clubs has completed a flush; single‑club combos have strong 4‑card flush draws. Flash called my sizable flop c‑bet, so his calling range is wide and contains many hands that now beat me (sets, better 9x, made straights like JT/10‑6, and two‑club combos that may have just made a flush). Pot is 1,400 and my stack is 5,175 — I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position with a marginal kicker and risk a check‑raise or large shove. I’ll control the pot and gain information by checking. Plan: call small–medium turn bets to realize equity (I’ll call roughly up to ~½‑2/3 pot sized bets), but fold to very large raises/jams that represent completed straights/flushes. If Flash checks back I keep the pot manageable and re‑evaluate the river for a small value bet on safe runouts or a showdown.

On the turn, the 3 of clubs completes a potential flush. After Minni led into me on the flop and then checked this turn card, she is showing either caution regarding the flush or a missed draw. While I have bottom pair and an open-ended straight draw, my hand has some showdown value and good equity to improve. Checking back allows me to see a free river card and control the size of the pot, which is sensible given the wet board and the potential for Minni to be trapping or holding a flush. I will check to realize my equity.

Pot is 1,400 and I have top pair (9♠5♣) with a very weak kicker. I bet 400 on the flop and was called; both of us checked the turn after the 3♣ completed the club draw — that check‑check line reduces (but does not eliminate) the probability Flash has a made flush or a monster. The river pairs the board (3♠) which doesn’t meaningfully improve many villain combos and actually makes trips possible but still uncommon. I want to extract thin value from worse one‑pair hands (7x/8x and some weaker 9x) while keeping the pot controlled and folding out missed draws and air. A small value bet ~1/3 pot (~400) is the right play: it’s big enough to get called by worse hands but small enough that I’m not overcommitted with a poor kicker. If Flash raises large I’ll give credit to a flush/straight/trips and fold.

On the river, the board has paired with a second trey, and I have two pair, sevens and treys, with a nine kicker. Minni has bet 400 into a pot of 1400. After leading the flop and checking the turn (where the flush came in), this small river bet could be a thin value bet with a better pair (like an 8 or 9) or a missed straight draw (like 5-6 or 10-J) attempting to buy the pot. Given the pot odds of 5.5 to 1, I only need to be correct about 15% of the time for a call to be profitable. My hand has enough showdown value to act as a bluff catcher in this spot, so I will call.

Heads-up on the button with K♥9♠ — a strong, playable hand in this spot and I have postflop position. Completing (calling 50) surrenders initiative and gives the big blind good odds; folding is too tight. I want fold equity and to take control, so I’ll open to a standard 3x BB. Raising to 300 (300 total committed) pressures weaker hands, builds the pot when I have the initiative, and keeps my postflop options open. If Flash three-bets big I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold to a large, polarized 3-bet); otherwise I’ll C-bet favorable flops and use position to outplay him.

I am holding Q2o in the big blind. Minni, who is on the button/SB, has raised to 300. While I have a massive stack advantage, Q2o is a very weak hand with poor playability out of position. The pot odds are 2:1, requiring roughly 25% equity, but Q2o is easily dominated and difficult to realize equity with post-flop. I will choose to fold and wait for a better spot.

In a heads-up situation from the button, I want to maintain a high level of aggression, especially with a significant chip lead. Qh 5c is a marginal hand, but it is strong enough to open for a raise to put pressure on the Big Blind and potentially take down the pot pre-flop or play the hand in position.

Heads-up vs the Button (Flash) who opened to 300 from the SB. I have A♣T♠ and ~70BB effective. Calling here is the best mix of EV and pot-control: the open is a standard 3x, and A‑T offsuit is strong enough to continue but not so strong that I want to commit a big 3‑bet out of position. A minimum raise to 500 is a weak sizing that won’t buy enough fold equity, and a larger 3‑bet would needlessly bloat a pot when I’ll still be out of position postflop. Pot is 400 and I only need to put in 200 more (300 total committed) — about 3:1 immediate odds — and I retain the ability to realize equity on many flops (top pair, broadway draws, straight/flush possibilities). Plan: call to see a flop; if I pair the Ace or get strong draws I’ll continue, otherwise proceed cautiously and fold to heavy, polarized aggression on unfavorable textures.

Check. I'm out of position with A♣T♠ on a very dry 7♦-4♣-3♦ flop. I have only an overcard (and a useful Ace blocker) but no pair or meaningful draws, so donk-leading OOP would bloat the pot with a marginal hand. Flash's SB open is wide, so many of his hands missed this texture — checking preserves pot control, lets me call reasonable continuation bets and induce bluffs on later streets. Plan: check and call small-to-medium c-bets (~1/4–2/3 pot) to realize equity; if I pair my Ace or pick up a strong draw I'll get aggressive on the turn; fold to large, polarized aggression if I haven’t improved and odds are poor.

On a low, somewhat connected board of 7-4-3, I have Queen-high and a backdoor straight draw with the 5. As the pre-flop aggressor, I will make a standard continuation bet of half the pot (300 chips). This puts pressure on Minni's likely range of overcards and tests if she connected with the low board. If she calls or raises, I can re-evaluate on the turn, but a bet here often takes down the pot immediately.

Heads-up vs the SB on a very dry 7♦-4♣-3♦ flop. I have A♣T♠ — two overcards with an Ace blocker and reasonable showdown value in heads-up. Flash's 300 is a standard ~1/3-pot c-bet and his range will include a lot of missed broadways, weak pairs and bluffs. Calling is the best line: I get ~300 to win ~1,200 (about 4:1, so I need ~25% equity), and A‑T has sufficient equity plus the chance to improve. Raising OOP with only overcards is thin (little fold equity and leaves me out of position), and folding surrenders too much. Plan: call to keep the pot controlled and see the turn; if I hit an Ace/T or a strong draw I'll get aggressive, but I'll fold to large polarized turn aggression if I haven't improved.

Turn: 7♦-4♣-3♦-4♥. I hold A♣T♠ and there is no bet to me (pot 1,200). The turn pairing the board makes trips possible (any 4 in Flash’s range) and strengthens many of his value hands (7x, 4x, 3x). I have only Ace-high with an Ace blocker — not enough to bet for fold equity OOP and it would needlessly bloat a pot I’m likely behind in. Checking preserves pot control, keeps his bluffs in his range, and lets me call small-to-medium barrels where pot odds + river card equity justify it. Plan: check now; call small/medium turn bets (roughly ≤ ~1/2–2/3 pot) to realize equity and fold to large polarized bets or shoves that represent trips/full houses. If he checks back I go to showdown with Ace-high.

The turn pairs the four on the board (7d-4c-3d-4h). I currently have Queen-high and a gutshot straight draw to a six. Minni has checked to me for the second time. While I could continue the aggression, checking back here allows me to see a free river card and potentially hit my straight or improve to a pair of Queens. It also prevents me from being check-raised off my equity. Since I have some marginal showdown value against her complete misses and a draw to a strong hand, checking is a solid play to keep the pot controlled.

Board is 7♦-4♣-3♦-4♥-9♦. I hold A♣T♠. Opponent (SB) c‑bet the flop and I called; both of us checked the turn. My plan on the turn was explicitly to check back and go to showdown if he also checked, which is exactly what happened. The river brings a diamond (9♦) that completes many flush draws — a hand I do not have — and there is no bet to me. Betting here would unnecessarily bloats the pot with a marginal Ace‑high, will get called by almost all the hands that beat me (9x, 7x, any made diamond flush, pairs), and will only fold out some of his weakest missed broadway combos. Given the line and the board texture, checking to showdown preserves stack, avoids difficult river-bluffs vs a deep opponent, and is the highest EV line. I check.

On a board of 7d-4c-3d-4h-9d, the river has completed a potential diamond flush and the board is paired with fours. I have Queen-high with no diamonds. After checking the turn to see a free river, I find that Minni has checked for a third time. While Queen-high has almost no showdown value against her range (which likely includes some small pairs or Ace-high), Minni has shown some stickiness in previous hands. Attempting a bluff here to represent the flush is risky given the pot size and my desire to protect my massive chip lead. Checking back is the most disciplined play to move on to the next hand without unnecessary risk.

Heads-up on the button with K♠J♠ — a very strong, playable hand in position. I want to seize initiative, get value from worse hands, and deny equity to random hands in the big blind, so a standard 3x open is appropriate. Raising to 300 (I’ve already posted 50, so total commitment = 300) gives good fold equity, keeps the pot manageable with deep stacks (~76 BB), and preserves positional advantage for postflop play. If Flash 3-bets big I’ll fold or re-evaluate based on sizing and tendencies; if he calls I plan to c-bet favorable flops and play for value/control on coordinated boards.

I am in the big blind with 6h 4d, which is a very weak hand. Minni raised to 3x from the button. While I am getting 3:1 pot odds to call, this hand plays poorly out of position and is often dominated. Given my significant stack advantage and the stage of the match, I prefer to fold and wait for a better spot rather than defending with such a marginal holding.

In a heads-up situation with a massive stack advantage, I'll continue to play aggressively from the button. 96o is a marginal hand, but it's worth a standard open-raise to 300 to test the big blind's defense and maintain initiative.

Call to 300 (put 200 more). Heads-up the small blind's open to 300 is wide, and 3♣6♠ has some showdown equity and can flop useful pairs/straights. Pot is 400; calling 200 to contest a ~600 pot requires ~33% equity — reasonable vs a wide button opening range. Raising to 500 with this raggy hand out of position is overplaying; folding concedes too much for only 100 chips already posted as the BB. Plan: defend cheaply, evaluate flop — fold to big aggression on dry boards, continue only with pair/strong draw or good backdoor equity.

I called preflop to see a cheap flop; flop (T T 3) gives me only a weak pair of threes on a paired, fairly dry board. Out of position I have very little fold equity and betting for value is thin because any Ten or many overcards beat me, while bluffs will often get called. Best line is pot control and to let the button act first — check and evaluate. Plan if Flash bets: call small, non‑committal bets (roughly ≤ ~1/3–1/2 pot) given his wide opening range, but fold to large, pot‑sized or overbets which strongly represent a Ten/trips or set. So: check now and react to his bet size on the turn/river.

On this paired board of 3s-Td-Th, I have the range advantage as the pre-flop aggressor. While my 96o hasn't improved, this is a good spot for a continuation bet to take down the pot immediately, as it is difficult for Minni to have connected well with this board unless she has a Ten or a pocket pair. A small bet of roughly 40% pot should be enough to fold out her air and overcards.

I checked the dry, paired flop (T T 3) with only a weak pair of treys. Flash’s bet of 250 into a 850 pot is very small (~29% pot) and consistent with a wide continuation/float sizing rather than a definitive ten/set. Calling 250 gives me ~22.7% pot‑odds (250/(850+250)), and while my chance to hit trips by the river is only ~8.5%, my current pair has showdown value against a lot of a wide button range (Ax, Kx, Jx, missed connectors, bluffs). Raising out of position with such a marginal hand is unnecessary and will often get me exploited or commit me to tough decisions; folding relinquishes too much vs this small sizing. Plan: call to keep the pot small, re‑evaluate on the turn — call small/medium bets and fold to pot‑sized/overbets that strongly represent a Ten/set; proceed aggressively only if I improve or see clear weakness.

Turn: I just improved to a full house (3♣ + 3♠3♦ on board with T♣T♦) — a very strong hand, but not the absolute nut: any Ten in Flash’s hand (trip Tens or quads) beats me. Flash’s line (button open, a small c‑bet on the paired flop) looks fairly wide, so his range contains many non‑Ten hands that I can extract from. Pot is 1,100; I want value from weaker pocket pairs and overcards that will call a modest bet while keeping the pot controlled against the relatively small fraction of his range that contains a Ten. I’ll lead for a small/medium value bet (~350–400, ~1/3–40% pot). If he folds I scoop 1,100; if he calls I re‑evaluate the river and extract more if it’s safe; if he raises pot‑sized or overbets I’ll assume a Ten/quads and proceed cautiously (fold or narrowly call depending on sizing). Betting 400 keeps pressure on worse hands, controls the pot vs the few hands that dominate me, and preserves maneuverability for river decisions.

The board has double-paired with 3s and Ts, making it very likely that Minni has some piece of it or at least a pocket pair that beats my 9-high. Her lead on the turn (a 'donk bet') after calling the flop suggests she either improved to a full house or is trying to take the pot away on a board that I likely missed. Since I have zero equity and no draw with 9-high, there is no reason to continue.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with T♦7♥. In heads-up play the button should play wide — T7o is marginal but playable in position and has reasonable equity versus a wide big-blind defending range. Raising to the minimum (to 200 total) seizes initiative, builds a pot when I have positional advantage, and can pick up the blinds outright or let me play a single-raised pot in position. If Flash 3-bets large I will fold this vulnerable hand; if called I’ll c-bet many favorable/dry flops and exercise pot control or fold to heavy resistance on scary boards. Given stack depth (≈82 BB) and opponent tendencies, a small standard open is the best long-term play here.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Eight offsuit. Minni, who is on the Button/Small Blind, has made a minimum raise to 200. Ace-Eight is a strong hand in heads-up play. While I will be out of position for the rest of the hand, I want to take control of the pot and potentially end the hand now or build a larger pot where I have the range advantage. I will 3-bet to 700.

Flash 3-bet to 700 after my min-open to 200. Heads-up on the button I should play wide and keep the post‑flop initiative when it’s profitable — T♦7♥ is marginal but playable in position. A flat call preserves my positional advantage, gives me correct pot odds (500 into a 900 pot → ~36% equity needed), and keeps the pot manageable with ~81 BB effective stacks. 4-bet bluff with T7o is too thin and folding is too nitty; plan is to see the flop, c-bet dry textures or pot‑control/check on wet boards, and fold to heavy resistance when I have little equity. Call 500 more (700 total) to realize equity and play post‑flop in position.

I have flopped Top Pair with an Ace, though my kicker (8) is relatively weak. On this board (A-K-9), I have a range advantage as the pre-flop 3-better. I will lead out with a continuation bet to get value from Kings, pocket pairs like Jacks or Tens, and various draws (like straight draws or diamond draws). This also helps protect my hand and maintain the initiative.

Heads‑up on the button with Td7h. I called the 3‑bet preflop to keep position and see a flop; flop is Ad 9h Kd and Flash leads small (500 into a 1,400 pot → pot now 1,900). Calling 500 gives me ~20.8% break‑even equity (500 / 2,400). My hand has reasonable but modest equity: ~24% to pair one of my hole cards by the river (6 outs) and ~4% to make a backdoor diamond flush (both turn and river diamonds) — combined roughly high‑20s% naive equity vs a wide range. Flash’s 3‑bet range is weighted to A/K and strong broadways, but a small c‑bet is also consistent with a wide continuation range, so there are enough bluffs/middling hands in his range to make a call profitable given the price and position. Raising here as a bluff is too thin (I lack the blockers and showdown value) and folding is too nitty given the pot odds. Plan: call 500 to realize equity, use position on the turn to either take the pot away if he slows or fold to heavy aggression if I don’t improve.

I have three of a kind (Aces) on a board of Ad-9h-Kd-Ac. My best five-card hand is AAA-K-9 (using the King and Nine from the board as my kickers since my 8 is lower). This means any Ace in Minni's hand with a kicker higher than a 9 (AT, AJ, AQ) or a full house (AK, A9, KK, 99) beats me, while lower Aces result in a chop. However, I still have a very strong hand in heads-up play and need to bet for value against her possible Kings, pocket pairs, or diamond draws. I will bet 1000 into the 2400 pot to continue building the pot and maintain initiative.

Fold. On Ad-9h-Kd-Ac I have Td7h and only 6 clean outs to improve (3 tens + 3 sevens) — roughly 6/46 ≈ 13% equity to win by the river. Facing a 1,000 chip bet into a 3,400 pot requires about 1,000/(3,400+1,000) ≈ 22.7% equity to break even, so a call is -EV. Flash’s line (preflop 3-bet, flop c-bet, turn raise) is heavily weighted to Ax, two-pair or sets that dominate me; I have poor blockers and a bluff-raise is thin against a deep, capable opponent. Preserving my ~7.1k stack and folding is the correct +EV play here.

In a heads-up situation from the Button, I have a massive stack advantage over Minni. 73s is a weak hand but playable in this position to maintain aggression and pressure the Big Blind. A standard raise to 300 allows me to take the initiative and potentially win the pot pre-flop or see a flop with control of the betting lead.

Heads-up vs a button open to 300. JcTd is a strong, playable hand against a wide opening range — good connectivity and decent equity — but it’s marginal for a 3-bet out of position. A min 3-bet to 500 has little fold equity and a larger 3-bet commits unnecessary chips to a very deep, aggressive opponent who calls big pots. I’ll keep the pot manageable, preserve stack depth for postflop play, and realize equity on flops where I can apply pressure. Plan: call preflop, evaluate flop texture and proceed aggressively on favorable broadway/connected boards or control pot/fold to heavy resistance on dry A/K high boards.

I have JcTd on Jh 6h Ks — top pair with a mediocre kicker on a K-high, two-heart flop. Flash opened wide from the button heads-up, so his range contains many Kx/AX/overcards that dominate me but also a lot of worse Jx and heart draws I can extract value from. With my stack (~68bb) vs his very deep stack, I don’t want to bloat the pot and play a huge multi-street pot out of position when many of his strong continuing actions (check-raise or large bet) will represent Kx or sets that currently beat me. Checking controls the pot size, lets him bluff/c-bet so I can call and realize equity versus draws and worse pairs, and preserves fold equity vs heavy aggression. Plan after this check: call small-to-medium c-bets and re-evaluate on the turn; fold to a large check-raise or very large bet that strongly polarizes to Kx/sets; if he checks back, look to value-bet or barre l on favorable turn cards (T or blank cards) where I improve or can credibly represent strength.

I have 7h3h on a Jh6hKs flop, giving me a heart flush draw. This is a good semi-bluffing opportunity. Minni checked to me, the pre-flop aggressor. A bet of 300 (half pot) is standard here to build the pot in case I hit my draw and to potentially win the hand immediately if she missed. Given the board texture has two Broadway cards, it favors my perceived range.

Call 300. I have JcTd — top pair with a medium kicker on Jh–6h–Ks. Flash opened wide heads-up and his 300 c-bet into a 600 pot (50% pot) is small enough to include many bluffs/draws as well as value (lots of Kx, some Jx, heart draws). Calling costs 300 to win a 1200 pot (≈25% required equity); my hand has better than ~25% equity versus his continuing range and also carries showdown value, so I want to keep the pot controlled and realize equity OOP. Raising here would bloat the pot with a mediocre kicker and put me in uncomfortable multi-street spots against Kx/sets or get called by worse hands; I’ll call and re-evaluate on the turn — fold to large polarizing aggression, call small-to-medium barrels, and extract value on favorable blank/T turns or if he checks.

Turn (Tc) gives me JcTd — two pair (Jacks and Tens). Heads-up versus a wide button opening/c-betting range this is a very strong improvement: I now beat most single-pair Kx/Ax hands, most Jx/Tx hands and a lot of his missed draws. The hands that still beat me are sets (KK/TT/JJ), two-pair combinations containing a K (KJ/KT) and made straights (AQ/Q9) — but my J and T block a number of those combos. With the pot only 1,200 and stacks deep, I want to extract value and deny equity to turn/river overcards/draws while keeping the pot manageable against a very deep, aggressive opponent. A value bet around half pot (~600) accomplishes that: it gets called by worse pairs and many draws, charges heart/backdoor cards, and avoids overcommitting in case Flash shows heavy aggression. Plan: bet 600 to get value; if he raises a small/medium sized 3-bet I will strongly consider calling with this two-pair, but re-evaluate/fold to an extremely large polarizing shove that represents trips/full house/straight if sizing makes a call incorrect.

On the turn, the board has become very coordinated with the Ten of Clubs, and Minni has led out for 600 into a 1200 pot. I have a heart flush draw (9 outs). The pot odds are 3-to-1 (calling 600 to win a total pot of 2400), which requires roughly 25% equity to be a break-even call. While my raw equity to hit the flush on the river is approximately 20%, the implied odds are significant given Minni's remaining stack. If I hit the flush, I likely have the best hand and can extract more value or potentially stack her. Raising here is risky against a lead on such a wet board, so calling to see the river is the most prudent play.

River is a clean blank (3c) and I hold JcTd — two pair (Jacks & Tens). Heads-up vs Flash (wide preflop opener who c-bet the flop and called my turn raise), his continuing range is heavy in single-pair hands (Kx, Ax, worse Jx) plus some sets/two-pair combos. Most of that range is behind my two pair; the hands that beat me are trips (KK/JJ/TT) or Kx hands that make two pair (KJ/KT), which are less frequent. I want to extract value from one-pair hands and worse J/T hands while keeping the pot from getting wildly large against the small subset of hands that crush me. A half-pot value bet (~1200 into 2400) hits that balance — it gets called by worse hands and charges missed draws/bluffs, but doesn’t overcommit me to a huge raise from a polarized hand. If he flat-calls I realize more equity; if he makes a large, polarizing raise I’ll re-evaluate and fold to an all-in or extremely large overbet that only very strong holdings would make.

On the river, the board is Jh 6h Ks Tc 3c. I missed my heart flush draw and ended up with a pair of 3s. Minni led out on both the turn and the river. This sequence of actions (calling the flop and leading into the aggressor on the turn and river) indicates significant strength, likely a King, a straight (Q9), or two pair. While I have a pair of 3s, it is essentially a bluff catcher on this board. Given the coordination of the board and Minni's betting lead, she is rarely bluffing here. I am getting 3-to-1 on a call, but my hand is too weak to justify it against this specific betting pattern. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button with Js2s. In a two‑handed game you should open very wide from the button — Js2s is marginal but playable because it’s suited and gives backdoor equity and positional advantage post‑flop. Limping here surrenders initiative and is exploitable; folding is too weak. I’ll open‑raise to 3BB (total 300) to take the lead, apply pressure to Flash’s wide defending range, and give myself the option to play post‑flop in position. Size is in line with table norms and preserves fold equity; if Flash 3‑bets large I’ll fold this holding, but versus a call I’ll c‑bet favorable flops and pot‑control or fold to heavy aggression on dangerous textures.

I have Qh6h in the Big Blind. Minni raised to 300 from the Small Blind. I need to call 200 more into a pot of 400, giving me 2:1 odds. Suited Queens are generally strong enough to defend the big blind in a heads-up situation. I will call and see a flop.

I am first to act on the flop. The board is 9s-Kd-Jh, and I hold Qh6h, giving me a gutshot straight draw (needing a Ten). This board is very coordinated and likely hits my opponent's raising range. I will check to see how the opponent reacts before deciding whether to continue with my draw.

Flop: 9s Kd Jh. I hold Js2s — a pair of jacks with a very weak kicker. Heads‑up my preflop raising range is wide and Flash’s check is consistent with weakness or a passive call. I want to extract value from worse jacks, 9x, Tx type hands and deny free cards (the K/ Q/ T turn cards can easily improve many parts of his range), but I also want to keep the pot manageable with a vulnerable pair. A small continuation bet (~1/3 pot) accomplishes value and fold equity while preserving stack size. If he flats I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn; if he check‑raises big I’ll give up against likely Kx or better. Betting 200 is the best balance here.

I have a gutshot straight draw with my Queen and the 9-K-J board, needing a Ten. Minni has bet 200 into a 600 pot, offering me 4:1 odds on a call. Although my gutshot only has about an 8.5% chance of hitting on the turn, the small bet size combined with potential implied odds and the fact that I have an overcard (Queen) makes a call profitable in the long run. I will call to see the turn.

On the turn, the board is 9s-Kd-Jh-4s. I am first to act and currently hold a gutshot straight draw with my Queen. Since I am out of position and have no made hand yet, checking is the standard play here to see how Minni reacts to the turn card. The 4s is largely a blank that doesn't change the board texture significantly.

I have top pair (J♠) with a very weak 2‑kicker. I led the flop and was called; the turn (4♠) is a low card that didn't complete obvious straights and only adds a second spade to the board (no flush yet). Flash checked the turn, showing either weakness or a passive call with a marginal hand. Heads‑up top pair is often still best and I want to extract value from worse Jx, 9x, Tx type hands while charging overcards and denying a cheap river. I also don’t want to bloat the pot with such a weak kicker, so I’ll make a modest value/protection bet of 400 (~40% pot). If he folds I take the pot; if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate the river; if he check‑raises large I’ll give credit to Kx/sets/straights and fold.

On the turn, the board is 9s-Kd-Jh-4s. I currently have a gutshot straight draw needing a Ten to complete a King-high straight (9-T-J-Q-K). Minni has bet 400 into a pot of 1000, which offers me 3.5-to-1 odds to call. While the direct mathematical probability of hitting a Ten on the river is only about 8.7%, the small bet size combined with the potential implied odds if I do hit my straight makes a call viable. Additionally, a Queen on the river could potentially give me the best hand if Minni is betting with a weaker pair or a draw. I will call to see the river card.

On the river, the board pairs the King, making the final board Ks-Kd-Jh-9s-4s. I hold Qh6h, which means my best hand is a pair of Kings with a Queen kicker. I have missed my gutshot straight draw. Since I am out of position and have very little showdown value against Minni's range, which has shown strength on every street, checking is the most logical play. I will check and hope for a check-back or evaluate if Minni bets, though a fold is likely given the strength she has demonstrated.

River brings K♠ and completes my back‑door spade flush (I hold J♠2♠). That turns a vulnerable one‑pair hand into a King‑high spade flush — strong versus a calling range that contained lots of Kx, Qx, Jx, and medium pocket pairs. Villain’s line (call preflop, call a small c‑bet on the flop, call my turn bet, then check the river) is consistent with many medium‑strength hands and some pocket pairs; a small number of combos still beat me (full houses: pocket JJ/99/44 or Kx hands that pair a board card, and two‑spade holdings with Q♠/A♠ make a better flush). Overall I’m ahead often enough to extract value. I’ll make a value bet about half the pot (900) to get called by worse pairs, Kx, and single‑paired hands while keeping the pot controlled against the rare full houses or better flushes; if he check‑raises big I’ll give credit to the very narrow combos that beat me and fold.

The board is 9s-Kd-Jh-4s-Ks. I hold Qh6h, which means my best five-card hand is two kings on the board with a Queen-Jack-Nine kicker. I have missed my gutshot straight draw. Minni has bet on every street, and her 900 bet into an 1800 pot on the river suggests she has a made hand, likely at least a King for trips or a Jack/Nine for two pair that beats my Queen kicker. Since I have no made hand and only a mediocre kicker to the board's pair, I cannot justify a call here against such consistent aggression.

I am holding Ace-King offsuit (Ac Kh) on the button. This is a premier hand in heads-up play. With a significant chip lead over Minni, I want to play aggressively and build the pot. I will put in a standard raise to 300, which is consistent with my previous pre-flop opening sizes, to gain initiative and extract value from weaker hands in Minni's range.

I’m in the big blind with A♥8♠ facing a button (SB) open to 300. I’ve already posted 100, so calling 200 (300 total) gives me good pot odds (~200 to win ~400 ≈ 33%) and A8o has solid equity versus a typical wide heads‑up opening range. The opponent is deep and aggressive, so I don’t want to bloat a pot out of position with an offsuit Ace by 3‑betting thin. I’ll defend by calling to see a flop and then play post‑flop based on texture: lead/raise when I pair the Ace or hit strong equity, otherwise control pot size and proceed cautiously against Flash’s aggression.

Check. I’m out of position with A♥8♠ on T♠‑2♣‑3♣ and I haven’t paired. This flop is low and relatively dry and misses a large portion of Flash’s SB opening range (broadways, Ax, medium pairs), so there’s decent chance to see a free turn or induce a bluff. My earlier plan was to control pot size with marginal holdings — betting here would bloat a pot OOP with only Ace‑high (I have no club backdoor) and invite a shove/raise from a very deep, aggressive opponent. I’ll check to realize equity and evaluate the turn. Plan: check‑call a small–medium c‑bet (≈≤½ pot, ~≤300) to see a turn, fold to a large c‑bet/raise unless I improve (pair or better), and if Flash checks back take a free card and possibly lead the turn if favorable.

I have Ace-high with two overcards and a backdoor nut flush draw on a relatively low board of Ts 2c 3c. Minni has checked to me after calling pre-flop. This is a standard spot for a continuation bet to maintain initiative and potentially win the pot immediately or set up a barrel on later streets if I improve. A bet of 300 (half pot) is appropriate to pressure her range which likely consists of many weak hands that missed this flop.

Flash's 300 c‑bet into a 900 pot is a small (≈1/3 pot) continuation bet from a wide BTN opening range. I have A♥8♠ — Ace‑high with no club backdoor, but the Ace blocks some of Flash's Ax/broadway combos. Calling 300 requires ≈20% equity (300 to win ~1500), which A8o has versus a wide c‑betting range, and calling keeps the pot manageable out of position. I’ll call to realize equity and see a turn; fold to a large turn raise/shove without improvement, but if I pair the Ace or pick up meaningful equity I’ll proceed aggressively. I avoid check‑raising or bloating the pot OOP with no draw against a very deep, aggressive opponent.

Out of position on T♠‑2♣‑3♣‑2♥ with A♥8♠ — I only have Ace‑high and no club backdoor. Flash is very deep and aggressive and I already called a small flop bet; the turn pairing the deuce helps his range (2x, Tx, some turned two‑pair/trips) and makes bluffing/float lines more likely. I don’t want to bloat the pot OOP with a marginal hand, so I’ll control the pot by checking. Plan: if Flash checks back I take a free river; if he bets a small probe (roughly ≤~1/3 pot, ≈≤400) I’ll call to realize equity; if he presses with a large bet/raise or shoves I will fold unless I improve. Checking preserves stack and gives the best long‑term expectation with this holding.

On the turn, the board pairs the deuce (2h), which is generally a blank card for both players' ranges unless someone is playing a very wide range of suited connectors or small pairs. I currently hold Ace-high (Ace-King) with no made hand. Minni checked to me again after calling my flop continuation bet. While I could bet again to represent a Ten or a high pair, checking back allows me to realize my equity and see a free river card. Ace-King still has significant showdown value in heads-up play against a range that includes missed draws and weaker high cards. I will check to keep the pot manageable and potentially induce a bluff or value-bet an Ace or King on the river.

I’m out of position with only Ace‑high (A♥8♠). Preflop I called, I floated the small flop c‑bet and checked the turn after Flash checked back — that line looks like either a missed broadway/weak hand or pot‑control with a medium pair; it also includes some slowplays but not many. The pot is 1,200 and both stacks are deep, so betting as a pure bluff here has limited fold equity versus a very deep, aggressive opponent who will call wide. I also block some A‑x combos but not enough to profitably shove or overbet OOP with no pair. The most +EV line is to preserve stack and take showdown: check and hope my Ace‑high is good; if Flash fires a small river bet I can consider a call depending on size, but I won’t bloat the pot myself with a marginal hand. Action: check.

On the river, the board is Ts 2c 3c 2h 7s. I hold Ace-King high. The action has gone: raise/call pre-flop, bet/call flop, and check/check turn. Minni has now checked to me on the river. My Ace-King has significant showdown value in a heads-up pot, as it beats all missed draws (like clubs or 4-5) and lower Ace-high or King-high hands. There is very little value in betting here; if I bet, Minni is unlikely to call with a worse hand, but she will certainly call with a Ten or any pair she might have. By checking back, I realize my equity and get to showdown for free, which is the most profitable play given the board texture and previous action.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the SB) with J♥7♦. In heads-up play the button should open a very wide range — J7o is well within that. I want to seize initiative, use position, and apply fold equity against a large-stacked, often-aggressive opponent. A standard 2xBB min-raise to 200 is the right play: it’s small enough to keep pots controllable when called but big enough to pick up the blinds uncontested often. Post-flop plan: c-bet dry boards and play aggressively in position; if Flash 3-bets large I’ll fold this hand (poor equity vs a 3-bet range), but I can call small 3-bets or continue vs weaker defenses. Raise to 200 to open the pot and keep options post-flop.

I am in the Big Blind with 8h 5s. Minni has opened for a minimum raise to 200. While the pot odds are attractive (100 to call into a pot of 300), 8-5 offsuit is a very weak hand that plays poorly out of position. I have a significant stack lead and there's no need to defend with such a marginal hand against Minni's frequent aggression. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with Jack-Four offsuit. While standard heads-up strategy often dictates opening a wide range from the button, J4o is near the bottom of that range. Given that I have a significant chip lead and have lost several recent medium-sized pots to Minni's aggression, I will choose to fold this marginal hand and wait for a stronger opening opportunity to minimize unnecessary variance.

Heads-up on the button with A♣7♥ — a strong, playable hand in position. I want to seize initiative, get value from worse aces/weak broadways and deny the BB cheap odds. Standard open-sizing in this game has been ~3x BB, so I’ll raise to 300 total (I’ve already posted the 50 SB) — big enough to have fold equity and deter speculative calls, but small enough to keep pot controllable with 87BB effective. If Flash 3-bets big I can fold or re-evaluate; if he calls I’ll c-bet favorable flops and use position to steer the hand.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Trey of spades, a strong suited hand in a heads-up context. Minni, on the button, has raised to 300. I have a significant chip lead, but Minni still has 85 big blinds. I will 3-bet to 1000 to take initiative and potentially end the hand pre-flop or build a pot with a hand that has good post-flop playability and nut potential.

Call. A♣7♥ heads-up in position is worth seeing a flop against Flash’s 3‑bet: I keep the pot controllable, retain positional advantage and get good pot odds. The pot is 1,300; calling 700 more (1000 total committed) gives me ~35% equity required and A7o typically has enough real equity versus a reasonably wide 3‑bet range. 4‑betting to 1,700 would unnecessarily polarize my range and risk committing with a marginal hand; folding is too tight. Plan after the call: proceed postflop using position — bet for value when I pair an Ace or 7, float on dry boards and re‑evaluate if Flash applies heavy pressure (fold to shove/very large 4‑bet unless I read him as extremely wide).

I have flopped top pair with the nut flush draw blocker (the Ace of Spades). The board is extremely draw-heavy with three diamonds. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor and have top pair, I should bet to get value from weaker Aces or diamond draws that might call. A bet of around half the pot is appropriate here to keep the pot controlled while still charging draws.

Call 1000. I have top pair (A♣7♥) but a weak kicker on a monotone Ad Td 5d flop — the main worry is Flash holding two diamonds (the nut flush) or an A with a better kicker / Ten for two pair. His flop sizing is a modest c‑bet (~1/3–1/2 pot) that can include a lot of bluffs and thin value hands given his preflop 3‑bet. Folding is too tight given the pot odds (call 1000 to win ~3000 ≈ 25% break‑even) and raising would unnecessarily polarize and commit with a vulnerable hand. I’ll call to keep worse Aces and bluffs in, use position to probe the turn, and re‑evaluate — proceed cautiously (check/call or fold to heavy turn aggression, bet for value if I improve or get a safe card).

I have top pair with a weak kicker on a monotone board where I do not hold a diamond. This makes my hand quite vulnerable to both existing flushes and any diamond on the river. Since I was the aggressor pre-flop and on the flop, checking here allows me to control the size of the pot and potentially get to a cheaper showdown or catch a bluff. If Minni bets, I will evaluate based on her sizing, but for now, checking is the most prudent play to avoid over-committing with a marginal hand.

Check. I have top pair (A♣7♥) but a very vulnerable hand on a monotone Ad–Td–5d flop; the turn 8♠ is a harmless card but it didn’t improve my kicker or change the diamond draw dynamics. Flash was the preflop 3‑bettor and c‑bet the flop — his range includes nut/near‑nut diamonds, two‑pair/trips (AT, TT, 55) and strong Ax hands. His check on the turn is ambiguous (pot‑control or a trap/check‑raise spot), and betting here risks getting check‑raised by hands that confidently beat me while bloating a pot I’m not comfortable committing a big portion of my 6,775 stack to. Checking keeps the pot manageable (4,000), lets me realize equity vs bluffs and worse Aces, and preserves the option to fold to heavy aggression. Plan: check to the river; if Flash bets a modest amount on a non‑diamond I will seriously consider a call for value, but I will respect large polarizing river bets (or a diamond river that completes obvious flush draws) and fold when necessary.

On this monotone board (Ad Td 5d 8s 6c), I have top pair with an Ace, but a very weak kicker and no diamond in my hand. After Minni called my 3-bet pre-flop and my continuation bet on the flop, her range likely includes diamonds, weaker Aces, or Tens. By checking the turn and now the river, I am looking for a cheap showdown. Minni's check on the turn suggests she might not have the flush yet or is playing a marginal made hand cautiously. Betting now would likely only result in being called by better hands (stronger Aces, two pairs like A8 or A6, or flushes) while folding out hands I already beat. Checking is the most consistent way to realize my equity.

Board: A♦ T♦ 5♦ 8♠ 6♣. I have A♣7♥ — top pair with a weak kicker on a very dangerous flop (three diamonds). Flash 3‑bet preflop and led the flop, which polarizes his range heavily toward diamond flushes, two‑pair/trips (AT/TT/55) and strong Ax combinations; his check on the turn and river is ambiguous (pot control or a trap), but it also makes it likely his range contains many hands that beat me. Betting here would only extract thin value from the narrow subset of worse A‑x that will call and would risk a check‑raise from hands that already crush me. With 6,775 behind and a 4,000 pot, I don’t want to bloat the pot or face a large polarizing raise with a vulnerable kicker. I’ll take the low‑risk line and check to showdown — realize equity versus bluffs and worse A‑x, avoid getting bluffed off or check‑raised out of the pot, and preserve stack for better spots.

I am on the button with J5 offsuit. While it's a weak hand, standard strategy in heads-up play allows for raising or calling from the small blind quite frequently. However, J5o is near the bottom of my opening range. Since Minni has been fairly active and I want to keep the pot small with a marginal hand, I will simply complete the small blind to see a flop for a cheap price. Folding is also an option, but in a 2-player dynamic, seeing a flop with a Jack-high hand is acceptable.

I’m in the big blind with Kc3s facing a limp from the button. Heads-up Kx has some value, but K3 offsuit is a marginal holding and I’ll be out of position postflop. Raising to the min (200) would invest extra chips OOP against a wide but unpredictable limping range — I prefer pot control and seeing a flop for free. Plan: check and evaluate the flop. If I pair the K or pick up strong draws I’ll take the initiative with a controlled c-bet; if I miss and face significant aggression I’ll fold to large bets to protect my stack. Preserving stack depth and playing postflop position-aware is the higher EV line here.

Check. K3o missed the flop — I have only K‑high with no heart to the two‑heart board and I’m out of position. Flash limped a wide range, so a bet here would be a pure, marginal bluff that can easily be raised or called by many hands that currently beat me (Qx, Jx, pairs) or have good equity. I’ll control the pot and see a free turn to improve or get more information. Plan: check and fold to a large isolation bet, call small bets to realize equity and look for a better spot on later streets.

On a board of Qh-4h-Js, I have middle pair (Jacks) with a weak kicker (5). Minni has checked to me. While I could bet for protection against flush and straight draws, middle pair is a classic 'way ahead or way behind' hand in heads-up play. Checking back allows me to control the size of the pot, keep Minni's bluffs in her range, and avoid a check-raise that would force me to fold a decent hand. I will check to see how the turn develops.

I have K♣3♠ on Q♥‑4♥‑J♠‑2♠. I missed — I only have K‑high with 6 clean outs to pair (three Ks, three 3s) and no real draw. Flash limped and has shown passivity (check‑check), so his range is wide but contains many hands that currently beat me (Qx, Jx, small pairs). Betting OOP here is a thin bluff that will be called by lots of hands or turned into a raise; I prefer pot control. Plan: check and see the river for free. On the river I’ll call small, reasonable bets to realize equity but fold to significant pressure — preserve stack and avoid bloating the pot with a marginal hand.

I have middle pair (Jacks) with a weak kicker. After checking the flop, Minni has checked again on the turn. While I could bet for value or protection against the flush draws on the board, checking back keeps the pot small and prevents me from getting blown off my hand by a check-raise. It also keeps Minni's potential bluffs in her range for the river. I will check and look to reach showdown or evaluate the river action.

Board: Q♥ 4♥ J♠ 2♠ A♦. I hold K♣3♠ — no pair, no draw, and the Ace on the river kills a lot of my outs and gives the villain additional top‑pair combos. Flash limped and played very passively (check‑check), so his range is wide but contains many hands that currently beat K‑high (A‑x, Qx, Jx, small pairs) and will call small bets. Betting here would be a thin bluff with limited fold equity and the risk of getting called by worse or getting raised by value hands. The pot is tiny (200) relative to stacks, so I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand. I’ll check to control the pot and fold to significant aggression; I’ll call very small, reasonable bets to realize equity but preserve my stack against pressure.

I have middle pair (Jacks) on an Ace-high board. Minni has checked on every street, which suggests she either has a very weak hand or a missed draw. While there is a chance she is trapping with an Ace that hit on the river, my hand has significant showdown value against her range of missed heart draws or smaller pairs. Betting here likely only gets called by hands that beat me (like a Queen or an Ace), so checking back to realize my equity and win the small pot is the most prudent play.

Heads-up on the button with Q♠4♠ — a suited queen is a very playable hand heads-up. Pot is 150 and only 50 to call, so I should open a wide range and take the initiative. A raise to 300 (standard ~3x-ish sizing) applies pressure to the big blind, gains fold equity, and keeps the pot and my remaining stack manageable while preserving position post-flop. If Flash responds with a large 3-bet I'll re-evaluate (often folding to heavy pressure), but vs a call I can play position and leverage my fold equity and backdoor flush/straight potential. Raising to 300 is the plus-EV play here.

I am in the Big Blind with 5h 2h, which is a very weak starting hand. Minni has raised to 3x the big blind from the button. Even with the suited potential, 52s is too weak to defend out of position against a raise. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button in a heads-up situation. While Jack-Six offsuit is a weak hand, the advantage of being in position post-flop allows for a wider opening range. I will raise to 300 to apply pressure and attempt to take the blinds, or play the hand in position if called.

Facing a standard heads-up button (SB) open to 300. I’m in the BB with J6s and stacks are deep (~106bb effective for me, Flash ~289bb). Pot is 400 and I need to call 200 more (300 total) — that gives me roughly 33% equity requirement. J6s has reasonable equity vs a wide SB opening range and plays well postflop (flush/straight possibilities), so defending is the correct default. A 3-bet is an option but J6s is marginal to polarize with against an aggressive, deep-stacked opponent; I prefer to keep the pot controlled and see a flop. Plan: call, continue with any pair or strong draw, fold to heavy pressure if the flop gives me nothing and I face large aggression.

Check. I have a weak made hand (pair of 6s with a mediocre J-kicker) and I’m out of position on a K-5-6 rainbow board. The preflop opener’s range contains a lot of Kx and broadway hands that currently beat me, and the board is fairly dry with only a few clean outs (6s or Js) to significantly improve. Pot control is the priority: check to see a turn for free, call small-to-medium continuation bets to realize equity against bluffs/worse pairs, and fold to large, polarized overbets that indicate Kx/sets. If I hit a 6 (trips) or a J (two pair) I’ll switch to a more aggressive line.

I have middle pair (sixes) with a Jack kicker on a board of Kd-6c-5d. While I have a piece of the board, the King is a significant card that is well within Minni's calling range from the big blind. Checking here allows me to control the pot size and realize my equity with a marginal made hand. It also keeps Minni's range wide, as betting might only get called by hands that beat me (like a King or a better pocket pair). I will check back to see the turn for free.

Turn pairs the board and I now have two pair (Kings and Sixes: K K 6 6 J). Flash opened preflop but checked both flop and turn, which often indicates a missed broadway or a weak holding rather than an immediate slowplay of a King/set — so I’m likely ahead of a wide portion of his range (A-x, Q-x, J-x, many medium pairs). I want to extract value and deny a free river card to any backdoor draws, while keeping the sizing small enough to fold out bluffs but not overcommit if I face a large check‑raise from a slowplayed K or a full house. Bet ~1/2 pot (300) to get called by worse hands and protect; if I face a big raise I’ll assume trips/sets and fold.

The turn pairs the King, which is a card that hits both ranges. After checking back on the flop with my pair of sixes, Minni leads out for half the pot. While she could certainly have a King, my hand has decent showdown value and the pot odds of 3:1 are favorable to see a river. I will call to evaluate the final street, as folding a pair here would be too tight given the board texture and my previous check which may have induced a bluff.

I have K K K 6 6 on the river (kings full of sixes) — a near-nut hand that only loses to the one remaining King (Ks) which would make quads. Flash’s line (opened, checked flop & turn, then called my 300 turn bet) looks like a wide calling range: Kx, medium pairs, some missed broadways and occasional bluffs. Those hands will often call a value bet here. Checking risks giving up value; I should extract. I’ll size up to get called by worse Kx, pocket pairs and weaker full houses while still leaving room to fold if I face a large raise that likely represents quads. Bet ~3/4 pot (900) to maximize thin value. If Flash raises massively I’ll assign quads and fold; if he calls I expect to be ahead and collect meaningful value.

On a board of 5d-Kd-6c-Kh-Kc, the final board is essentially three of a kind (Kings) with a 6 and 5. My hole cards are Jc-6d. My best five-card hand is K-K-K-J-6. In this scenario, any hand with an Ace or a Queen (which are very common in a Big Blind defending range) beats me. Furthermore, any pocket pair from 77 to QQ also beats me, as their full house (e.g., K-K-K-7-7) or high-kicker hand would be superior. Minni's line of checking the flop and leading on both the turn and river suggests she either has a King, an Ace, or a pocket pair. Since most of her semi-bluffs or 'thin value' hands (like Q-high or A-high) actually beat my J-kicker on this specific board texture, calling is not profitable. I will fold.

Heads-up on the button with pocket deuces — this is a clear open-raise spot. I’ve already posted 50, the pot is 150 and calling 50 is too passive; raising takes the initiative, builds the pot with a hand that plays very well heads-up, and generates fold equity against Flash’s wide BB defending range. I’ll make a standard 3xBB open to 300 total (250 more), which is big enough to discourage some light 3-bets but leaves plenty of stack to maneuver postflop. If Flash calls I’ll c-bet on many dry flops and plan to set-mine (call reasonable 3-bets) or fold to very large 3-bets depending on sizing. Raise to 300.

I am in the Big Blind with K6o. Minni has raised to 300 from the Button (Small Blind). While K6o is not a premium hand, in a heads-up situation, it's generally strong enough to defend against a standard open, especially with a stack size advantage. However, my previous play has shown that I tend to be cautious when the board doesn't hit my range. Given the pot odds (calling 200 to win 400), I will call and see a flop.

On a board of Ah-8d-8h, I have King-high with no immediate draws or pairs. This board texture heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's range, as they frequently hold Aces. I will check to see how Minni reacts to the flop. If she bets, I will likely fold unless the bet size is extremely small, as I have very little equity here.

I opened to 300 preflop and Flash just called. Flop is Ah 8d 8h (pot 600) and Flash checked — this is a very bad board for my pocket deuces: I’m behind to any A or 8 and only have set-outside equity (about ~17% to hit a set by river). Betting as a bluff can fold some overcards, but the board is paired and Flash has shown a tendency to call flop bets; I don’t want to inflate the pot with an underpair that has poor equity and that will fold to aggression. I’ll pot-control and try to realize equity: check here. Plan: if Flash bets a small probe (roughly ≤1/3 pot) I’ll call to see a turn and keep set-mining alive; if Flash barrels for a large chunk of the pot I’ll fold (unless the turn pairs me). If I hit a 2 on the turn I’ll start building the pot for value.

On a board of Ah-8d-8h-7h, I have the second-nut flush draw with the King of Hearts. Minni checked back on the flop, which often indicates she does not have an Ace or an 8. I will check the turn to see her reaction. If she checks behind, I get a free card to try and hit my flush. If she bets, I have sufficient equity with the flush draw and overcard to potentially call or check-raise depending on the size.

I opened preflop with 22 and pot-controlled the flop (Ah 8d 8h) because my hand is an underpair with only set equity. The 7h on the turn doesn’t improve me and Flash checked again, so I’ll stick to the plan: keep the pot small and realize my equity. Betting here bloats the pot with very poor equity (only two deuces left) and is vulnerable to check-raises or calls by hands that still beat me (A‑x, 8‑x, bigger pairs). I’ll check back and see a cheap river — if a 2 hits I can start building for value; if Flash bets the river I’ll call small bets for pot odds but fold to large river barrels.

The river 9s did not complete my flush draw, leaving me with King-high (Kh 6c) on a board of Ah-8d-8h-7h-9s. Minni checked both the flop and the turn, which typically indicates a weak hand or a missed draw. While I could attempt to bluff to represent an Ace, an Eight, or a flush, my King-high may have some showdown value against her missed straight draws or lower high cards (like QJ or JT). Given the small pot size and my stack lead, checking is the most prudent action to reach showdown without further risk.

Board: Ah 8d 8h 7h 9s. I hold 2d 2c and never improved. I’ve been pot-controlling since the flop because this is a very bad board for an underpair — opponent’s check‑check line through flop and turn is consistent with a wide but dangerous range (any A, any 8, mid pairs, or even two hearts for a flush), plus some missed overcards. Betting the river would be a pure bluff and my fold equity is low given Flash’s call-happy tendencies in earlier hands; many of the hands that will fold (garbage like KQ) are also the minority of his checking range, while most real pairs will call. I preserve my stack and realize equity by checking to showdown — no reason to inflate a pot I’m unlikely to win. Action: check.

In a heads-up situation, I am on the button with 8c 4c. While not a premium hand, being in position allows for more flexibility. I will put in a standard raise to 300 to apply pressure on the big blind and potentially take the pot down pre-flop. If called, the suited nature of the hand provides some post-flop playability.

Heads-up in the big blind facing a 300 open from the button. I have Q7 hearts — marginal but playable: suited Q7 has decent equity (backdoor flush/straight potential and good two‑pair possibilities) and the call is cheap. Calling costs 200 into a 400 pot (good pot odds) and with our stack depth there are solid implied odds if we hit. I don’t want to 3‑bet light OOP with a hand that’s often dominated (a min‑raise to 500 would commit more chips and still leave me out of position postflop). Plan: call to see the flop, proceed aggressively only with top pair, strong draws, or two pair+, otherwise play cautiously and fold to heavy action. If Flash 4‑bets preflop I’ll fold.

I have Q♥7♥ on a 6♠ J♦ 2♥ flop — no pair, just queen-high with only a backdoor heart/runner-runner possibilities. Facing the preflop raiser heads‑up and out of position, I should stick to my plan: proceed only with top pair/strong draws/two‑pair+. C‑betting OOP with a marginal hand is unnecessary and will get me in trouble vs a calling or raising range. I’ll check to control the pot, fold to any meaningful flop bet, and if Flash checks back I’ll see a free turn and reassess (bet or realize equity if I pair or pick up relevant draws).

I am on the button with 8c 4c. After raising pre-flop and being called, the flop comes 6s Jd 2h, which is a very dry, rainbow board. Minni checked to me. Although I haven't connected with the board, I have the range advantage as the pre-flop aggressor on such a disconnected texture. A standard continuation bet of 300 (half pot) is likely to force folds from many of Minni's non-pairing hands and keep the initiative.

Heads‑up, I’m OOP in the big blind with Q♥7♥ on 6♠ J♦ 2♥. I checked to control the pot and face a 300 c‑bet into a 900 pot — calling 300 requires roughly 25% equity. My hand is just queen‑high with only backdoor (runner‑runner) heart possibilities and only ~6 immediate outs (3 queens + 3 sevens) to improve; realistic equity versus a button’s c‑betting range (Jx, broadways, Ax, some pairs) is well under the required 25%. I also have very little fold equity OOP and raising here would be a thin, high‑variance bluff. Given stack depths and position, the correct, lowest‑variance play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button with 9♣5♦ — a marginal but acceptable hand in position. Pot is 150 and it's only 50 to call, but I prefer to take the initiative and not give Flash a cheap flop. I’ll open to 300 (≈3x BB): large enough to generate fold equity and reduce opponents’ pot odds to call, but still small relative to deep stacks so I can fold to a big 3‑bet. If Flash 3‑bets big (a sizable 3‑bet/shove) I’ll fold this holding; if he calls I’ll use position to c‑bet most favorable flops, control the pot on wet/connected boards, and proceed cautiously with second‑pair/weak draws. Overall this raise widens my profitable heads‑up range and pressures the big blind.

I hold Ace-Jack offsuit in the Big Blind, which is a premium hand in heads-up play. Minni raised from the button, and I should 3-bet for value and to take initiative. Based on previous hands, a 3-bet size of 1000 has been effective at either winning the pot pre-flop or isolating against a weaker range.

Flash 3‑bets to 1,000 — a standard heads‑up 3‑bet size, not a shove. I’m on the button with 9♣5♦: a marginal hand but playable in position. It’s 700 more to call into a pot of ~1,300 (I’ll be committing 1,000 total), so the pot odds and positional advantage justify a cold‑call. I won’t 4‑bet bluff to 1,700 with this holding; instead I’ll call to see a flop, c‑bet only on favourable, dry textures, control the pot on wet/connected boards, and fold to heavy turn/river aggression unless I improve. Call to 1,000.

I hold middle pair (Jacks) with an Ace kicker on a board that contains one overcard (King) and several potential draws (straight and flush). Having been the pre-flop 3-bettor, I have a range advantage that includes AA, KK, and AK. I will lead out with a continuation bet of 700, which is roughly 35% of the pot. This amount is large enough to get value from weaker Jacks or draws (like QT, T9, or spades) while keeping the pot manageable if I am beat by a King.

Heads‑up vs a preflop 3‑better. Board 8♥ J♠ K♠, Flash bets 700 into ~2,700. I hold 9♣5♦ — no pair, no spade, only 6 clean outs (3 nines + 3 fives) → ~24% to hit at least a pair by the river. Calling 700 to win ~3,400 requires ~21% equity, so pure pot‑odds + equity makes a call marginally +EV. I have position, which lets me float and try to take the pot on a favorable turn or fold to heavy turn aggression. I will not raise — a bluff‑raise here with 95o into a 3‑bettor’s range is too thin and I risk committing too much with very poor equity. Plan: call 700 to see the turn; fold to significant turn pressure if I miss, and if I pair the 9 or 5 re‑evaluate (proceed cautiously vs continuing aggression).

The turn card is the Five of Spades, which completes the flush draw from the flop. I have middle pair with an Ace kicker, but the board is now quite coordinated (three spades, straight possibilities with QT or T9). Given that I was the pre-flop aggressor and bet the flop, checking now is the best move for pot control. Betting again would likely only be called by better hands (Kings or Flushes) and doesn't accomplish much against her likely calling range. By checking, I can see how Minni reacts and decide whether to call a bet or see a cheap river.

I turned a small pair (5s) with 9♣5♦ on a board that now shows 8♥ J♠ K♠ 5♠. Pot ≈3,400, stacks deep-ish (I: 9,675, Flash: 26,925). Flash was the preflop 3‑better, c‑bet the flop and then checked the turn — that check is ambiguous: it can be pot‑control with Kx/Jx or a slow‑play/trap with a spade flush, or simply weakness. With three spades on board there are now many flush combos in his 3‑betting range, so my lone pair is vulnerable.

Betting here for thin value is unattractive — a small probe bet (~1k) will often be called by hands that already beat me (Kx/Jx/flush draws that completed) and will expose me to a check‑raise from flushes; a larger bet is even worse. I have position and can realize my equity by checking, keeping the pot under control and seeing the river for free. If the river improves me to two pair/trips I can extract; if Flash fires a small river bet I can call; if he shows heavy aggression I can fold without committing a big portion of my stack.

Plan: pot control and reevaluate on the river — check back the turn.

On the river, the board is 8h-Js-Ks-5s-7d. I have second pair (Jacks) with an Ace kicker. The turn 5s completed the spade flush, and both players checked, which suggests Minni likely doesn't have a flush or a very strong King, as she would have been incentivized to bet for value after my check. My hand has significant showdown value, but betting is unlikely to get called by worse hands (maybe a weaker Jack or a pocket pair like Tens). By checking, I can reach showdown and win the pot against her missed draws or weaker pairs without risking more chips against a potential trap or a marginal King.

Check. Flash’s line (preflop 3‑bet, flop c‑bet then passive turn/river) looks like Kx/Jx or pot‑control hands and some missed spade draws. With three spades on board my lone pair of fives is thin and often dominated by his value combos or crushed by a spade flush — a value probe will get called by hands that beat me and fold out only pure bluffs. Checking keeps the pot small, preserves my stack and showdown value, and lets me fold to significant river aggression if he suddenly raises. If Flash fires a small bet I can call for pot odds; I won’t bluff‑inflate the pot here.

I am holding 7d 3c on the button. This is one of the weakest hands in Texas Hold'em and generally performs poorly post-flop. Despite being in position, the range disadvantage is significant, and I should not be committing chips with this hand, especially since Minni has shown willingness to defend her big blind or call pre-flop raises. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the SB) with 5♦8♣ — a marginal but perfectly playable hand in position. I want to seize initiative, apply fold equity, and keep the range wide from the button. The pot is small (150) and the minimum raise to 200 is cheap insurance to win the pot preflop or play postflop in position. Flash is deep-stacked and will defend sometimes, but a small standard raise keeps the pot controlled and lets me c‑bet many flops or check and realize equity when appropriate. If Flash 3‑bets big I’ll concede this hand given its weak showdown value; if he calls I’ll play aggressively in position. Raising to the minimum balances aggression and pot control here.

I am in the big blind with 6s 4d, which is a very weak hand. Minni has raised to 200. Although I am getting 3:1 odds to call, this hand plays poorly out of position and has very little potential. Given my significant stack lead, there is no reason to defend with such a weak range of cards.

I am on the button with Ac4c, which is a strong starting hand in heads-up play. Standard strategy dictates a raise to build the pot and exert pressure. I will raise to 300, which has been my consistent opening size throughout the match.

Heads-up vs a 300 open from the button, K♦7♣ is worth defending from the big blind but not strong enough to build a large pot out of position. The pot is 400 and I must call 200 more (I’ve already posted 100), so calling 200 (total commitment 300) gives ~33% immediate pot odds; K7o has reasonable equity vs a standard button opening range and dominates many hands I’ll face. I don’t want to 3-bet light and play a big deep-stacked pot out of position with a marginal hand — that invites difficult decisions against a competent opponent. I’ll flat call to keep the pot controlled, realize equity, and then fold to large turn/river aggression when I miss, or apply pressure / extract value if I hit a King or a strong draw on the flop.

I defended preflop with K♦7♣ to realize equity against a wide button opening. The flop (8♥ 5♠ 2♦) misses my hand but I still have an overcard (K) and no meaningful backdoor draws. We're out of position and stacks are deep relative to this small pot (pot = 600), so I want to keep the pot controlled and avoid getting bloated with a marginal holding. Flash is capable of frequent continuation bets here, so checking lets me see their intent without committing chips. Plan: check and evaluate—call small-sized continuation bets to realize equity and look to hit a king on later streets, but fold to large pressure since my hand has limited showdown value and almost no redraws. If Flash checks back I can consider a delayed small bet on the turn or take a free card.

On a dry flop like 8h-5s-2d, as the pre-flop aggressor, I should maintain pressure with a continuation bet. My Ace-high often has the best value currently, and a bet can deny equity to Minni's random overcards or force folds from hands that missed completely. A standard half-pot bet is appropriate here.

Flash's c-bet of 300 into the current pot is small (you get roughly 25% pot odds to call). With K♦7♣ I have one overcard and no meaningful draws, but versus a wide button continuation range my hand has ≳25% equity and is worth seeing the turn. I'm out of position and stacks are deep, so I don't want to 3‑bet light and bloat the pot with a marginal hand. Folding is too passive given the pot odds. Plan: call the 300 to realize equity and evaluate the turn. If I pair my King or the opponent checks I'll consider aggression; if I miss and face significant turn pressure I'll fold to avoid tough, bloated decisions.

I defended preflop with K♦7♣ and called the small flop c‑bet to realize equity. The 9♠ on the turn still misses me, but I’m first to act out of position and the pot is only 1,200. Flash opens/CBs wide and his check/turn range will include a lot of missed overcards and thin holdings that will fold to a small delayed c‑bet. I’ll make a modest probe (~1/3 pot = 400) to take the initiative, pick off those hands, and keep the pot controllable. Plan: fold to a sizable raise (K7 has poor showdown equity and no redraws); if called, re-evaluate the river and proceed cautiously — only invest more if I improve or get a clear read that I have value.

On the turn, Minni leads with a small bet of 400 into a 1200 pot after I continuation bet the flop. This 'donk bet' often signifies a marginal hand that has improved (like pairing the 9) or a draw trying to set a cheap price to see the river. My Ace-high still has some showdown value in heads-up play, and I am getting 4:1 pot odds (400 to call into a total pot of 1600). Given the favorable price and the possibility that Minni is betting a draw or a weaker Ace, I will call to see the river and evaluate.

Check. The river (8♥ 5♠ 2♦ 9♠ 9♣) paired the board and I have K♦7♣ with no pair or relevant redraws. I probed the turn and Flash called — that line is consistent with hands that were already in his calling range on the flop (8x, 5x, 2x, small pairs and some broadways) and the river pairing greatly increases the showdown strength of many of those hands (two pair or trips if he held a 9). My hand has almost no showdown value and very little fold equity now, so a bluff is very thin and a value bet is impossible. Best EV line is to check and concede the pot if he bets — fold to any meaningful river aggression; if he checks back I show or muck at showdown.
