On the river, the board pairs with a nine. Minni, who led into me on the turn, has now checked. This check suggests she doesn't have a nine (trips) and is likely in showdown mode with a marginal hand like a pair of eights, fives, or a missed draw. While a bluff might force a fold from a weak pair, my Ace-high has significant showdown value in heads-up play against her potential missed draws or lower high-card hands. There is little value in betting as I'm unlikely to be called by a worse hand. I will check back to see the showdown.

Heads-up on the button (I am the small blind) with 6♦9♦ — a suited connector with good post‑flop playability. Pot is small (150) and I act first preflop; calling 50 keeps the initiative with the big blind. Versus a single opponent and deep stacks (~88bb Effective), I want to seize initiative and play in position, so I’ll open‑raise. A standard 3bb size (raise to 300 total) pressures weak high cards, builds a pot when I have position, and preserves fold equity. I’m committing an extra 250 chips (~2.8% of my stack) — a small price for the initiative. Plan: if Flash 3‑bets large I’ll fold most of the time; if he calls or flats I’ll c‑bet favorable flops (especially with diamonds or connected boards) and use positional advantage to realize equity or pick pots.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Four offsuit. Minni, on the button, has raised to 3x the big blind. While the price to call is 2 to 1, Ten-Four offsuit is a very weak hand to play out of position. Given my significant chip lead and the fact that there are many hands remaining, there is no need to defend with such a marginal holding.

I am on the button with King-Jack offsuit, which is a very strong hand in heads-up play. Standard strategy dictates a raise here to build the pot and take control of the hand in position. I'll raise to 300, which has been my consistent sizing throughout the match.

Heads-up vs a standard 3x open to 300 from the SB. K♥T♦ is a playable, but not premium, hand heads-up. Calling 200 into a 400 pot gives ~3:1 pot odds (need ≈25% equity) — KTo has sufficient equity vs a wide SB opening range. I prefer to keep the pot manageable out of position, see a flop and play based on texture rather than inflate the pot with a marginal hand by 3-betting. I’ll call and evaluate on the flop (if faced with a 4-bet I’ll fold unless it’s small and I get good odds).

Heads-up on a Q♠Q♦9♥ flop and I’m out of position with K♥T♦ — I have no pair and essentially no meaningful draw. Flash opened widely pre, so his c-bet range is broad, but a paired-queen board heavily favors the opener (many Qx, pocket pairs and strong one-pair hands). Leading out OOP with a naked bluff is thin and will bloat the pot with very little equity. I’ll check to control the pot and see how he plays it. Plan: call small-to-medium continuation bets to realize equity and re-evaluate on the turn; fold to big, polarizing sizings or a raise; if he checks back I can consider a delayed bluff or a pot-control line on later streets depending on the runout. Check keeps the most options and minimizes risk with a marginal hand.

I have King-Jack offsuit on a paired flop of Q-Q-9. While I haven't hit a piece of the board, my hand has decent showdown value and I have position. A continuation bet on this texture is standard as it puts pressure on Minni's non-paired hands and allows me to represent a Queen or a pocket pair. I will bet 300, which is half the pot, to maintain aggression.

Flash opened wide pre and I checked the flop to control the pot with K♥T♦. His flop bet is small (300 into a 900 pot — ≈1/3 pot), so calling 300 gives me roughly 20% pot odds. KTo has limited raw equity vs a polarized Qx-heavy c-bet range, but versus a wide, small-bet continuation range it has about the equity needed to justify a call. Raising OOP with no pair and no real draw would bloat the pot and put me in a very uncomfortable spot versus strong Qx or pocket pairs; folding here would be too nitty given the tiny sizing and Flash’s aggressive tendencies. I'll call to keep the pot manageable, see the turn, and then fold to large turn aggression unless I pair or pick up a clear improvement (or re-evaluate for a delayed bluff if the runout is favorable).

Flash checked the turn. I’m out of position with K♥T♦ on Qd Qs 9h 8d — no pair and only thin river equity (a J, K or T helps, roughly mid-teens%). Villain opened wide pre and fired a small 1/3-pot c-bet on the flop, so his range contains a lot of Qx, medium pairs and bluffs. His turn check skews the range toward pot-control Qx and air. Betting OOP here is a thin bluff: it will fold only the worst of his holdings and will be called or raised by many hands that already beat me (Qx, 9x, decent Ax/Kx). I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand or face a raise from a deep-stacked opponent. Best line is pot control — check and see the river for free, then decide: chase a bluff/value bet only if the river gives a clear improvement (pair/straight) or if villain checks again and the card is highly favorable; fold to significant aggression without improvement.

I have King-high on a paired board. Minni called my flop continuation bet, which suggests she might have a piece of the board (like a 9 or a small pocket pair) or a draw. The 8 on the turn doesn't significantly help my hand. By checking back, I control the size of the pot and get to see a free river. I still have some showdown value against her air/floats, and I can potentially improve to a pair or a straight (with a Ten) on the river.

Board: Qd Qs 9h 8d 8s. My K♥T♦ has no pair — my best 5 is just the board. Villain opened wide pre, c‑bet a small size on the Q‑Q‑9 flop and then checked the turn; on this river any Qx or 8x in his flop‑betting range now makes a full house and beats me. Betting as a bluff here has very thin fold equity (many of his continuing combos are now full houses or strong value hands) and with deep stacks it’s not worth risking chips for a low chance of success. I’ll check and take the cheap showdown; if he bets I’ll fold to preserve my stack.

The board is double-paired with Queens and Eights (9h-Qd-Qs-8d-8s). My final hand is two pair, Queens and Eights, with a King kicker. Minni checked the river after I checked the turn. While I lose to any hand containing an Ace or a Nine, I have showdown value against her total air or small cards that didn't pair up. There is no value in betting because I am unlikely to get called by a worse hand, and checking allows me to get to a cheap showdown.

Heads-up on the button (last to act postflop) — J♠2♣ is weak but perfectly reasonable to open here to seize initiative and exploit the wide BB defending range. Table standard is ~3xBB; I’ll raise to 300 total to get fold equity, keep the sizing consistent with prior opens, and preserve a manageable pot with deep stacks. Plan: if Flash calls I’ll use position to C-bet favorable flops; if Flash 3-bets large I’ll concede this marginal hand.

I am in the Big Blind holding 9s 6d, a weak offsuit hand. Minni has raised to 3x from the button. While the pot odds are 3:1 to call, 9-6o is generally a fold in this position against a standard opening range. Given my significant stack lead and the fact that I'll be out of position for the rest of the hand, there is no need to defend with such a weak hand.

I am on the button (Small Blind) with Ace-Trey offsuit. In heads-up play, any Ace is a strong starting hand. Raising to 300 is a standard opening play to take control of the hand and potentially win the blinds immediately or set up a continuation bet on the flop.

Facing a button (SB) open to 300 while I’m in the big blind with 5♦2♣. This is one of the worst hands — very poor equity and terrible playability out of position. Calling 200 for a 400 pot only needs ~33% equity, but 5-2o is frequently dominated by a button’s raising range and won’t realize equity OOP on multi-street against a deep, often-aggressive opponent. A 3-bet bluff here is unnecessary and thin. With ~89 BB effective and many better spots to pick, the +EV play is to fold and conserve my stack.

Heads-up on the button (I posted SB). I have pocket deuces — a strong heads-up hand with excellent set-mining potential and great position postflop. With only the big blind to beat and deep effective stacks (8875 vs 30975), I want to take the initiative, get value from his wide defending range, and avoid giving a free flop. A standard, non-committal raise to 300 (3x BB) achieves that: it’s small relative to stacks, builds the pot when I hit a set, and gives fold equity against marginal hands. If Flash 3-bets big I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold to a large 3-bet or call a small squeeze depending on size), but 300 keeps my options open and maximizes expected value heads-up.

I am in the Big Blind with T9o. Minni has raised to 300 from the Button/Small Blind. T9o is a solid hand to defend in a heads-up situation, as it has good connectivity and potential for strong pairs or straights. Given the 2:1 pot odds and the fact that we are playing heads-up, calling to see a flop is a standard play.

On this flop, I have middle pair (Nines) with a Ten kicker on a board paired with Aces. Minni raised pre-flop, which often represents high cards or pairs, so she could easily have an Ace. However, since I am the Big Blind and checked, I need to see how Minni responds to this board. Since the action is on her and there is no current bet to call, I will check to see her next move. If she bets, I will have to decide whether my pair of Nines is good enough to continue against her range, but for now, checking is the standard move.

Flop is A♣ A♦ 9♣ and I hold 2♣2♥. My pocket deuces have almost no showdown value versus Flash’s cold-calling range from the BB — that range contains lots of A-x (many trips combos) and strong one-pair hands. Betting here would get called by Ax and be crushed by any Ace (or get check-raised), so it’s a poor spot to build the pot out of position. I want to control the pot and avoid committing chips with a hand that is far behind most of his range. Check and reevaluate: if Flash bets a very small probe I may call to see a turn, but I’ll fold to meaningful aggression. If he checks back I keep the pot small and can decide on turn whether to bluff-catch or apply pressure depending on the card and action.

I have a pair of Nines with a Ten kicker on a board showing trip Aces and a Queen. Minni checked back on the flop, which might suggest she lacks an Ace or is playing a marginal hand cautiously. The Queen on the turn is an overcard to my pair and potentially completes some of her broadway draws or makes her a better pair. Since I have some showdown value but am vulnerable to many cards in her range, I will check to keep the pot small and see how she responds.

Board is A♣ A♦ 9♣ Q♣; I hold 2♣2♥. My pair has almost no showdown value vs Flash’s calling range (many A-x, some Qx, club combos). I have 11 clean outs to improve on the river (9 clubs + 2 deuces ≈ 24% equity), so I want to realize that equity cheaply rather than build the pot out of position and get check‑raised or called by better hands. Betting here would often be called by Ax or Qx and be unprofitable; checking keeps the pot small and lets me see a free river where I can shove/value when I hit or fold to real aggression. Plan: check; call a small probing bet sometimes with my ~24% equity, fold to a large turn bet/check‑raise, and on the river bet for value if I make the flush/trips or bluff selectively if the runout and action justify it.

On the river, the board is 9c-Ac-Ad-Qc-9h, and I have the 9s in my hand, giving me a Full House (Nines full of Aces: 9-9-9-A-A). While any Ace in Minni's hand would give her a higher Full House (Aces full of Nines: A-A-A-9-9), she has checked on both the flop and the turn, which suggests she is unlikely to hold an Ace. Furthermore, I hold the third Nine, making it very unlikely she has the case Nine for four-of-a-kind. Since the pot was checked down, I will place a value bet of 400 into the 600 pot, hoping to get a call from a Queen, a smaller pocket pair, or a high-card hand that might interpret the bet as a late attempt to steal the pot.

Pot is 1,000 and Flash puts out a small river probe of 400. Calling 400 to win a pot of 1,800 after my call requires ~22.2% equity (400/1800). My 2♣2♥ gives me no improvement over the five-card board (A A 9 9 Q) — I can’t beat a river A, 9 or Q, but I will split with any hand that doesn’t pair one of those ranks. Rough counting: only around ~28–30% of reasonable BB calling range contains an A, 9 or Q that actually beats me, so ~70% of his range will, at worst, produce the board (a split). Given the pot odds and the fact this is a small probe rather than a polarizing shove, calling is +EV. Raising would be a pure bluff that can easily be crushed by full houses / made hands and is unnecessary. I’ll call the 400 to realize equity and split vs most of his missed/weak hands; fold to a large raise if he shows serious aggression.

I am on the button with a pocket pair of fours (4d 4s). Pocket pairs are strong starting hands, especially in a heads-up situation against Minni. Given the current stack sizes, I want to build a pot and maintain aggression. I will raise to 300 to take control of the hand pre-flop.

Call to 300. In heads‑up the SB’s open to 300 is very wide and I only need ~33% equity to justify calling 200 into the 400 pot. J♥5♦ is marginal but has enough showdown/backdoor equity vs a wide button range. 3‑betting light OOP with this hand just bloats the pot and will be called too often; folding is too nitty. I’ll call and play the flop cautiously out of position — continue with a pair, strong draws or favorable textures, otherwise check/fold to heavy pressure. Preserve stack and exploit postflop opportunities against Flash’s wide opens.

Flop analysis — K♠ 7♥ 6♥. I have J♥5♦: no pair, only a lone heart and backdoor improvement. Flash opened wildly from the SB, so his range contains a lot of Kx/overcards/weak made hands and bluffs, but I’m out of position and my raw equity is low. Goal is pot control and to avoid bloating a marginal holding with deep stacks. I’ll check and see what Flash does: if he checks back I get a free card; if he bets a small c‑bet (≈≤200 into 600) I will call to realize my backdoor equity and see a turn; I will fold to larger continuation bets (>~300) because I’m unlikely to have the fold or showdown equity vs his value range. No check‑raise or lead — preserve stack and wait for better streets or stronger holdings.

I have a pocket pair of fours on a 6h Ks 7h board. While I have an overpair to the six and seven, there is a king on the board and the texture is quite connected with potential straight and flush draws. Since Minni checked to me, I will place a continuation bet to test her hand and potentially take down the pot right now. A bet of half the pot (300) is standard here to charge draws and see where I stand.

Flash opened wide from the SB and fired a small c-bet of 300 into a 900 pot. I hold J♥5♦ on K♠ 7♥ 6♥: no pair, only backdoor heart possibilities and 6 simple outs (three J's and three 5's) to make a pair by river (~24% by river) plus a small chance of runner-runner hearts. Calling 300 into the current pot requires roughly 20% equity to break even (300 / (900 + 600) ≈ 20%), so versus a reasonably wide SB range this is marginally +EV. Raising OOP with this very marginal holding (no pair, only backdoor equity) bloats the pot unnecessarily and will get called too often; folding would be too passive given the cheap price and likely frequency of bluffs in Flash's range. I will call 300, keep the pot under control, and reevaluate on the turn: if I pair or the turn gives meaningful equity I can proceed, if the turn is a blank and Flash barrels large I'll fold to preserve my stack.

Board: 6♥ K♠ 7♥ K♥. My hand Jh5d: no pair, one heart. I called the flop to keep the pot controlled and realize my backdoor equity; the turn paired the board and added a third heart, so I now have a one‑card heart draw to the river (9 hearts left) plus J/5 pair outs — roughly 14 unique outs (~30% to improve by the river). However a lot of Flash’s range (Kx, two hearts, made sets) just improved or plays very well here, and I’m out of position. Betting OOP bloats the pot with a marginal holding and will be called or raised too often by an aggressive, wide opponent. I’ll check to control the pot and see what Flash does: if he checks back I get to a cheap river and can re-evaluate; if he fires a small bet (≈≤300 into the 1200 pot) I’ll call because the pot odds (~20%) justify my ~30% equity; if he barrels large (≈≥600 or a polarizing shove) I’ll fold to preserve my stack. Action: check.

On this board (6h Ks 7h Kh), the second King on the turn significantly changes the board texture. While I have two pair (Kings and Fours), my pair of fours is extremely weak, as any 6, 7, or King in Minni's range now beats me, and the third heart on the board completes potential flushes. Minni checked to me again, which could be a sign of weakness or a trap with a King or a flush. Given my hand's low showdown value and the likelihood that Minni has at least a pair or a draw that won't fold to a single bet, I will check behind to control the pot and see the river for free.

River pairs my Jack giving me one pair (J♣ on river pairs my J♥). Board K♠ K♥ 7♥ 6♥ J♣. Flash opened insanely wide from the SB and c‑bet the flop (300) but then checked the turn when the board paired (the turn line is showing a lot of weakness/missed hands rather than strong value hands or nut hearts). That makes Kx/trips and two‑heart nut flushes less likely than his raw preflop range would suggest (he could have them, but his turn check reduces their frequency).

My J♠5♦ (J♥5♦) is now medium-strength showdown value: ahead of a lot of missed overcards and bluffs but behind any king, a completed flush (if he held two hearts) or full houses. Betting for thin value here would mostly get called by better hands (Kx/full houses) or fold out only the worst hands — and it can induce a raise from hands that already beat me. I’m out of position and don’t want to bloat the pot with a one‑pair hand that has limited protection.

Given those factors, the lowest‑variance, highest‑EV line is to check and go to showdown. If Flash suddenly leads into me from the SB (unlikely given his turn check) I’ll call small/medium bets for pot odds but fold to large, polarized shoves. For now: check and show.

On this board of 6h-Ks-7h-Kh-Jc, my pocket fours are effectively counterfeited. The best five-card hand I can make is the board's two pair with the Jack kicker (K-K-J-7-6), as my fours are lower than the six and seven on the board. Minni has checked for the third time. While this might suggest weakness, she called my flop bet, indicating she likely has some piece of the board or an Ace-high. Since almost any card in her range (A, Q, T, 9, 8) beats me by improving the board's fifth card, I have essentially zero showdown value. However, betting into her is risky because she is unlikely to fold a 6, 7, or Jack. I will check behind and hope for a chopped pot or an unlikely win against a very low hand.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the small blind) Kd+7h is marginal but perfectly playable in position. Calling 50 is cheap, but limp/calling gives Flash good odds to see a flop and lets him dictate action. I want initiative and fold equity, so I open to a standard size to pressure the big blind and define the hand. Raising to 300 total (≈3x BB) is a normal heads-up open with my stack (~88bb) — it keeps worse hands from seeing a free flop, preserves postflop maneuverability if called, and is consistent with table sizing. If Flash 3-bets big I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold K7o out of position), but for now force a decision and take the lead.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Trey offsuit. The opponent (Minni) has raised to 300 from the button. While Q3o has some raw equity against a wide button opening range, it is a very weak hand that plays poorly out of position. Given the stack depths and the lack of connectivity or high-card strength, I will choose to fold and wait for a better spot.

K6o is a strong enough hand to open for a raise from the button in a heads-up situation. It has decent high-card value and I want to maintain an aggressive posture to put pressure on the big blind. A standard 3x raise is appropriate here.

Fold. Facing a standard 3x button open to 300 while I'm in the big blind out of position with 8♣5♠ — a weak, offsuit, disconnected hand with poor postflop playability. Pot is 400 and calling 200 requires ~33% equity to justify; 8-5o doesn't have that against a reasonable button opening range. Flash has a very deep, aggressive stack and will have positional advantage postflop, so calling often leads to difficult OOP decisions. A 3-bet bluff here is unattractive (no good blockers and too high-variance vs his stack/ tendencies). Preserve my 88BB stack and fold to this open.

Heads-up on the button with Ad3h is a clear open-raise spot — the hand has good heads-up equity and I want to play postflop in position. The pot is 150 and calling 50 (limping) concedes initiative and gives Flash cheap realization; a raise to 300 (3x BB) is standard at this table and gives useful fold equity while still small relative to my 8,775 stack. Sizing rationale: 300 pressures weaker big-blind holdings, reduces profitable 3-bets compared with a min-raise, and still leaves plenty of chips for postflop play if called. Plan: if called I’ll c-bet appropriate textures and use position to extract value or fold when clearly dominated; if met with a large 3-bet I’ll respect the strength and fold rather than committing with a weak kicker. Overall raising to 300 maximizes my long-term expected value here.

In a heads-up situation, defending the Big Blind with a King-high hand like K6o against a standard 3x raise is a standard play. The pot odds are favorable (calling 200 into a 400 pot), and my hand has sufficient equity against a typical button opening range. I will call and see a flop.

On this dry board of 7h-4d-Ts, my King-high (Ks6c) hasn't improved but maintains some back-door potential. Minni is the pre-flop aggressor. Standard strategy in a heads-up pot is to check to the aggressor. If she bets, I will re-evaluate based on the size, as King-high has some showdown value and can potentially peel one street depending on the price. For now, checking is the most appropriate action.

I raised preflop and villain (BB) called — heads-up his calling range is wide and the flop (7h‑4d‑Ts) is relatively dry. I hold Ad3h: an overcard Ace with a weak kicker and only very weak backdoor potential, so my hand has decent showdown equity but little made strength. A half‑pot continuation bet (300 into 600) applies pressure to a lot of his missed holdings (Kx, Qx, small pairs, many junk hands), extracts value from worse A‑x, and keeps the initiative while remaining small enough to fold to serious resistance. Plan: bet 300 to take the pot now; if I face a large check‑raise I will give it up (my kicker is poor), if called I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn and barreL only with a meaningful improvement or a turn that favors my perceived range.

On this dry 10-7-4 rainbow board, my King-high (Ks6c) has some marginal showdown value but did not connect with the flop. Minni, as the pre-flop aggressor, has followed up with a standard continuation bet of 1/3 pot (300 into 600). While I have two overcards to the 7 and 4, the Ten is well within her opening range. Given the stack depths and the fact that I'm out of position with only King-high and no real draws, calling here is speculative and likely leads to difficult decisions on later streets. I will fold and wait for a better spot to contest a pot.

I am holding pocket eights (8h 8s) on the button. This is a very strong starting hand in a heads-up situation. I will raise to the standard 300 chips to build the pot and maintain an aggressive posture, which has been my consistent opening size throughout the match.

Heads-up vs Flash (button/SB) who opened to 300. I have 7♣8♦ — a playable, connected but offsuit hand that has decent equity vs a wide button opening range. Pot is 400 and it costs me 200 more to call (my total commitment will be 300). Calling here gives ~3:1 pot odds and lets me realize equity and see a flop without bloating a pot out of position. A 3‑bet with 78o OOP is suboptimal (it just enlarges the pot while I’ll still be out of position and Flash has a very deep stack), and folding is too tight heads‑up. I will call to see the flop and re-evaluate: continue with pairs/strong draws, fold to heavy pressure on dry flops or a significant 3‑bet preflop.

Heads‑up out of position on a low, paired flop (4♦4♣2♦). With 7♣8♦ I have no pair and only a very weak backdoor diamond/straight possibility — equity is tiny. Flash opened wide from the button so they'll c‑bet a lot, but leading or barreling OOP with air just bloats the pot unnecessarily. I’ll check and keep the pot under control: plan to call small continuation bets to realize equity (roughly ≤1/3 pot) and fold to larger, committed pressure; if Flash checks back I get a free turn to potentially improve or take a cheaper stab later. Checking is the lowest‑variance, most +EV line here.

On a paired board of 4-4-2, my pocket eights are an overpair, which is a very strong hand. Minni checked, which is expected as the defender. I will place a continuation bet of 300 (half-pot) to extract value from smaller pairs (like 33 or 22), Ace-high hands, and diamond draws, while also protecting my hand against overcards that might come on the turn.

I checked the flop to keep the pot small with a completely missed hand (7♣8♦) on 4♦4♣2♦. With the pot at 900 and a bet of 300 I need ≈25% equity to justify a call (300/(900+300)). I have no pair and only remote backdoor outs; versus the button’s wide preflop raising/c-betting range my equity here is likely below that threshold. My plan was to call small continuation bets (~≤1/3 pot) and fold to larger committed pressure — this 1/2‑pot bet crosses that line. A check‑raise bluff OOP with no real draw is too risky given his deep stack. I’ll fold and conserve chips for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) I should be opening a wide range — K♥2♣ is weak but perfectly fine to raise for initiative and fold equity. The pot is 150 and calling costs only 50, but calling surrenders initiative and lets the big blind see a cheap flop with positional advantage postflop; a min-raise to 200 is the standard heads-up open size to steal the blind and keep the pot manageable. If Flash 3-bets big I’ll fold this holding; if he calls I’ll use my button (postflop) advantage to continuation-bet or control the pot depending on the texture. Total to put in the pot (including my SB) is 200.

84o is a very weak hand to defend from the big blind, even against a min-raise. While the pot odds are 3-to-1, the hand realizes its equity poorly out of position. Given my significant stack lead, there is no reason to play suboptimal hands and potentially get into a difficult post-flop spot.

In a heads-up situation as the small blind with K3o, I have a King-high hand which is strong enough to open or call against the big blind's wide defending range. Given the current stack sizes where I have a significant lead and the relatively low cost to complete the bet, raising is a standard aggressive play to take control of the hand or win the pot pre-flop. I will raise to 300.

Heads-up vs Flash (button) who opened to 300. I hold K♥7♥ in the big blind — a suited king with reasonable equity and backdoor flush potential. Pot is 400 and I must put in 200 more to call, so I’m getting ~33% immediate pot odds. Flash’s button opening range will be wide heads-up, so K7s has playable equity and good postflop playability, but I’m out of position. A small 3-bet or a min‑raise is possible, but with a marginal hand and OOP I don’t want to invest more and take the initiative into a deeper stack opponent. Folding is too tight here. Plan: call to see a flop, continue with top pair/strong draws, and be ready to fold to heavy aggression on dry boards where I miss.

Check. I’m out of position with K♥7♥ on Qs-4s-2h and I’ve completely missed — only a very poor runner-runner heartbackdoor. Flash opened wide and will c-bet a large portion of the time, so I prefer pot control and the ability to float rather than bloating the pot OOP with a marginal hand. Plan: check and call small probes to float (≈≤1/3 pot ≈ 200) and look to take the pot on the turn; fold to large bets/disciplined pressure (≈≥1/2 pot ≈ 300) unless I improve (a King or a meaningful heart) or get a strong read to continue. If Flash checks back I get a free turn card and can lead or take another line if I pair or pick up equity.

On a board of Qs-4s-2h, my K-high has some showdown value but is vulnerable. Minni checked, which is standard for the big blind after calling a pre-flop raise. This dry board doesn't hit a lot of ranges, although Queen-x is certainly in her range. A continuation bet here serves to win the pot immediately if she missed or has a weak draw, and it prices out hands like random undercards. Since I have a significant stack lead and want to maintain pressure, I will bet half the pot.

Flash has c-bet small (300 into a 600 preflop pot -> pot now 900). I hold K♥7♥ and have missed the flop (Q♠4♠2♥) — only weak backdoor heart/fold equity and a King overcard. Button open-raises wide heads-up and will c-bet a very wide range here, so a cheap call to float and retain options is the best play. Calling 300 (≈1/3 pot) keeps the pot manageable, lets me see a turn card, and allows me to either take the pot away on a favorable turn or fold to strong turn aggression if I don’t improve. Raising OOP with this marginal hand is too thin and folding is too nitty given the price and opponent’s wide range. Plan: call now; if I pair a King or pick up a heart I’ll start getting aggressive, otherwise fold to large disciplined barrels (~≥1/2 pot) on the turn.

Board is 2♥ 4♠ Q♠ 7♠; I hold K♥7♥ — I’ve just paired my 7 (second pair) but the board has three spades, so many of Flash’s calling/c-bet combos turn into a made flush and top-pair (Qx) still dominates me. Pot is 1,200 and there is no bet to face on the turn. With a marginal pair and a vulnerable kicker I want pot control rather than bloating the pot OOP. Betting here will get called by many hands that already beat me (Qs, any spade flush) and only fold pure air occasionally, so it’s thin EV to bluff; checking preserves fold equity on later streets and lets me see a river cheaply. Plan: check and evaluate the river — call small bets but fold to large, and only get aggressive if I improve or get a clear read.

On the turn, the 7 of spades completes potential flush draws and doesn't improve my King-high hand. After Minni called my continuation bet on the flop, it is likely she has at least a piece of the board (a Queen, a 4, or a 2) or a draw. Since she checked again on the turn, I have the option to take a free card. Betting again (double barreling) with just King-high is risky here, as the turn card is more likely to help her range or entice her to stay in with a draw. Checking back allows me to see the river for free and potentially win at showdown if the hand stays small and she has a worse hand like a lower Ace-high or a missed straight draw.

I have K♥7♥ — second pair with a decent kicker on 2♥-4♠-Q♠-7♠-6♣. Flash opened wide and c‑bet the flop, then checked turn and checked the river; that line signals weakness or missed equity more often than a value-heavy line, but his calling range still contains too many Qx and two‑spade combos that beat me. Betting risks getting thin‑called by hands that already beat my pair while only folding out pure air I can beat if I just check. Pot control is the right play heads‑up with a vulnerable second pair OOP: I’ll check and take the showdown rather than bloating the pot into marginal spots.

On the river, the board is 2h-4s-Qs-7s-6c. I have King-high (Kd-3h). I raised pre-flop and continuation bet the flop, but checked the turn when the third spade arrived. Minni has checked all the way on the river. While King-high rarely wins at showdown, it does have some marginal value against busted straight draws or lower high cards. Given that Minni called the flop bet, she likely has at least a piece of the board or a draw. If she had a flush or a strong Queen, she might have bet the river for value. However, she could also be planning to check-call with a weak pair. Since I am significantly ahead in total chips and the pot is relatively small, I will check back and take the showdown rather than risking a bluff against a range that could easily include a small pair.

Raise to 200 total (2x BB). Heads-up on the button I should be opening very wide — 9♣4♣ has suit and some playability, so I want initiative and fold equity. A 2x raise is standard: it keeps the pot manageable with a marginal hand, pressures the big blind, and allows me to c-bet favorable flops or check and realize equity in position. If Flash 3-bets large I can fold this marginal holding; if he calls I’ll proceed postflop with position and look to exploit favorable runouts. (Raising to 200 means I add 150 more chips, for a 200 total commitment this street.)

I am holding Jack-Nine offsuit in the Big Blind. Minni has raised to 200 from the Small Blind (a min-raise). This gives me excellent pot odds to call (100 to win 300), and J9o has enough high-card value and connectivity to play profitably in a heads-up pot, especially given the current stack depths. Folding would be overly conservative, and while 3-betting is an option, calling allows me to see a flop and realize my equity without bloating the pot unnecessarily out of position.

On this Queen-high flop (Qh-8h-2c), I have Jack-high with no direct draw, though a Ten on the turn would give me an open-ended straight draw. I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor. The standard and most prudent play here is to check to Minni to see how she proceeds. Leading out (donk betting) doesn't serve much purpose with this hand strength.

I raised preflop to take the initiative with a wide heads‑up range. Flop is 2♣ Q♥ 8♥ — I have 9♣4♣: no pair but a runner‑runner (backdoor) club flush possibility and I'm in position. Flash checked, so c‑betting is the standard play to use my initiative, take folds from unpaired hands and small pairs, and charge any heart draws. I don't want to overcommit with a marginal hand, so I choose a sizing that preserves fold equity but keeps the pot manageable. Betting 250 (~60% pot) achieves that: it puts pressure on worse hands and draws, but allows me to fold if met with significant resistance (check‑raise). If called I'll proceed cautiously — continue with clubs or strong turns, otherwise check back to realize equity.

On a board of Qh-8h-2c, my Jack-Nine offsuit has very little current value. I have two overcards to the 8 and 2, but I am behind any Queen or pocket pair. While a Ten would give me an open-ended straight draw, I am facing a bet of 250 into a pot of 400 (62.5% pot) from Minni. Calling would mean peeling with only a gutshot-like potential or hoping to hit a Jack or Nine that might not even be the best hand. Given that I am out of position and have no piece of this board, folding is the most disciplined and mathematically sound decision.

I am on the button with 5d 6d, which is a strong suited connector and an excellent hand for opening in heads-up play. I have a significant stack advantage over Minni. Raising here allows me to take control of the pot and leverage my position post-flop. A standard 3x open to 300 is appropriate.

Heads-up on the button vs my big blind — Flash opened to 300, a very common steal size. Pocket Jacks are strong heads-up and perform well as a 3-bet for value and fold equity against a wide button opening range. I’ll 3-bet to 1,200 total: large enough to charge worse broadways and medium pairs and to make some hands fold, but leaves me plenty of stack (≈8.4k) to play postflop. Plan: if he calls I’ll continuation-bet favorable, run-of-the-mill flops and control the pot on heavy overcard textures; if he 4-bets big or shoves I’ll re-evaluate (JJ is often dominated vs a polar 4-bet, so I’ll fold to extreme all-ins but can continue vs smaller, non-polar 4-bets).

I opened with a standard 3x raise with 5d 6d, and Minni has responded with a 3-bet to 1200. I am getting 3:1 pot odds (900 to call into a total pot that will be 2400) and I have the advantage of position for the rest of the hand. 5d 6d is a strong suited connector that performs well in 3-bet pots post-flop, especially when deep-stacked (Minni still has over 80 big blinds). I will call to see a flop and utilize my positional advantage.

I 3-bet pre with JJ and got a call, so my range is strong and I still have initiative. The flop Kc 4h 4d is fairly dry with one overcard (K) that hits the caller’s defending range, but there are also a lot of hands (A-high, Q-high, weaker broadways, medium pairs) that missed. I’ll make a small c-bet (~1/3 pot) to get fold equity, deny free cards, and extract value from worse pairs and broadways while keeping the pot manageable with my medium-strength pair. If he folds I take the pot; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn; if he check-raises large I’ll heavily discount my hand and likely fold to a polarized shove.

On a board of Kc 4h 4d, the advantage lies heavily with the pre-flop 3-bettor (Minni), as her range is much more likely to contain strong Kings (AK, KQ) and overpairs (AA, QQ, JJ). My hand, 5d 6d, has no pair and only very weak backdoor straight and flush possibilities. While the 800 bet is small (1/3 pot), calling with 6-high in a 3-bet pot is not profitable without a significant draw or a clear path to bluffing. Since the board is very dry and Minni's range is strong, I will fold and preserve my stack.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the SB) with 9♥5♣ — a marginal hand but playable because I have position and the stacks are deep (~109 BB). I don’t want to just limp and give the big blind free equity; opening a wide range is standard heads‑up to seize initiative and use position postflop. I’ll raise to 300 (3×BB) — large enough to get some folds and define Flash’s range, but small relative to effective stacks so I can continue postflop. Plan: fold to a sizable 3‑bet (my hand has poor equity), call small-ish 3‑bets only if the odds and read justify it, and c‑bet most dry flops to leverage position.

I have been dealt pocket Jacks, a premium hand in heads-up play. My opponent, Minni, has been playing aggressively from the button. I want to build a pot for value and potentially end the hand pre-flop or define her range. A 3-bet to 1000 is consistent with my previous betting patterns with strong hands.

I opened to 300 heads‑up with 9♥5♣ intending to use position postflop, but Flash 3‑bet to 1000 which is a sizable shove-sized re‑raise. The pot is 1300 and calling 700 would make the pot 2000, so I need roughly 700/2000 ≈ 35% equity to break even on a call. 9♥5♣ offsuit has poor equity and is frequently dominated by a standard 3‑betting range (pairs, broadways, suited Aces/connectors). With deep stacks this would put me in difficult multi‑street spots against an aggressive opponent, and my preflop plan was to fold marginal hands to a sizable 3‑bet. I preserve stack and wait for a better spot — fold.
K8o is a strong enough hand to raise from the button in a heads-up situation. I will raise to 300 to take the initiative and either win the pot immediately or play a controlled pot in position.

Heads-up vs the button (SB) opening to 300: their range is wide. I’m in the big blind with K♣7♦ — not strong enough to 3‑bet for value and I don’t want to bloat a pot out of position with a dominated hand. Pot is 400 and calling 200 more gives me ~3:1 effective odds to see a flop; K7o has reasonable equity and good postflop playability versus a wide open. Plan: call to see a flop, continue only if I improve (top pair, good draw) or can realize equity cheaply; fold to heavy aggression on dry flops. So I’ll defend by calling.

Heads-up vs a wide SB opener and I’m out of position with K♥7♦ on a very dry 9♠ T♣ 4♥ board. I missed - no pair and only a remote backdoor heart/straight. Pot is small (600) and I have some showdown value as king-high, so I don’t want to bloat the pot OOP by barreling with air. Plan: check and see what the button does — call small continuation bets to realize equity, fold to large aggression or apply more selective bluffs later when I pick up equity or position. Preserve stack and keep the pot manageable.

On a board of 9s-Tc-4h, I have King-high with an overcard. As the pre-flop aggressor, I will place a continuation bet of half the pot (300 chips). This allows me to take the pot down immediately if the opponent missed the flop, while still keeping the pot manageable if I am called. My King provides some limited showdown value if the hand continues without further improvement.

I missed the flop with K♥7♦ on 9♠‑T♣‑4♥ — no pair and only remote backdoors. Flash is a wide SB opener and the flop bet is small (300 into the pot → pot now 900), so calling gives me ~3:1 pot odds (need ~25% equity). Against a wide c‑bet range my K7 has reasonable showdown equity and it’s cheap to continue out of position. Raising here is unnecessary and risky — I’d bloat the pot OOP and get value‑or‑folded by worse hands I want to keep. Plan: call the 300 to see a turn, fold to large raises or heavy turn aggression on blanks, and play aggressively if I pair a King/7 or pick up strong equity.

I’m out of position with K♥7♦ on 9♠‑T♣‑4♥‑T♦ and I still have no pair or meaningful draw. Flash is a wide, aggressive opener who c‑bet the flop; I called to realize equity cheaply. There’s no value to fire a bluff OOP and betting here only bloats the pot when I’m behind to most of the hands that continue (Tx, sets, two pair, strong queens/aces). Checking preserves stack and lets me either see a cheap river or induce a bluff from his wide river‑barrel frequency. Plan: check and fold to significant turn pressure, call small turn bets to realize equity, and only become aggressive if I pair or pick up real equity.

The turn pairs the Ten on the board (9s-Tc-4h-Td). My King-high has some showdown value against various draws or lower air hands that Minni might have called the flop with. Since Minni checked, I will check behind to control the pot and see a free river card. Betting here doesn't achieve much as I'm mostly called by better hands and I don't want to face a check-raise on this board.

River: I have K♥7♦ on 9♠ T♣ 4♥ T♦ A♠. Villain opened preflop, c‑bet the flop and then we both checked the turn — I never improved and have only king‑high. There is no bet to call, and betting as a bluff OOP is thin: I don’t block many A/T combos, the preflop raiser’s range contains lots of hands that won’t fold (Ax, Tx, some 9x), and a river raise would put me in a bad spot. Best play is to check to showdown and fold if he fires — preserve my stack rather than bloat the pot with a weak bluff.

On the river, an Ace appears, which is a significant scare card. Minni has checked for the third consecutive time after calling my flop bet. While my King-high has very little showdown value against her likely range (which could include small pairs or a 9), checking behind is the most prudent play. I'm not confident a bluff would force her to fold a pair, and checking allows me to win against her missed straight draws (like 8-7 or J-8) without risking more chips.

Heads-up on the button (SB) with 6♦4♠ — deep stacks (~105bb) and pot is only 150 with 50 to call. In heads-up I should be playing a wide, aggressive range from the button to seize initiative and use position postflop. 6-4o is marginal but perfectly reasonable to raise here; a small raise to 200 total (min-raise) keeps the pot manageable, applies pressure to the BB and lets me play the flop in position. Plan: fold to a significant 3-bet (little equity vs a strong 3-bet), otherwise if called use position to c-bet favorable flops and control the pot on dry/high boards. Raise to 200.

I have been dealt a pocket pair (6s, 6h) in the big blind. The small blind (Minni) has raised to 200. This is a very standard situation where I have a strong enough hand to either call or 3-bet. Given the stack sizes (Minni has about 10k left, I have nearly 30k), and the relatively small raise size, calling to see a flop is a solid play, but 3-betting for value and to take control of the hand is also effective. In a heads-up situation, pocket sixes are well ahead of an average opening range. I will 3-bet to 700 to build the pot and define Minni's range.

I opened wide from the button which is fine heads-up, but facing Flash’s 3‑bet to 700 this hand becomes marginal. The pot is 900 and calling 500 means I need ~500/1400 ≈ 35.7% equity to break even. 6♦4♠ has very poor equity vs a typical BB 3‑bet range (dominated by Ax, broadways and many suited hands) — well below that threshold — and I’ll be playing a bloated pot out of position on future streets if I call. A small 4‑bet bluff to 1,200 would be thin too (Flash calls/shoves a lot and has a deep stack), so this is not a profitable spot to force the issue. Fold and preserve the stack to pick off better spots in position.

I am currently holding 3s 9d on the button in a heads-up situation. While I have a significant chip lead over Minni, this hand is extremely weak and does not play well post-flop. Given the current game state and the fact that I want to protect my stack while waiting for better opportunities, folding this hand pre-flop is the most prudent strategy.

Heads-up in the SB/button with Kc6d. This is a marginal but playable hand in position — calling 50 (total commit 100) preserves pot control, lets me realize equity and use position postflop. Raising to 200 is an option as a steal, but Flash has shown frequent aggression and 3-bets; I don’t want to inflate the pot or get 3-bet out of position with a dominated hand. I’ll call the 50 to see a flop and proceed based on texture (bet/raise when I hit top pair or strong draws, check/fold to heavy aggression when I miss).

With Ten-Seven offsuit in the Big Blind, and Minni simply limping in from the Small Blind, I have the option to check and see a flop for free. My hand has some playability but isn't strong enough to value raise, and there's no immediate need to bluff. I will take the free information of the flop.

The flop has come out paired with Aces (Ad Ah 4d). I have Ten-high and no real connection to this board. In a limped pot, there is little incentive to bluff into a paired board where the opponent could easily be holding an Ace or a small pair. Checking back allows me to see the turn for free and keeps the pot small.

Flop: Ad Ah 4d — I hold Kc6d. I completely missed: no pair, no meaningful draw (only a tiny runner-runner diamond possibility). Flash checked to me; heads‑up this board is highly favorable to any Ace or pocket pair and Flash has shown he’s sticky and capable of calling/raising postflop. I could attempt a small c‑bet to steal, but the risk of a check‑raise or being called by better hands is high and I have essentially no showdown equity. With a 10k stack and the pot only 200, I’ll control the pot and check down — preserve chips, fold to significant aggression on later streets, and only turn into an aggressor if I improve (pair a K/6 or get safe runouts) or if Flash shows clear weakness on the turn.

The board currently shows four-of-a-kind Aces (Ad Ah As 4d). In this situation, every player has at least quads. The winner is determined by the fifth card (the kicker). My best five-card hand is A-A-A-A-T. If Minni has a King, Queen, or Jack, she beats me. If she has a Ten, we chop. If she has anything lower than a Ten, I win. Since the pot is limped and very small (200), there is no value in betting; a better kicker will never fold, and a worse kicker is unlikely to call. Checking is the most logical play to see the river for free and potentially reach a cheap showdown or chop.

Board is trips Aces (Ad Ah As) with a 4d; I hold Kc6d and have no pair and essentially no draw (can only hit a lone K or 6 on the river — very weak two outs). Flash checked flop and checked turn; this could be a trap with an Ace or a pot-control line, and betting/raising here would bloat the pot with almost no showdown equity and little fold equity. Pot is small (200) relative to my 10k stack — preserve chips and avoid getting exploited. I will check and see the river; plan is to fold to meaningful aggression unless I pair up or the river action clearly indicates a missed hand from my opponent.

The board shows triple Aces (Ad Ah As) with a Queen (Qs) and a Four (4d). My best five-card hand is A-A-A-Q-T. Since the pot was limped and has been checked through the flop and turn, it is unlikely either player holds the fourth Ace (Ac) for quads. However, any hand with a King or Jack kicker (or a pocket pair higher than sevens) would beat my Ten kicker. There is no value in betting here; a better hand will not fold, and a worse hand is unlikely to call. Checking is the most prudent action to reach a cheap showdown or induce a check-back.

Board has trip Aces (Ad Ah As 4d Qs) and I hold Kc6d — I missed completely with essentially zero showdown equity (only two weak pair-outs). Flash has checked every street; that line is consistent with either a pot-control Ace or a disguised strong hand, and bluffing here has very little fold equity against hands that beat me. Pot is tiny (200) relative to my 10k+ stack, so the correct, low-variance line is to check and conserve chips; I will fold to any meaningful river bet and only show down if Flash also checks.

I have Jc9c on the button (small blind) in a heads-up situation against Minni. This is a very strong speculative hand that plays well post-flop due to its flush and straight potential. Since the current pot is small and I have a significant chip lead (nearly 3:1), I want to play this hand aggressively to put pressure on Minni. A standard raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is a good way to build the pot while maintaining control of the hand.

Flash (button/SB) opened to 300. I hold K♠ T♣ in the big blind with ~104BB effective against a very deep, aggressive opponent who opens widely from the button. KTo is a strong heads‑up holding but it's vulnerable to 4‑bets and playing large pots OOP. I prefer to keep the pot controlled and realize equity against his wide opening range rather than inflate the pot with a light 3‑bet. I will call the 200 more (300 total) and evaluate the flop — proceed aggressively with top pair or strong draws, fold or pot‑control on dry boards if he barrels big, and fold to a large preflop 4‑bet since KTo has poor roll‑off vs a polarized 4‑bet range.

I called preflop with K♠T♣ and the flop (9♥ 2♥ 8♦) is fairly dry with two hearts — I have two overcards but no immediate made hand or relevant single‑card straight draw. Flash opened wide from the button and will c‑bet a high frequency, so my priority is pot control and equity realization out of position. Checking keeps the pot small (600) and lets me see how he responds: I’ll call small‑to‑medium continuation bets to continue against his wide range and re‑evaluate on the turn, but I’ll avoid bloating the pot with a thin bluff here (and I’m vulnerable to hearts and paired boards). Therefore I check and plan to call reasonable c‑bets and fold to heavy pressure or turn aggression that looks value‑heavy.

I have flopped top pair with a Jack kicker on a board of 9h-2h-8d. This is a strong hand in a heads-up pot, but the board is somewhat coordinated with straight draws and a heart flush draw. Minni checked to me, which is expected. I will place a continuation bet of 400 into the 600 pot to extract value from weaker hands like middle pairs, pocket pairs, or draws, and to protect my equity against those draws.

Flash is a very wide, aggressive button opener who c-bets a lot. I checked to keep the pot small with K♠T♣ and now face a smallish flop probe (400 into a 1000 pot ≈ 40% pot). Calling 400 here gives me ~22% break‑even equity to see the turn and KTo has enough equity vs a wide c‑bet range (plus 6 clean outs to pair). Raising OOP with two overcards would bloat the pot and force difficult decisions on later streets, so I prefer to call, control the pot, and re‑evaluate on the turn. Plan: call to see a turn — fold to heavy turn pressure if the board develops in a way that strongly favors his value range (hearts, paired board, or big raise), and if I pair my K or T I will start extracting value/raise.

Flash checked to me on the turn after c-betting the flop. I hold K♠T♣ on 9♥ 2♥ 8♦ A♣ — no pair and the Ace is a very scary card for Flash’s wide opening range (lots of Ax and hands that benefit from an A‑turn). Betting here as a bluff has poor risk/reward: I may fold out some marginal hands, but many of Flash’s checking combos (A‑x, some hearts, medium pairs) will call or check‑raise, and a check‑raise on deep stacks would put me in a very uncomfortable spot with no showdown value. Pot control is the priority: checking keeps the pot manageable and lets me see the river cheaply and realize my equity (I can continue only with improvement or cheap rivers). Therefore I check and plan to fold to large turn aggression from him, call smallish river bluffs/cbets, and extract if I pair a K or T on the river.

I currently hold second pair (nines) with a Jack kicker. The Ace on the turn is a significant scare card. While it is in my perceived range as the pre-flop aggressor, Minni called a bet on the flop and might have been floating with an Ace or has now improved to a pair of Aces. Given that my hand has decent showdown value but is now likely beaten by any Ace, checking back is the best move for pot control. This allows me to see the river for free and potentially catch a bluff or value-bet if the river is favorable and she checks again.

I check. I never improved on A♣ 6♦ river and K♠T♣ has poor showdown value versus Flash’s wide but aggressive raising/c-betting range — he likely has many Ax, one-pair 9x/8x combos or medium hands that will call a bluff. Betting here would bloat the pot with little fold equity (many of his check-turn combos will call a small bet) and we should prioritize pot control with deep stacks. Check and see showdown; if he bets into me I’ll fold to meaningful river pressure and only entertain calling very small thin bluffs.

I have second pair (Nines) with a Jack kicker. The board has an Ace, which slowed down the action on the turn. Minni has checked the river as well, suggesting she doesn't have a strong Ace or has a marginal hand like an Eight or a small pocket pair. My hand has significant showdown value in this heads-up pot, and there is little to be gained by betting, as most hands that call would likely beat me or be a thin value-call from an Eight. Checking back allows me to realize my equity and win the pot against her range of missed draws and smaller pairs.

Heads-up on the button with A♥ K♦ — a premium hand and massive equity vs the big blind’s very wide defending range. Limp/calling is unnecessarily passive; I want to take the initiative, build a pot in position, and deny equity to speculative hands. I’ll raise to 300 total (3x the BB), which is big enough to extract value from worse hands and fold out some weak draws but not so large that I commit my deep ~98BB stack unnecessarily. Plan: if called I’ll continuation-bet most flops (especially A/K-high textures), and if I face a sizable 3-bet I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing and range (call or 4-bet/commit vs large overbets).

In a heads-up situation, Ten-Five suited is a standard defending hand from the big blind. The pot odds are 2:1, as I need to call 200 more to win a total pot of 400. Given the equity of a suited Ten against a typical button opening range, calling to see a flop is the most appropriate move, despite being out of position.

On this flop of Jc-6d-5s, I have bottom pair with a Ten kicker (5h). The board is relatively dry, although there are some straight draws (47, 78, 34). As the Big Blind defender, I should check to the pre-flop aggressor. If Minni bets, I will likely call one street to see if I improve or if she is continuation-betting air, given the pot odds and my pair.

Heads-up in position with A♥K♦ after my 3x preflop raise. The flop 5♠6♦J♣ is fairly dry and Flash checked — I have two overcards and good equity vs a wide BB defending range, plus fold equity. I want to take the initiative, deny free cards to draws/small pairs, and extract value from worse Jx, pairs and draws, so I’ll continuation-bet ~2/3 pot. A 400-chip c-bet applies meaningful pressure while leaving room to navigate turn decisions: if called I’ll reevaluate based on the turn card; if face a large check-raise I’ll give strong consideration to folding to avoid bloating the pot with just overcards.

I have bottom pair (fives) on a fairly dry board. Minni, as the pre-flop aggressor, is likely to continuation-bet a wide range of hands on this texture. Getting 2.5:1 pot odds, folding a pair is too weak. Calling allows me to see a turn card and re-evaluate Minni's strength on the next street without bloating the pot with a marginal hand.

On the turn, a King of Clubs appears, which is an overcard to my bottom pair of fives. Since I was the caller on the previous street and I am out of position, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. Leading out here doesn't accomplish much against Minni's range, which likely includes many hands that improved with the King or already had me beaten. By checking, I can see how she reacts to the overcard and decide whether to continue based on the size of her bet, or potentially get a free look at the river if she checks back.

I hold A♥K♦ and the K♣ on the turn gives me top pair with the best possible kicker. The pot is 1,400 and Flash checked after calling my 400 flop c-bet, which leaves him with a wide calling range (Jx, small/medium pairs, some Kx, occasional draws). I want to extract value and deny free cards to any backdoor draws, so I should take the initiative. A ~2/3-pot bet targets Jx and most pairs while not being so large that I fold out too many worse hands. I’ll bet 900. If I get check-raised large I’ll heavily discount it (likely sets or KJ) and fold to massively overbet raises; I’ll call smaller raises and re-evaluate on the river if called.

Minni is firing a second barrel on a turn card (King) that significantly favors her pre-flop raising range. My bottom pair of fives with a weak kicker is now in a very difficult spot. Calling 900 into 1400 out of position with such a marginal hand is likely negative EV, especially since I will likely face another large bet on the river. It's time to fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with A5o. In heads-up play, an Ace is a very strong starting hand. I will raise to 300 to build the pot and take control of the hand. Minni has been active and has roughly 100 big blinds left, so I want to maintain pressure while having a hand with reasonable showdown value or a chance to improve.

Fold. Flash opened to 300; calling 200 into a 400 pot gives me ~33% pot odds. My 2♠6♣ is a very weak hand with far less than the required equity against a reasonable button opening range and plays poorly out of position. A minimum 3‑bet to 500 would commit too many chips as a bluff with almost no fold equity from this opponent and little playability postflop. Preserve my stack and wait for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button with A♦5♦ — a very playable, suited ace. I want initiative and to deny Flash a free flop, so I’ll open-raise rather than just completing. A standard 3x BB open is appropriate here: raise to 300 total (I’ve already posted 50, so I’m putting in 250 more). This size extracts value from Flash’s wide defending range, keeps the pot manageable with deep stacks, and preserves positional advantage for postflop play. Plan: c-bet favorable flops, use position to realize equity, and re-evaluate vs 3-bets (call small/medium 3-bets occasionally, fold or 4-bet selectively to large aggression).

I am holding 7h 2h in the Big Blind. While the hand is suited, 7-2 is the weakest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Facing a 3x raise from Minni on the button, there is no strategic reason to call out of position with such a poor hand. I will preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am on the button (Small Blind) with Jack-Five offsuit. In a heads-up situation, I want to maintain pressure on the shorter stack. While J5o is a relatively weak hand, I have a significant chip lead and have successfully won several pots recently by opening for 3x. I will raise here to try and take the pot pre-flop. If I get called, I will have the advantage of position throughout the rest of the hand.

Flash (Button/SB) is opening frequently to 300 — a wide, aggressive range. In heads-up I can defend fairly wide from the big blind; K♠8♣ (K8o) has decent high-card value and blocks some of his broadway combos. Pot odds are attractive (call 200 into a ~400 pot → finishing pot ~600; ~3:1), so a flat call is the standard, conservative play: defend the blind, see a flop, and then play according to texture. I avoid a small min‑3bet here because K8o is marginal to build a big pot out of position against an aggressive opponent. Calling 200 more (300 total committed preflop) keeps the pot manageable and preserves stack for postflop play.

Flop: 6♥ 9♥ 4♥. Holding K♥8♣ I have king-high and a 4‑to‑a‑flush (9 outs → ~35% to hit by river, ~19% on the turn) plus a useful blocker to many heart combos. Flash is a wide, frequent opener so his continuation range is large — many hands I beat, but his range also includes two‑heart combos that already have a made flush. Out of position with only a draw and one overcard, I want to control the pot and realize my equity rather than build a big pot now. Checking induces his c‑bet and lets me call favorable sizes to see the turn; if he checks back I can proceed based on the turn card (bet hearts/air, pot‑control on blanks). Action: check to preserve fold equity, keep pot manageable, and play the draw on later streets.

On this monotone board (6h 9h 4h), I have no hearts in my hand and only Jack-high. However, as the pre-flop aggressor in position, I can represent a strong hand or a high heart draw. Minni's check allows me to place a continuation bet to test her strength. If she doesn't have a heart or a piece of the board, she is likely to fold. If she calls or raises, I can safely assume I am behind and will look to get away from the hand unless the turn significantly improves my position.

Call 300. Flash is a wide, aggressive button opener and he’s c-betting a small size (300 into the ~600 pre-bet pot), so defending a wide range from the big blind is correct. The pot is 900 and calling 300 gives me ~25% break-even equity (300/(900+300)). I hold K♥8♣ which gives me a 4‑card heart draw with 9 outs (~35% to make a flush by the river, ~19% on the turn) plus decent showdown value with the King — and K♥ blocks some of his stronger heart combos. Raising here would bloat the pot out of position with only a draw and a marginal high card; folding is too tight given the pot odds and his wide range. Plan: call the flop, see the turn — if I hit the flush or pair I’ll extract value; if I miss and face heavy aggression I will fold; if he checks I can take a controlled line on later streets.

Board: 6♥ 9♥ 4♥ Tc. I hold K♥8♣ (king‑high + four‑card heart draw). Flash is a very wide, aggressive button — frequent small c‑bets on the flop and likely to continue barreling. I have 9 hearts to make a flush plus a few pair outs (3 kings, 2 non‑heart eights) — roughly 14 unique outs (~14/46 ≈ 30% raw chance to improve on the river), though some of those improvements (K/8) can still lose to stronger hands. With deep stacks and being out of position, I don’t want to bloat the pot with only a single‑card draw and a marginal high card. Checking controls the pot, keeps his bluffs and weaker holdings in range, and gives me the correct pot odds to call a reasonable turn bet. Plan: check here; call small–medium bets to realize equity, fold to large overbets/raises, and if he checks back re-evaluate river (either take a free card or apply selective pressure).

I decided to check on the turn. The Ten of Clubs did not improve my hand, and since I have no hearts in my hand, I have zero equity on this monotone board. Minni showed strength by calling the flop continuation bet, suggesting she has a piece of the board or a flush draw. Continuing to bluff into a range that has already shown interest is risky, especially with a hand that has no backup outs. By checking, I control the pot size and can evaluate the river action.

I improved to top pair on the river (K♠ on board with my K♥8♣). The flop was 6♥9♥4♥ so a made heart flush is possible, but Flash’s line (small flop c-bet then check on the turn) makes two‑heart, locked‑in flushes less likely than a wide c‑bet/bluff or single‑heart hands. With pot = 1,200 I want value from worse hands (A‑highs, Q/J/10/9 pairs, missed floats, etc.) while keeping the pot controlled against the unlikely but possible flush/two‑pair combos. A half‑pot value bet (~600) accomplishes that: it’s big enough to get called by most worse hands and small enough to avoid bloating the pot out of position. If he raises large, I’ll respect it (my top pair with a weak kicker is vulnerable to rivered two‑pair/flush), but as faced with a checked turn and a wide opponent, betting for value is the +EV line.

On the river, the board is 6h 9h 4h 10c Ks. I have Jack-high with no hearts. Minni called my pre-flop raise and my flop continuation bet on a monotone board. This indicates she likely has a piece of the board or a heart draw. Her lead on the river for 600 into an 1200 pot after checking the turn suggests either value (a flush, or a King that just hit) or a bluff. However, since I have no showdown value whatsoever—losing to almost any pair, Ace-high, and even Queen-high—I cannot call. I also lack a heart in my hand to use as a credible bluff-blocker if I were to raise. Therefore, the most prudent action is to fold and move to the next hand.

Heads-up on the button with T5o — this is a hand I want to play aggressively preflop rather than limp-call. Flash defends the big blind widely and the table's standard open size is around 300, so I’ll open to 300 (about 3x the BB) to take the initiative, gain fold equity, and deny him good pot odds to see a flop. If he folds I pick up the 150 pot; if he calls I keep positional advantage and will c-bet on favorable textures but play cautiously with T5o (fold to a sizeable 3‑bet). With deep stacks I avoid bloating the pot with a marginal hand but still want to apply pressure from the button.

In a heads-up situation, 7-6 offsuit is a standard defending hand from the big blind against a 3x raise. I am getting 2-to-1 odds to call and see a flop. Given my significant chip lead and the stage of the game, I can afford to play this speculative hand out of position, though I will need to be cautious post-flop.

I have middle pair (7s) on a board with a King. This is a solid hand to check-call with in a heads-up pot to keep the pot small and potentially catch a bluff or see a cheap turn. Leading out doesn't accomplish much as I'll likely only be called by better hands or draws.

I was the preflop aggressor and have position; the flop K♦-7♠-2♠ is fairly dry and often misses Flash’s wide defending range. My hand T♠5♣ has no pair and only a very weak backdoor possibility, so I don’t want to inflate the pot but I do want to use my positional initiative and fold out unpaired hands and small pocket pairs. I’ll make a small continuation bet (~1/3 pot) to maximize fold equity while keeping the pot controllable. Plan: bet 200; fold to a meaningful check‑raise; if he simply calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and proceed cautiously unless I improve or get a cheap showdown.

I have middle pair (sevens) on a board with a King. Minni has made a small continuation bet of 200 into a 600 pot (1/3 pot size). Given the 4-to-1 pot odds and the fact that middle pair is a strong enough hand to defend against a wide range of continuation bets in a heads-up pot, calling is the most appropriate action. Raising would likely only get action from better hands, and folding is too weak given my pair. I will call and see how the turn develops.

I have middle pair (sevens) on a board that didn't change much with the turn 3. In heads-up play, this hand has significant showdown value but is not strong enough to bet for value. Checking is the standard play to control the pot and allow Minni to continue her aggression with a wide range of hands, including bluffs and draws. I will check and likely call a reasonable bet.

I opened preflop and c-bet the dry K♦-7♠-2♠ flop and got called. The 3♣ on the turn is a blank for my T♠5♣: backdoor spade is gone and I only have 6 outs (three tens, three fives) to pair on the river (~12–14% equity). Flash’s flop call and turn check are consistent with Kx, small/mid pockets, missed spade draws or a pot‑control line — and the turn check also keeps the option to pot‑control with a real hand or trap with a set. With ~SPR ≈10 and deep stacks I don’t want to bloat the pot with a thin double‑barrel bluff that can easily be called or raised and put me in tough spots. I’ll control the pot and realize my equity by checking back the turn. Plan: if I pair the river I’ll value-bet; if the river is blank I’ll evaluate a small river bluff only if the card and opponent’s tendency justify it, otherwise fold to significant aggression.

On the river, the board is Kd 7s 2s 3c 4h and I hold second pair with a 7. Minni checked back on the turn, which often indicates she either has a marginal hand (like a weak 7 or an Ace-high) or a missed draw she is giving up on. My hand has strong showdown value in a heads-up pot, but betting is unlikely to get called by many worse hands. By checking, I allow her to bluff with missed draws or simply take the hand to showdown. Given my significant chip lead and the board texture, checking is the most prudent way to realize my equity and minimize risk.

Preflop I opened to take the initiative with T♠5♣ and followed through with a small c‑bet on the dry K♦‑7♠‑2♠ flop. Flash’s call on the flop and then check on the turn are consistent with Kx, small/mid pocket pairs, or a passive defense with missed/backdoor holdings. The turn 3♣ was a blank for my hand (I had ~12–14% to improve on the river) and the river 4♥ gives me nothing — no pair, no straight, no flush. Pot is only ~1,000 and Flash has shown a tendency to call down in similar spots; a river bluff here would be thin and likely called too often for a positive EV. I prefer not to bloat the pot with a totally missed hand and will take the safe line: check and show down if Flash checks, but fold to meaningful river aggression.

I am on the button with a strong starting hand, Ace-Five suited. This is an easy opening raise in a heads-up situation to build the pot and exert pressure on the big blind. I'll stick to my standard sizing of 300.

I’m in the big blind with 8♣2♠ facing a 3x SB open to 300 (pot 400, I must call 200). 82o is one of the worst hands in heads‑up play: very poor equity and playability, and I’ll be out of position postflop. Calling 200 into a 400 pot requires ~33% equity to break even — 8‑2o doesn’t come close vs a reasonable opening range. A light 3‑bet bluff could be considered vs an extremely passive opener, but Flash shows frequent aggression and large stacks make a marginal bluff unnecessary. Fold and preserve stack for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button (SB) with Qc5c — a suited queen is a perfectly fine hand to play aggressively heads-up. There's only 50 more to call into a 150 pot, but raising to 200 (the min-raise) grabs the initiative, folds out some of BB's weaker holdings, and lets me play the flop in position with decent flush and Q-high equity. I won't get overly committed with this hand: if Flash 3-bets large I'll fold; if he calls I can c-bet favorable textures or realize equity with backdoor/flush potential. With deep stacks and only a small extra risk, the +EV play is to raise and pressure the big blind.

I am in the Big Blind with 9h 4h. Minni, on the Button, has raised to 200 (a min-raise). I only need to call 100 more to see the flop. With pot odds of 3:1 (calling 100 to win a total pot that will be 400), and considering that 94s has decent playability in a heads-up pot, I have enough equity and the right price to defend my blind against a small raise.

I have flopped two pair (Nines and Fours) on an Ace-high board. In heads-up play, the Big Blind acts first post-flop. While my hand is very strong, checking to the pre-flop aggressor is a standard play to see if they will continuation bet with their range, which includes many Aces that I currently beat. This allows me to either check-call or check-raise depending on the sizing and my read on Minni's aggression.

I was the preflop raiser and I’m in position. The flop A♣ 9♠ 4♦ checked to me — my Q♣5♣ has almost no showdown value (only runner-runner club backdoors) but I have a range advantage as the raiser. Against a checked flop heads‑up a standard c-bet will fold out lots of Kx/Qx and small pairs while getting called by Ax/strong pairs; I want to seize the initiative without bloating the pot with a weak hand. I’ll make a half‑pot continuation bet (200 into 400). Plan: if Flash raises I’ll assume strength (likely an Ace or big pair) and fold; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and proceed cautiously.

I have flopped two pair (Nines and Fours) on a dry Ace-high board. While the Ace is a scare card for most of my range, it is also a card that Minni is likely to continuation bet with frequently. By check-raising here, I am targeting her range of one-pair Aces (like AJ, AT, etc.) which she is unlikely to fold, as well as protecting my hand against potential runner-runner draws. Raising to 600 builds the pot and defines the hand strength early.

Flash check‑raised my 200 c‑bet to 600 on A♣‑9♠‑4♦. The pot is ~1,200 and it’s 400 to call (min reraise to 1,000). I c‑bet this board with a marginal hand (Qc5c) to take it down, but a heads‑up check‑raise here is very value‑heavy (Ax, 9x/4x, sets) and only occasionally a bluff. My hand has almost no showdown value — only runner‑runner club backdoors and a ~24% chance to pair by the river, much of which still loses to Ax — so my realistic equity vs his polarized check‑raise is below the ~20% needed to justify a call. Reraising as a bluff would be costly and unnecessary; preserving stack and waiting for a better spot is +EV. Fold.

I have Ace-Eight offsuit on the button. In a heads-up situation, this is a premium hand that I should almost always be raising. Minni has been folding to many of my button raises recently, so a standard 3x raise is the most effective play here to either take the pot immediately or build a pot where I likely have an equity advantage.

Flash (SB/Button) opened to 300 heads-up. I have K♦2♣ in the big blind. In heads-up I need to defend wide — K2o is marginal but acceptable to call rather than folding. Pot odds: calling 200 into a 400 pot means I need ~33% equity (200/(400+200)), and versus a wide button opening range I expect at least that. I don't want to 3-bet with K2o since it will often be dominated by better Kx and would bloat the pot against a deep, aggressive opponent. Plan: call to see the flop, value or raise if I hit a king, pursue backdoor draws selectively, and exercise pot control / fold to heavy aggression on unfavourable boards.

I defended K♦2♣ preflop heads-up intending to keep a wide calling range. The flop 8c-4h-6h is fairly dry and I missed — I only have K-high and no heart backdoors. With deep stacks and a small 600 pot I want to keep the pot under control and let the opener take the initiative. Checking preserves my options: I can call a small c-bet to see a turn, fold to heavy aggression, and only invest more if I pair or the action strongly favors a bluff. Check now.

I have hit top pair with the best possible kicker (Ace). The board is somewhat coordinated with potential straight and flush draws (8-6-4 texture with two hearts). Since Minni checked to me, I will place a standard continuation bet of half the pot to get value from weaker hands and draws, while also protecting my equity.

Flash opened from the button and then fired a small flop c-bet (300 into a 900 pot). I checked to keep the pot small and to realize equity. My hand K♦2♣ has very little immediate equity — no hearts/backdoor flush and only three kings left (~12% to pair by river). Calling 300 into the 900 pot (pot will be 1200 after the bet) requires ~25% equity purely to break even, so I’m not calling because I’m a strong drawing hand — I’m calling to defend a wide heads-up range, keep the pot manageable, and see a turn where I can fold to real aggression or extract value if I pair. Plan: call the 300 to see the turn, fold to a significant raise, and if I pair a king or the button checks I will proceed for value or a delayed bluff.

Turn gave me K♠, so I have top pair (K♦2♣) with a very weak 2-kicker on 8♣-4♥-6♥-K♠. Pot is 1,200 and Flash checked to me — his line (open pre, small c‑bet on the flop) represents a wide range: missed overcards, small pairs, and a fair number of heart floats/draws. The board has two hearts, so my primary goals are to charge and deny equity to heart draws and to extract value from worse pairs (8x/6x/4x) and some worse Ks. Betting also prevents him from realizing free equity on the river when he has a draw.

I’ll bet a standard protection/value size: half‑pot (600). This charges the draws and gets called by many worse hands while keeping the pot manageable with a weak kicker. If Flash check‑raises large I will strongly favor folding (I don’t want to get committed with top pair and a bad kicker). If he just calls, I’ll re‑evaluate on the river and size for thin value or pot‑control depending on the card.

I currently hold second pair with an Ace kicker on a board where a King just appeared. Minni's decision to lead out (donk bet) on the turn is interesting; it often signifies either a King, a draw, or a bluff leveraging the overcard. Given the 3:1 pot odds and my hand's showdown value, a call is the most prudent play. It allows me to maintain pot control and evaluate the river while keeping Minni's potential bluffs in the hand. Raising would likely only be called by better hands (Kings or better) and fold out hands I am currently beating.

I have top pair (K♦2♣) on a relatively dry river (8♣-4♥-6♥-K♠-3♠). I bet the turn for protection/value (600) and Flash called, so his range still contains lots of worse pairs (8x/6x/4x), Kx with better or worse kickers, and missed heart floats. The river 3♠ is a blank — it doesn’t complete hearts or give obvious two‑pair/straights — so this is a spot to extract thin value. Betting a meaningful but not bloating size (~1/2 pot) will get called by worse pairs and many Kx’s while folding pure missed draws. If Flash check‑raises big I will strongly lean to folding given my weak kicker; if he just calls I go to showdown.
