Bet half‑pot to balance value and protection while keeping risk reasonable relative to my stack and his calling tendencies.

On a board of 8c-4h-6h-Ks-3s, I hold second pair (eights) with an Ace kicker. Minni's line of check-calling the flop and then leading (donk betting) on both the turn (when the King appeared) and the river is polar. She is either representing a King or better, or she is leveraging the scare card to bluff with missed draws like the flush (two hearts on the flop) or straight draws (9-7, 5-7). Note that 5-7 would have made a straight on the turn, but most other draws missed. Given the 3:1 pot odds and the fact that I don't block any of the missed heart draws, this is a reasonable spot to call and keep her bluffs in her range. I have noticed a pattern where Minni leads out on later streets to push me off marginal hands, so I will call to see if my pair of eights is sufficient.

Heads-up from the button (SB) Q4o is marginal, but against a wide BB range I want to take the initiative. I’ll open-raise to a standard 3x-style size (300) to gain fold equity and keep pot control. If Flash 3-bets big (e.g. ~1000+) I will fold this hand; if he just calls I’ll c-bet on most dry, favorable flops and proceed cautiously on coordinated/paired boards to avoid bloated pots with a weak kicker. Raising to 300 also matches table sizing and reduces frequency of facing large 3-bets.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Deuce offsuit. Minni has raised from the Button to 300. With a pot of 400 and only 200 more to call, I am getting 2:1 odds to see a flop. While A2o is a relatively weak hand, it is strong enough to defend the big blind against a standard button open in a heads-up situation. I will call and evaluate based on the flop.

I have flopped two pair (Aces and Deuces) on a board of Ac-9c-2h. This is a very strong hand in a heads-up pot. Since Minni was the pre-flop aggressor, I will check to her to allow her the opportunity to continuation-bet with her wide range, including weaker Aces, flush draws, or air. If she bets, I will likely check-raise to build the pot and get value from those hands.

Heads-up I opened to 300 and Flash just called. Flop 9c Ac 2h is dry but contains an Ace that hits a lot of his calling range. I have Qc4h — no pair but a backdoor club possibility and a valuable club blocker. Standard plan here is a wide continuation bet to take the pot down and deny equity to non‑Ax hands. I’ll make a small ~1/3 pot c‑bet (200 into 600) to apply pressure while keeping the pot manageable versus a deep stack. If he folds I win; if he calls I proceed cautiously on the turn (look to check/control or fold to a large check‑raise unless I improve — if the turn brings a club I can continue more aggressively).

I have flopped two pair (Aces and Deuces). This is a very strong hand in a heads-up pot. Minni has made a small continuation bet of 1/3 pot. I will check-raise here to 700 to build the pot immediately and get value from her range, which includes weaker Aces, flush draws, and occasionally Nines. By raising now, I protect my hand against potential draws and set up a larger pot for future streets where I can potentially play for Minni's entire stack.

Flash check‑raised my small 1/3 pot c‑bet (200) to 700 on 9c Ac 2h. Pot is 1500 and it costs me 500 more to call (i.e. commit 700 on the flop) — I’m getting ~25% pot‑odds (500 into a 2000 final pot). I have Qc4h: a 10‑card club draw (≈38% to hit a flush by the river) plus three queens to pair (≈12.5%); raw chance to hit a club or pair by river is ~48%, but versus a value‑heavy check‑raise range (lots of Ax and sets) my realistic equity is lower (roughly 30–35%). That still clears the ~25% threshold, so a call is +EV here. Raising as a bluff is too risky with only a draw and would bloat the pot against many made hands; folding surrenders fold equity and immediate equity. I'll call 500 more now to see the turn, plan to fold to a large turn barrel if I don’t improve, and if I hit a club (or a strong river) I’ll shift to value/aggression.

I have two pair (Aces and Deuces) on a board that remains relatively safe after the turn 3d. Minni called my flop check-raise, which suggests she likely has an Ace with a medium kicker or a flush draw. I need to continue betting for value and to charge any draws. A bet of 1400 into the 2000 pot (70% pot) is a strong lead that targets her range of weaker Aces (AJ, AT, A8) and protects against the club flush draw.

I called the flop with Qc4h hoping the backdoor club could come, but the turn (3♦) did not improve me and it also kills the backdoor flush (I needed both turn+river clubs). The pot is 3,400 and Flash bet 1,400; calling 1,400 to win 4,800 requires ~29.2% equity. My realistic outs are three queens and three fours (≈6 outs → ~13% to hit by the river), and that’s before considering that many of Flash’s check‑raise/turn‑barrel hands (A‑x, sets, two‑pair) make my outs often dead or second‑best. Raising as a bluff here is too risky and bloat the pot with very little fold equity. Per my plan to fold to a large turn barrel if I didn’t improve, this is an easy fold — preserve the 11,575 stack and wait for a better spot.

I am in the small blind with Kd 5s. In heads-up play, any King is typically strong enough to open for a raise to put pressure on the big blind and take control of the pot. I will use my standard opening size of 300 chips.

Facing a Button (SB) open to 300 heads-up. I’m in the big blind with 5♣4♥. Pot is 400 and I need to call 200, giving ~25% immediate pot odds (need ~25% equity). Against a wide HU opening range this hand has adequate equity and some playability (connectivity and backdoor straight/flush potential), and stacks are deep so implied odds help. I won’t 3‑bet — the hand has poor blockers and I’ll be OOP postflop, so a 3‑bet would be a weak bluff. Plan: call, check and evaluate the flop, continue with any pair/2‑pair/strong draw or well-priced equity, fold to large aggression on dry boards. Folding is too tight given the price. Calling is the best long‑term EV play here.

I check. I have a marginal but playable hand: middle pair (4s) with a 5 kicker plus a 4‑out gutshot to a 7 (turn or river makes a straight) and only a double backdoor heart flush (needing both turn+river hearts). Versus a wide HU SB opening range this pair has decent showdown value and some fold equity against pure overcards. I’m out of position so I prefer pot control and to let the opener take the initiative. Plan: check and call a standard c‑bet (small → ~1/3–1/2 pot) to realize equity and re‑evaluate on the turn; fold to very large bets/overbets or a sizable check‑raise since the hand is vulnerable and stacks are deep (opponent can apply large pressure). Betting myself would bloat the pot OOP with a marginal holding, so checking is the lowest‑variance, +EV line here.

I have King-high on a board (8-4-6) that is likely to have connected with the Big Blind's defending range. Since the board is somewhat coordinated with straight and flush possibilities, checking back allows me to control the pot size and potentially see a free turn card to improve my hand or reassess.

Check. The turn (8♦) paired the board and eliminated my double backdoor heart draw; I’m left with a weak single pair (4s) and very limited improvement potential. Flash checked the flop, which indicates a wide/missed range or pot control, so betting OOP now would bloat the pot with a marginal hand and expose me to check‑raises or heavy value from any 8x/6x. I’ll control the pot size, realize showdown equity, and plan to call a small turn bet but fold to large overbets or a check‑raise that commits a big portion of my stack.

On a board of 8h-4d-6h-8d, I have King-high. Minni has checked twice (flop and turn), which indicates weakness or a marginal hand. While I could bet to try and fold out small pairs or better high cards, checking back on the turn allows me to control the pot size and realize my showdown value with King-high. Betting here might induce a check-raise from a slow-played 8 or a draw, which I want to avoid. I will check and look to reach showdown or evaluate on the river.

Both streets were checked and I only have a weak pair (4s) with a poor kicker. The opponent checked flop/turn/river — their line is consistent with missed overcards, medium pair or pot control; it’s also possible they have a J or a 6 which beat me. Betting here would mainly fold out air and get called by hands that dominate me, so a river bluff has marginal fold equity and risks a costly call from better hands. With a 600 pot and deep stacks I prefer the low-variance line: check to showdown and realize my equity rather than bloating the pot with a marginal hand. Check.

I have King-high on a board that has paired with eights and contains a Jack. Minni has checked on the flop, turn, and river, which strongly suggests she has a weak hand or a marginal one that she's looking to take to showdown cheaply. While my King-high might lose to small pairs like fours or sixes, it beats all lower high-card hands and missed draws. Since there is little value in betting (as I'm unlikely to fold out better hands or get called by worse), checking back to realize my showdown value is the best move.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) I should be opening a very wide range — 9♣6♠ is marginal but perfectly playable in position. Raising gains the initiative, puts pressure on the big blind and lets me play the pot multi-street with positional advantage. The pot is only 150 and a raise to the minimum 200 is a small, low-risk investment relative to my 11.8k stack. Plan: if Flash folds I take the pot; if he calls I use position to c-bet good textures and control the pot on dry ace/king-high boards. If he 3-bets big I’ll fold to avoid playing dominated hands out of position; I may call small 3-bets depending on sizing and implied odds.

I am in the Big Blind holding Jack-Trey of diamonds. Minni, on the Button/Small Blind, has made a minimum raise to 200. I need to call 100 more into a pot of 300, giving me 4:1 pot odds. Suited hands, even with a weak kicker like a 3, have enough playability and equity to defend against a small raise in a heads-up situation. Calling is the standard play here to see a flop.

I have flopped two pair (Jacks and Treys) on a coordinated board containing a flush draw and several straight possibilities. In this heads-up situation, being out of position, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. If Minni bets, I intend to check-raise to extract value from her range and protect my hand against the various draws present on the board.

I was the preflop aggressor in heads-up, so a continuation bet is standard. The flop Tc Js 3c is relatively dry and Flash’s check looks like weakness or a marginal holding. My 9c6s has no pair and only a very weak backdoor club possibility, so I have poor showdown equity but decent fold equity as the raiser. I’ll make a half-pot c-bet (200 into 400) to take the initiative, fold out weaker hands, and keep the pot manageable. If Flash raises big I’ll fold; if called I’ll re-evaluate the turn and only continue barreling on club or other improving cards that fit my story.

I have flopped two pair (Jacks and Treys) on a board with several draws (flush and straight). Minni has made a continuation bet of 200 into a pot of 400. This is a strong hand in a heads-up pot, but it is vulnerable to many turn cards. I will check-raise here to extract value from her range (like top pair, gutshots, or flush draws) and to protect my hand by making it expensive for her to see the next card. A raise to 700 is a standard size that forces her to pay to continue.

I was the preflop aggressor and made a standard 200 c-bet on Tc Js 3c. Flash’s check‑raise to 700 is large and polarized — it often represents a strong made hand (top pair, two pair, sets) or a very strong two‑club combo draw. I hold 9♣6♠: no pair, no real straight draw and only a tiny backdoor club-runner (~4% to make a flush on both turn+river). Calling 500 into a 1300 pot requires ~28% equity; my hand is well below that against a check‑raise range. Re‑raising as a bluff is too high risk given effective stacks and the line; folding preserves my stack for better heads‑up spots. I fold.

In a heads-up situation, I have a significant stack lead and am on the button. While 6-2 suited is a weak starting hand, it has some playability and I want to maintain an aggressive opening strategy from the small blind to exert pressure. Raising to 300 is my standard opening size.

Heads-up vs a button open to 300 with Q♥4♦ in the big blind. Pot is 400 and I need to call 200 more (my total commitment would be 300), so I’m getting ~33% equity (200/600). The button’s opening range heads-up is very wide, so Q4o has reasonable equity and some blocker value to Qx. 3-betting here would be a thin, non-blocker bluff out of position against an aggressive opponent with deep stacks — not worth the risk. I’ll defend the blind and see a flop, planning to fold to heavy turn/river aggression if I miss and to continue only when I pair the queen/4 or pick up legitimate draws. Action: call to 300.

Check. I have Q♥4♦ on 2♥-5♠-3♠: no pair, no spade, but the 4 gives me an open-ended-ish straight draw (A or 6 complete — ~8 outs, ~31% to hit by river). I’m out of position vs the preflop raiser and they checked to me, which often indicates weakness or a pot-control line. Betting OOP with just a draw is thin — it risks a check-raise and bloats the pot when I don’t yet have showdown or nut-equity. I’ll control the pot and realize equity by checking; plan is to call reasonable turn bets to see my outs, fold to heavy aggression if I miss, and become aggressive only when I pair the Q or complete the straight.

On the flop of 2h 5s 3s, I have bottom pair (Deuces) and a flush draw (Six-high). This is a strong semi-bluffing candidate in a heads-up pot. By betting, I can either win the pot immediately if Minni has missed the board entirely or build a larger pot for when I improve to a flush or two pair/trips. I will bet 400, which is roughly two-thirds of the pot, to exert pressure.

I defended the big blind with Q♣4♦ preflop and checked the flop to realize equity. Board 2♥-5♠-3♠ gives me an 8‑out straight draw (A or 6) and 3 extra queen outs (queens are a bit vulnerable vs stronger hands). Flash’s c‑bet is small (400 into a 1000 pot) — calling 400 gets me ~22% pot‑odds (400 to win ~1800). My raw chance to improve by the river is ~31% with straight outs alone and up to ~42% if you count queens, so I have sufficient equity to continue. Raising OOP with a marginal draw is thin and risks bloating the pot against a deeper, aggressive opponent, so the +EV line is to call, see the turn, and fold to heavy turn aggression if I miss. If I pair the Q or complete the straight I’ll start playing for value.

Board is 2♥-5♠-3♠-9♣, I hold Q♥4♦. I defended the blind and called the flop; on the turn I have 8 clean straight outs (all Aces and Sixes) to hit a 5-card straight on the river (8/46 ≈ 17.4%), and 3 queens that can pair me (bringing raw improvement to ≈24%), but queens are vulnerable and often still behind. Pot is 1,400 and stacks are deep (I have 10,775, Villain 27,825). There is no bet to face right now, so betting/leading OOP with a marginal draw is thin — it risks a check‑raise and bloats the pot when I’m usually behind. Checking controls the pot and realizes equity: if Villain checks I see a free river; if he bets I’ll call small bets that give correct pot odds (~≤350) to chase and fold to large aggression, and only turn aggressive if I pair or complete the straight. Preferred +EV line is to check and see River.

On the turn 9c, the board is 2h-5s-3s-9c. I currently hold bottom pair (Deuces) with a flush draw (Six-high) and a gutshot straight draw (needing a four). Minni has checked to me after calling my flop bet. While I could continue betting as a semi-bluff, checking here allows me to realize my significant equity (14 outs to improve) for free. By checking, I avoid the risk of a check-raise that might force me off my draw, and I can still win the pot on the river if I hit my flush, straight, or trips, or potentially win at showdown with my pair of deuces if the river goes check-check.

The river A♣ completes the A-2-3-4-5 wheel using my 4♦ — I have a made straight. Heads‑up versus a wide button-opening range that c‑bet the flop small (400) and then checked the turn, Flash is likely to have a lot of single‑pair hands (Ax, 9x), weak pairs or missed draws that will call a value bet on the river. Very few hands beat me here — mainly rare 4+6 combinations that make 2‑3‑4‑5‑6; A4 hands tie. Those are uncommon in a standard button open-raising range. With the pot at 1,400 I want to extract value from worse (A‑x, 9‑x, pocket pairs, some river bluffs) without scaring them off, so I’ll bet a medium size. Betting ~50% pot (700) is large enough to get value from most of Flash’s calling hands while not overbloating the pot against the very unlikely combo hands that beat me. If he raises big I’ll re-evaluate (a large river raise is polarized and likely beats me), but the +EV line now is a value bet.

On the river Ace of clubs, the board is 2h-5s-3s-9c-Ac. I currently hold a pair of deuces, which is the bottom pair on this board. My flush draw and straight draw both missed. Minni has led out for 700 into a 1400 pot after I checked back the turn. While the Ace is a classic scare card that she could be using to bluff, it is also very much in her range to have an Ace or a better pair (3s, 5s, or 9s) given the pre-flop and flop action. Calling with bottom pair against a half-pot lead on a board with three overcards and potential straights is a low-probability play. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with KcQc is a strong opening hand. I want to take the initiative and use my postflop positional advantage, so I should raise rather than just complete. The min-raise to 200 is a bit small and gives more room for a 3-bet; a standard 3xBB open to 300 pressures weaker broadways and pocket pairs while still leaving plenty of stack behind to play postflop. Stack sizes (my 12,125 vs Flash 27,725) mean I don't need to commit now — just build value and fold equity. Raise to 300 total.

I am in the Big Blind with 5h 3h. Minni raised to 300 from the Small Blind. I need to call 200 more to see a flop in a pot of 400, giving me 2:1 pot odds. Suited connectors/gappers like 53s are playable in a heads-up situation against a wide button-opening range. I have a substantial stack lead and the hand has potential for straights or flushes, so I will call to see the flop.

On this flop of 9s-8h-Qh, my hand (5h-3h) has very little equity. I have no pair, no immediate straight draw, and only a backdoor heart flush draw. As the Big Blind defending against a pre-flop raise from the Small Blind, the standard play is to check to the aggressor and see how they react. I do not have a strong enough hand or draw to lead out into the pot.

Flop: Qh 9s 8h. I hold KcQc — top pair with a good kicker in position heads‑up. Flash checked after calling preflop, so this is a clear c‑bet spot to get value from worse Qx, medium pairs (9x/8x) and Jx, and to deny free cards to hands that can outdraw me (main concern is rare JT straights which already beat me). There are only two hearts on board (no made flush draw), so protection needs are moderate. A half‑pot bet keeps the pot controlled vs a deeper stack, extracts thin value, and retains fold equity against floats. Plan: bet half the pot (300 into 600); if called re‑evaluate on the turn and continue extracting or pot control as appropriate; fold or severely tighten range if I face a sizable check‑raise (that typically indicates a very strong hand here).

On a flop of 9s-8h-Qh, my hand (5h 3h) has virtually no equity. I have no pair and no immediate straight draw. While I have a backdoor flush draw, it is not strong enough to justify calling a half-pot bet (300 into 600). The board texture heavily favors a standard opening range, and continuing with 5-high here is a losing play in the long run.

I am on the button with 8h 6c. In a heads-up situation, 86o is a marginal but playable hand. The pot is currently 150, and it costs me 50 more to call from the small blind. Given the deep stacks and the need to maintain a balanced range, I will raise to 300 to put pressure on the Big Blind and take control of the hand pre-flop, as I have been doing frequently in previous hands.

Flash (button/SB) opened to 300 — in heads-up that range is wide. 9h8s is a playable, connected hand with decent equity and postflop playability vs a wide opening. Calling 200 more (total commit 300) gives ~33% required equity and keeps the pot from bloating with a marginal 3-bet; stacks are deep enough to realize implied odds but I’m out of position so I don’t want to build a big pot unnecessarily. Plan: call, see the flop, continue aggressively on favorable connected/drawy boards and fold or pot-control against heavy resistance on dry, adverse boards.

Flop is 3c-3d-5s — a very dry, paired board. My 9h8s has no pair and virtually no realistic draws (only remote two-card runner straights), so I have very little equity. Flash opened wide pre and will c-bet this texture a high percentage; I don’t want to build a pot out of position with air. Check to control the pot and fold to normal continuation bets. If Flash checks back I can reassess and potentially bluff on a favorable turn, but default line is to pot-control and let this one go unless the street changes.

The flop is 3c-3d-5s, a very dry, paired board that generally favors the pre-flop aggressor. Since I raised pre-flop, I can represent a wide range of hands including overpairs and high-card combinations. My current hand (8h 6c) has missed the flop, but a continuation bet is standard here to put pressure on the Big Blind's range, which consists mostly of unpaired high cards that will likely fold to a bet. I'll bet half the pot to keep the price reasonable while maximizing fold equity.

The flop (3c-3d-5s) is extremely dry and paired. My 9♥8♠ has no pair and basically no realistic draws — only remote two-card runner possibilities — so my equity is near zero. Flash opened wide preflop and will continuation-bet this texture at a high frequency, so calling 300 requires ~25% equity which I don’t have. Out of position I don’t want to inflate the pot with air, and a bluff-raise OOP against a wide opener’s c-bet/raise range is thin and high variance. Preserve stack for better spots and fold.

Heads-up on the button (I have position) with 9c4h — a weak holding, but in heads-up play my open-raising range should be very wide. Folding every marginal hand here surrenders initiative and makes me exploitable. Calling 50 to see a flop is cheap, but raising gets fold equity and lets me play the hand on my terms. I’ll open to a standard 2xBB (200 total). If Flash 3-bets big, I’ll fold this hand — it’s not worth getting committed out of position to a strong 3-bet. If called, I’ll use position to c-bet favorable textures or check/call small flops and avoid bloating the pot without improvement.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Trey offsuit. Minni has opened for a small 2x raise to 200. While the pot odds are 3:1 to call, this hand is extremely weak and plays poorly out of position. Given my significant stack lead, I prefer to fold these bottom-tier hands and wait for better opportunities to exploit my advantage rather than risking chips with a marginalized hand.

I am holding 82o, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands in Texas Hold'em. While I have the chip lead and the advantage of position post-flop as the Button/SB, 82o has very poor playability and realization of equity. Minni has been calling or raising frequently enough that limping or raising with junk like this is likely to lead to a lost pot. I will choose the disciplined route and fold this hand.

Heads-up on the button with T8 suited — a strong, playable hand with position and good postflop playability. The pot is 150 and it’s only 50 to complete, but I want to take the initiative and avoid playing passively out of the small blind. A standard 3x BB open to 300 matches the table tendencies (many players open to ~300) and gives good fold equity against the BB’s wide calling range while still leaving plenty of stack behind to maneuver postflop. If Flash 3-bets I can comfortably call or fold depending on sizing and plan to play the flop in position; if called I’ll c-bet favorable textures and leverage position. Raise to 300 to build the pot and capitalize on positional advantage.

I am in the Big Blind holding Ace-Deuce offsuit. The Small Blind (Minni) has raised to 300. Given the pot odds and the fact that any Ace has decent equity in a heads-up pot, I will call to see a flop. While Minni has been aggressive, my hand is strong enough to defend the blind.

I am first to act on a paired flop of 4s-9s-4c. I hold Ace-high (Ah-2c), which has some showdown value in a heads-up pot but is vulnerable. Checking is the standard play here to see how Minni, the pre-flop aggressor, reacts to this board texture. Depending on her bet sizing, I can decide whether to continue.

I was the preflop aggressor and have position on a dry, paired flop 4s-9s-4c. My T8h has no made hand but has two overcards to the 9 and reasonable blocker dynamics vs a wide BB calling range. Against a wide check/call range this is a good spot to take the initiative and apply fold equity; a half‑pot continuation bet pressures unpaired overcards, small pocket pairs, and single‑spade hands while still leaving plenty of room to navigate post‑flop. Plan: bet ~1/2 pot (300). If Flash folds I take the pot; if he calls I will proceed cautiously on turn (check back blanks, continue with equity or strong turn cards). If he check‑raises large on the flop I will assume a very strong holding and fold.

On a paired board of 4s-9s-4c, I have Ace-high. Minni's 300 bet into a 600 pot (half pot) is a standard continuation bet. My Ace-high has significant showdown value against her wide opening range from the Small Blind, which likely includes many unpaired hands and air. Folding here would be too tight given the pot odds (3:1) and the likelihood that she is continuation betting with many hands I currently beat or have decent equity against. I will call to see how the turn develops.

The turn card is the Queen of Spades, which completes potential spade flushes and adds a higher card to the board. I am still holding Ace-high (Ah-2c) on a board of 4s-9s-4c-Qs. Since Minni checked behind on the turn (or it is my turn to act and she has not bet), checking is the most appropriate action. I want to get to showdown as cheaply as possible with my Ace-high, and checking allows me to control the pot size while giving Minni the opportunity to check behind or bluff with a hand I might beat.

Board: 4♠ 9♠ 4♣ Q♠. I hold T♥8♥. Pot 1,200. I was the preflop aggressor and c‑bet the flop; Flash called. The Q♠ on the turn completed a three‑spade board, which significantly improves Flash’s range — many of his check‑call flop hands now have a made spade flush or at least a very strong Qx/9x/4x. My hand has no pair and only thin river outs (three tens + three eights ≈ 12% to pair on the river), so my showdown equity is low against a realistic calling range. Betting the turn would be a pure bluff that’s likely to get called or check‑raised by better hands (and if he has the flush I’m drawing dead). With deep stacks a bet here commits me to difficult decisions and has poor fold equity versus a wide, calling BB. I’ll check to control the pot, avoid getting raised off a bluff, and re‑evaluate on the river: if Flash checks a blank I’ll often check back and take the free card; if he bets big I’ll fold; if the river gives me a cleaner pair (T or 8) I can consider a value/call or a small river bet depending on sizing.

I have Ace-high on a board that has paired fours and includes a nine and a queen. Minni checked the turn after I called her flop bet, which suggests she might be looking to take her hand to showdown or has given up on a bluff. My Ace-high has significant showdown value against her range of missed draws (like JT or KJ) and weaker high cards. Betting would unlikely result in a better hand folding or a worse hand calling. Therefore, checking to reach showdown for free is the optimal play.

Board: 4♠ 9♠ 4♣ Q♠ 6♦. I hold T♥8♥. I was the preflop/ flop aggressor (raised pre, c-bet flop) and Flash called. The turn Q♠ made a three‑spade board, which only gives an actual flush to hands holding two spades, but it meaningfully improves Flash’s range (Qx, 9x, 4x, or two‑spade combos). I have no pair and no spade blocker to credibly represent the flush or a protected Q. My showdown equity is essentially zero, and a river bluff needs a fairly high fold frequency to be +EV (e.g. a 300 bet requires ≈20% folds). Given Flash’s tendencies in earlier hands (sticky, lots of check‑calls and light calls), his fold frequency here is likely low. I prefer pot control and avoiding a costly bluff against a calling station with a big stack. Check to keep the pot small and preserve stack for better spots; if Flash leads out big I will fold.
I have Ten-Nine suited on the button, which is a strong starting hand in heads-up play. I will put in a standard raise to 300 to build the pot and take control of the hand with position.

Heads-up vs the button (Flash) who opened to 300. I hold J♥7♠ in the big blind. Pot is 400 and it’s 200 to me to call (i.e. I’d have 300 in the pot total). Flash has been opening wide from the button in this game, so his range is relatively broad — J7o has reasonable equity vs a wide opening range. Calling here gets good pot odds (200 into 400 → 2:1, need ~33% equity) and keeps the pot under control. I’m out of position postflop (the button acts last after the flop), so I don’t want to 3-bet light to a tiny sizing (to 500) and play a bloated pot OOP with a marginal hand. Plan: call to see a flop, proceed aggressively when I hit a pair or a strong draw, otherwise control the pot and fold to heavy pressure. Therefore I will call the raise.

Flop: Kc 4h Ah. I hold J♥7♠ — no pair, only J-high and a very slim backdoor heart possibility (need both turn+river hearts). I'm out of position and Flash (button) opened wide preflop but checked the flop, so checking for pot control is the best line. Betting OOP with a bare Jack is speculative: it may fold some air but will be called/raised by many Kx/Ax and broadway hands that dominate me. Plan: check, see the turn, and only continue if I pair or pick up a real draw (or face a very small bet I can call). Otherwise fold to meaningful pressure.

I have a backdoor flush draw on a board that heavily favors my pre-flop raising range, as it contains both an Ace and a King. Since Minni checked to me, I will place a standard continuation bet of 300 to represent a strong hand and pressure her to fold her air and weak holdings.

Facing a 300 bet into a 600-pot (900 current) on Kc 4h Ah with Jh7s in the big blind, out of position. Calling 300 to win 1200 requires ~25% equity. I have no pair and only a weak backdoor heart possibility (one heart in hand with two hearts on board is a backdoor flush — ~4.2% to complete by river) and ~24.2% to pair either hole card by the river, but pairing often still loses to Ax/Kx which are well-represented in a button's c-bet range. Flash has been opening wide from the button, but this flop specifically heavily favors A/K and the overcards dominate my hand. Raising as a bluff OOP is risky — committing more chips out of position with very little fold equity and large stacks behind is unfavorable. Given the pot odds and my realistic equity vs a reasonable c-bet range (likely well under the 25% break-even), the +EV play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

Heads-up with 8♠2♣ — a very weak hand. I’m on the button (posted SB) so I’ll keep the pot small and use positional advantage postflop rather than bloating the pot with a marginal holding. Flash has shown a tendency to defend and be sticky, so a min-raise will often be called and put me in tough spots. I’ll just call the 50 more (total commitment 100) to see a cheap flop and fold to significant aggression if I miss.

I am in the big blind with 8h 2s. The action is checked to me as the small blind completed. 82o is a weak hand, but since there is no raise and I am already in the big blind, I will check to see a flop for free. There is no reason to bluff here pre-flop against an opponent who often calls or plays wide from the button.

I have Eight-high on a board of Jack-Seven-Six. I have very little equity here, and since the pot was limped pre-flop and my opponent checked to me, there is no reason to inflate the pot with a weak hand. Checking back allows me to see a free turn card and potentially improve or evaluate my opponent's next move.

Heads-up on the button with 8♠2♣ on J♣7♥6♦ — I completely missed. This is a rainbow, fairly dry board: no pair, no real straight or flush draw. I only have 6 outs to pair by the river (~12.8% to hit on the turn, ~24% by the river). Flash in the BB is a sticky defender who calls small bets a lot, so a c‑bet here is unlikely to fold out enough of his range and will often get called. I continue with pot control and preserve stack — check behind. If Flash bets I will fold to meaningful pressure (only consider calling very small probes if I have a specific read he’s bluffing). If he checks back, I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and look for cheap steal or a river bluff only if the runout and action justify it.

I have Eight-high with no draws on a board of King-Jack-Seven-Six. There was no action pre-flop or on the flop. While the King is a potential bluffing card, there's very little reward in a 200-chip pot to risk chips with a hand that has zero equity. I will check and hope to see a free river or fold if Minni decides to bet.

Heads-up with 8♠2♣ — essentially a bluff-catcher with no real equity. The turn (K♠) didn’t help me: I have no pair and no realistic draw on a fairly dry, rainbow board (J♣ 7♥ 6♦ K♠). Flash has shown a tendency to defend and call small bets, so barreling here is unlikely to fold him often and just bloats the pot out of position. I’ll keep pot control and check behind on the turn to preserve my stack and see a cheap river. If Flash leads on the river I will fold to normal/large sizing and only consider calling very tiny probes if I have a concrete read he’s bluffing. If he checks back I’ll re-evaluate the river and only attempt a bluff on a very favorable runout or give up.

I have 8-high with no showdown value on a board of J-7-6-K-6. The pot was limped and has been checked through every street. While I could attempt a small bluff to take down this tiny 200-chip pot, Minni's checks don't necessarily mean she will fold a better high-card hand or a weak pair in a spot where I have shown no strength. Since the pot is so small and I have absolutely nothing, checking and conceding the pot is the most prudent play.

River: J♣ 7♥ 6♦ K♠ 6♠. I hold 8♠2♣ — no pair, no relevant blockers to K/J/6. Both of us checked earlier; Flash is a sticky, calling opponent who defends and calls small bets often in this game, so a bluff here would need an uncomfortably high fold rate to be +EV. Pot is only 200, so risking chips to shove or sizeable-bet into a caller-heavy opponent is unnecessary. I’ll preserve stack and check back to avoid being called down by worse hands while saving my chips for better, more credible bluff spots.

I am on the button with a pocket pair of deuces. In a heads-up situation, any pocket pair has significant value. My opponent, Minni, has been active but I have a substantial chip lead. Raising here is the standard play to take control of the hand and potentially end it pre-flop, or at least build a pot where I am likely ahead against many of her random big blind hands.

Call to 300. J♠4♠ is marginal but playable heads-up and has useful flush/backdoor equity. Calling the extra 200 into the 400 pot gives ~33% pot odds and, with ~113bb effective, decent implied odds vs a wide button opening range — Flash has been opening frequently. A 3-bet bluff here would be thin and ill-advised OOP, so I’ll see a flop and proceed cautiously: fold to large, non-indicative aggression on very dry boards; continue/raise when I hit a jack, strong pair, or a good flush/straight draw; use position-adjusted aggression only when appropriate.

Flop is 4c Td 4d and I hold Js4s — I have trips (444) with a Jack kicker. Heads-up this is a monster on a paired board; the only realistic hand that currently beats me is pocket tens (TT) making a full house, and pocket fours are impossible since I have a 4. Flash is an aggressive, wide opener so his range contains a lot of single-pair hands (Tx, Jx, Kx, Qx) and dominated holdings that will gladly call for value. I want to build the pot and charge any T/pair or worse hands while still allowing a raise that identifies the rare TT. A pot-sized value bet (~600 into 600) gives good price pressure, extracts value from top pairs/kickers, and keeps the decisions clear if Flash raises. I'll bet 600 to get value and to protect my hand against unlikely turn runouts.

I started the hand by raising pre-flop with a pocket pair of deuces, which is a strong starting hand in heads-up play. The flop of 4c-Td-4d is relatively safe for my small pair, although the Ten is a concern. Minni has led out with a pot-sized raise (600 into 600). This lead from the big blind on a paired board often indicates either a piece of the board (like a Ten or a Four), a flush draw, or a pure bluff trying to capitalize on the fact that I likely missed that board. Given the stack depths and the pot odds (calling 600 to win 1200), I have a clear call here to see what happens on the turn. Folding immediately to one bet with a pocket pair on this board would be too weak, especially since her range contains many air hands and diamond draws.

I have the full house (444JJ) on the turn — an extremely strong, near‑nut hand heads‑up. Given Flash’s wide preflop raising and flop call, his continuing range contains a lot of single‑pair hands and medium pocket pairs; after seeing Jh there are very few combos that actually beat me (primarily pocket tens and the rare pocket jacks). This is a pure value spot: I want to charge every worse pair/Tx and medium pocket pair while still sizing to avoid overcommitting to the tiny portion of his range that dominates me. I will bet a large but non‑commitment size (3,000 into the 1,800 pot, ≈1.67× pot). That size extracts value from the hands that will call (T‑x, top pairs, smaller pocket pairs) and protects against giving a free river, yet if Flash raises strongly it heavily polarizes him toward the very few hands that beat me and lets me re‑evaluate (call smaller raises, fold or proceed cautiously to very large shoves).

I am holding pocket deuces on a board of 4c-Td-4d-Jh. My opponent, Minni, led into me on the flop and has now followed up with a significant overbet of 3,000 into a 1,800 pot on the turn. While deuces were a reasonable hand to call a single bet with on the flop, the Jack on the turn creates another overcard. Minni's aggressive line (donking the flop and overbetting the turn) strongly suggests she has at least a Ten, a Jack, or potentially a Four. Even if she is on a diamond draw, her overcards (like Q, K, or A) have significant equity against my small pair. Given that I have a massive chip lead and the hand is only on turn 120 of 150, there is no reason to risk a large portion of chips on such a weak holding. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Heads-up on the button (I already have the 50 SB posted) with Ah8s — a strong, playable hand heads-up. Limping/calling 50 is too passive and surrenders position advantage. I want initiative and fold equity versus Flash’s wide BB defending range, while keeping the raise size reasonable for deeper stacks. I’ll raise to 300 (3x BB total) — that balances aggression and pot control, isolates to one opponent, and leaves room to play postflop. If Flash calls I’ll c-bet favorable Ace-high/dry flops and control the pot on wet/connected boards; if he 3-bets large I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold to extreme sizes or call/4-bet depending on the sizing). Amount 300 is the total I’ll commit to the pot (including my posted 50).

I am in the Big Blind with a pocket pair of Threes (3s 3d). Minni has opened from the Button to 300. Pocket pairs play well in head-up pots, and even though Threes are a small pair, they have significant showdown value or 'set-mining' potential. Folding is too weak against a standard button open. While I could 3-bet to try and take the pot down now, calling allows me to see a flop for a relatively cheap price and keep the pot controlled, especially since I am out of position. I will call the 200 more.

On a flop of 7h-Jc-Th, my pocket threes are very weak. This board is highly coordinated and likely hits a significant portion of Minni's button-opening range, including many overcards, straight draws, and flush draws. Since I am out of position and have very little equity to improve (only two remaining threes in the deck), I will check and likely fold to any significant aggression.

I was the preflop aggressor (raised to 300) and Flash checked the J♥–Jc–Th two-heart flop to me. I hold A♥8♠ — Ace-high with a gutshot to a 9 (4 outs) and a backdoor heart possibility (a heart on the turn gives me a one-card flush draw to complete on the river). Heads‑up against Flash’s wide defending range, this is a textbook spot to continuation-bet to get folds from worse Jx/TX/low pairs and to charge broadway/straight draws. I’ll size to ~0.6 pot (350 into 600) to preserve fold equity while keeping the pot manageable if called. Plan: if he folds we take it; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn — continue on a 9 or heart, pot‑control or check on blanks, and fold to strong check‑raises or large turn aggression since I’m only currently Ace‑high with limited outs.

The board (7h Jc Th) is extremely coordinated and connects well with Minni's button-opening range, containing many overcards and potential draws. My pocket threes are an underpair to three of the board cards and have very low equity to improve (approximately 4% for a set on the turn). Given that I am out of position and facing aggression on a board that favors the pre-flop raiser's range, a fold is the most disciplined and correct play.

In a heads-up situation on the button, Ace-six offsuit is a strong starting hand. I will raise to 300, which has been my standard opening size, to take control of the pot and put pressure on the big blind.

Facing a standard 3bb open from the button. 8c3d is a very weak, offsuit junk hand and not something I want to 3-bet with out of position, but calling 200 more (300 total) is cheap given the pot (I get ~25% immediate pot odds). Flash has shown a wide, aggressive opening frequency; with deep stacks it's correct to defend a bit wider heads-up and see a flop. Plan: call to see the flop, play cautiously out of position — fold to heavy aggression on dry, high-card boards and only invest further when I pair/improve or can realistically realize equity. Do not 3-bet or force bloated pots with this hand.

I have 8c3d and the flop is Qc 8s 5h — I’ve flopped a pair of eights (weak kicker) on a fairly dry Q-high board. Flash opened wide preflop and is aggressive, so his range contains lots of Qx/overcards but also many bluffs. I’m out of position, so leading here (donk-bet) is suboptimal; pot control and denial of extra streets to better Qx hands is preferable. Plan: check and allow Flash to either c-bet (so I can call and control the pot with a made hand) or check back (I’ll re-evaluate on the turn). I’ll call reasonable c-bets but will be prepared to fold to large overbets that polarize to better hands.

I am the pre-flop aggressor with Ace-high on a fairly dry board (Q-8-5 rainbow). Minni checked to me. A continuation bet is standard here to take down the pot or deny equity to hands like J-T, T-9, or small connectors that missed. I'll bet 300, which is half the pot, to maintain pressure.

I have middle pair (8♣) with a very weak kicker (3♦) on Q♣ 8♠ 5♥. Flash opened wide preflop and has been showing an aggressive, wide c-betting frequency; this small bet (300 into a ~900 pot) is consistent with a wide, often weak range as well as some top-pair hands. Raising out of position with a weak kicker is unnecessary and can put me in bloated pots where I’m behind to Qx or overpairs. Calling is cheap here (≈25% pot odds) and preserves pot control while keeping worse hands and bluffs in. Plan: call the flop, reevaluate on the turn — fold to large polarized aggression if I don’t improve; extract value or protect if I pair up again or the board becomes favorable.

Check. I have middle pair (8) with a very weak 3-kicker on Qc‑8s‑5h‑Js. Pot is 1,200 and stacks are deep, so my priority OOP is pot control and avoiding getting bloated with a vulnerable hand. Flash’s line (wide preflop raise, small flop c-bet that I called, then a turn check) looks like a mixture of medium-strength hands and bluffs; the J on the turn helps his range (Jx/Qx/overcards). Betting here only protects a little and often folds out worse or induces a raise I don’t want to face — checking keeps bluffs in and lets me call reasonable river bets or fold to big polarized action. I’ll check and re-evaluate on the river.

On this turn, the Jack of Spades adds some connectivity to the board, but doesn't significantly improve my Ace-high. Minni has checked for the second time after calling a flop continuation bet. My Ace-high still has some showdown value against missed draws or lower kickers, and I don't want to turn my hand into a bluff against a range that likely contains Queens, Jacks, or middle pairs that aren't folding yet. Checking back allows me to see a river for free and potentially realize my showdown value or improve to the best hand.

I have middle pair (8♣) with a very weak 3‑kicker on Qc 8s 5h Js Td. Flash opened wide and c‑bet small on the flop (I called), then checked the turn and checked the river — a line consistent with either medium/top pair pot‑control or pure air. The river completed a lot of overcard/straight combinations (a 9 in hand makes the straight), so my hand is vulnerable and has limited value. Betting thin for value here would often fold out worse hands I beat and can get me raised by better hands; checking preserves showdown and avoids bloating the pot OOP. With pot control and decent showdown equity versus Flash’s checking range, I check to take the pot to showdown for free.

The board is Qc-8s-5h-Js-Td. My Ace-high has very little showdown value here, as almost any hand Minni called the flop with (pairs or draws) has likely overtaken me or already held a pair. Specifically, any 9 makes a straight (8-9-T-J-Q). Since Minni checked for the third time, she might be weak, but she could also be trapping with a marginal made hand or a straight. Given that I have a significant chip lead and my hand has zero equity against any pair, checking back is the most prudent play to see the showdown without risking more chips on a board that evolved poorly for my range.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with KcTs — a strong, position-sensitive hand. Pot is 150 and it’s only 50 to call, but I want to take initiative and apply pressure with a standard 3xBB-sized open to generate fold equity and build a pot I can play in position. Flash is deep-stacked and will defend wide, so 300 is big enough to fold out some weak holdings while not so large that I’m committing myself versus a 3-bet. Plan: c-bet favorable flops and use position to extract value or take the pot away.

I am in the Big Blind with T4o, a very weak hand. Minni has raised to 3BB from the Button. While I am getting 2:1 odds to call, T4o is near the bottom of my range and plays poorly out of position. Given my significant stack lead and the goal of maximizing my stack over the remaining hands, there is no need to enter a pot with such a weak holding.

Trey-eight offsuit is a very weak hand even in a heads-up situation. It has poor connectivity and limited potential for improvement post-flop. Given the stack advantage and the quality of the hand, folding is the standard and safest play.

Heads-up on the button (I’m SB) with Th5s — a marginal hand but well within a standard heads-up open range. Pot is 150, to-call 50 and min-raise is 200. With deep stacks (133bb vs 265bb) I want to seize the initiative and apply pressure while risking a small portion of my stack. A 2x-style open to 200 gives me fold equity to take down the blinds, keeps the pot small if called, and lets me play position postflop. Plan: fold to a large, polarizing 3-bet; call or continue cautiously vs small 3-bets depending on size; c-bet favorable flops and check/move to pot control on whiffy textures. Committing 200 total here is the right balance of aggression and preservation of stack.

I am in the Big Blind holding Queen-Eight offsuit. Minni, on the button, has min-raised to 200. With 100 more to call into a pot of 300, I am getting 3:1 pot odds. Q8o is a standard defending hand in the Big Blind against a small open from the button, especially since I am deep-stacked and have a significant lead. I will call to see the flop.

On this flop of Ad-Ah-Qc, I have flopped queens up (two pair, Queens and Aces) with an 8 kicker. This is a very strong hand, though I must be wary of an Ace or a better Queen. Minni raised pre-flop and has checked or bet small in similar spots before. Given the board is highly paired and static, checking to the aggressor is standard to see how they react. If Minni checks behind, I likely have the best hand. If Minni bets, I will call to keep their bluffs in and control the size of the pot, as raising might fold out everything I beat except a better Queen.

I opened preflop and Flash checked the dry but heavily Ace‑weighted flop Ad Ah Qc. My T♥5♠ has no showdown value and doesn’t block strong parts of his range, so I should use my initiative to pick up the pot or force weaker holdings to fold. A small c‑bet (~150 into a 400 pot, ~35% pot) has a low break‑even fold frequency (~27%) and will fold many of Flash’s non‑Ace hands while keeping the pot manageable if called. Plan: bet 150 now; if Flash raises big I’ll fold, if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn and mainly give up unless I improve or get a clear read.

On a board of Ad-Ah-Qc, I have flopped Queens and Aces with an eight kicker. This is a very strong hand on a very dry, paired board. Minni has made a small continuation bet of 150 into 400. While I have a strong hand, an Ace is very much in Minni's range, and there are few hands that will call a check-raise that I actually beat (mostly lower pocket pairs or pure bluffs). By calling, I keep her range wide, allowing her to continue bluffing on later streets or value betting worse hands. I'm getting excellent odds to see a turn and maintain pot control.

On the turn, the board is Ad-Ah-Qc-2c. I hold Queens and Aces with an eight kicker. This is still a very strong hand, but checking to the aggressor remains the best play for pot control and to keep Minni's bluffing range active. If I bet now, I risk folding out her air or getting raised by a hand that has me beat, like an Ace or a superior Queen. By checking, I can gauge her interest in the pot and potentially catch another bluff or see a cheap river.

Board: Ad Ah Qc 2c. I hold T♥5♠ — no pair, no club, no meaningful equity. On the flop I c-bet small and got called, so Flash’s calling range contains many Qx, medium pairs and some Ax (trip Aces) and club backdoors. Flash checking the turn is ambiguous — could be a weak holding or a trap with trips, but many of his non-A holdings will fold to continued pressure. I want to seize the initiative and force folds from Qx and medium pairs while keeping the pot controllable against Ax. Betting ~300 into the 700 pot (~43% pot) gives a reasonable break‑even fold frequency (~30%) and is large enough to apply pressure but small enough that I can fold to a sizeable check‑raise without committing a big portion of my stack. Plan: bet 300 now; if Flash check‑raises significantly I’ll fold (I have no pair). If he calls, I’ll pot‑control on the river (check behind unless the river gives a very clear bluffing opportunity or I pick up equity).

On a board of Ad-Ah-Qc-2c, I have two pair, Queens and Aces. Minni's small bet on the turn (300 into 700) suggests she could be betting a wide range, including weaker Queens, pocket pairs, or air, though she could also be slow-playing an Ace. Given the pot odds (3.33 to 1) and the strength of my hand, calling is the most profitable decision. Raising would likely only be called by better hands (like an Ace or a superior Queen), while folding is too tight for a hand this strong on such a board.

On a final board of Ad-Ah-Qc-2c-8s, I hold Aces and Queens with an eight kicker. This is a strong showdown hand, but I am out of position. Minni has been betting small on previous streets, which could indicate a weak Queen, a slow-played Ace, or a bluff. Leading out now would likely cause her to fold her bluffs and only call with hands that beat me (like an Ace or a better Queen). By checking, I allow her to continue bluffing or to check behind with marginal hands that I beat. I will check and plan to call a reasonable bet.

Pot is 1,300. I opened, c-bet flop (Ad Ah Qc) for 150 and barreled the turn (2c) for 300; Flash called both streets. I have T♥5♠ — no pair, no club, no meaningful blocker to A or Q. His line of calling flop and turn and then checking the river is consistent with a calling-range that beats me: Qx, medium pocket pairs, occasionally the Ac (trip Aces) or slow-played strong hands. Bluffing here is thin: I lack blockers to make an Ace or Queen rep credible, and a small bluff will often be called while a large bluff commits too much against hands that already call down. Over 150-hand strategy and deep stacks favor not throwing more chips away with a near-zero equity hand. I’ll pot‑control and check behind to avoid getting exploited.
In heads-up play, T9o is a strong opening hand from the button. I'll make a standard raise to 300 to take control of the hand and put pressure on Minni.

Flash opened to 300 from the button (3x). In heads-up that sizing represents a very wide range, but I'm out of position. 9c6h is marginal but defendable from the big blind with deep stacks. The pot is 400 and I only need to call 200 more (total 300), so I get ~33% pot odds. Calling keeps the pot size reasonable and preserves post‑flop playability; 3‑betting OOP with 9‑6o is a poor EV play and folding is too nitty heads‑up. Plan: call to see the flop, play passively OOP — check back many dry boards, continue only with clear equity (pairs, two pair+, or strong draws), fold to large pressure when missed.

I called preflop with 9♣6♥ as a marginal defend and planned to play passively OOP. Flop is 3♦ T♦ K♥ — I completely missed: no pair, no diamond draw, no meaningful backdoor. This is a dry board where betting OOP with air is a losing play. Check to keep the pot controllable and fold to normal continuation bets. If Flash checks back I get a free turn; if he c‑bets very small (rarely) I might call only if I believe he bluffs a lot, but I’ll fold to standard or large c‑bets (~>200–300). Preserve the stack and wait for a spot with real equity or a better bluffing opportunity.

On this flop of K-T-3, I have middle pair with a decent kicker. This is a board that generally favors my pre-flop raising range, though the King is a big card for both players. Minni has checked to me. I'll make a continuation bet of 300 to extract value from draws (like flush draws or straight draws) and weaker pairs, while also protecting my hand against overcards.

Out of position with 9♣6♥ on 3♦ T♦ K♥. I have no pair, no diamond draw and only a weak backdoor heart — essentially zero real equity. Pot is 900 and the bet is 300, so I need ~25% equity to justify a call. Even versus a wide heads‑up c‑bet range my hand’s equity is well under that, and being OOP makes any post‑flop playability terrible. Raising as a bluff OOP with no fold equity or equity backup is a negative‑EV line. I defend marginally preflop, but on this dry, high‑card flop I should concede the pot, preserve a large stack (12,425) and wait for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button (small blind). 7♠4♦ is one of the worst hands — poor suits and connectivity — but folding now simply throws away my 50-chip SB. Calling an extra 50 to complete to 100 gives me good pot odds (50 into a 200 pot ≈ 25%) and the positional advantage post‑flop. Raising to 200 would bloat the pot with a weak hand and make me vulnerable to a 3‑bet from an often aggressive BB (Flash). Plan: call cheaply to see the flop, use position to c-bet small if I hit or to check‑fold vs large aggression when I miss; pursue value if I pair a 7 or 4 or pick up a strong draw.

I have T8o in the big blind. Minni has limped in from the button/small blind. This is a very weak play that allows me to see a flop for free. However, T8o is a decent hand to check and see a flop with, and since I am already in the big blind and there is no raise to call, I will check to see the flop and preserve my stack.

I have top pair with an Eight on a very dry board (8d-2c-3s). Minni limped pre-flop, which often indicates a wide, weak range. Since I have top pair, I have the best hand most of the time here. I will bet to gain value from her potential draws or weaker pairs (like 2x, 3x, or smaller pocket pairs) and to protect my hand from overcards.

Facing Flash’s 150 into a 350 pot on 8♦-2♣-3♠ with 7♠4♦: pot odds to call are 150/(350+150)=30%, but I only have ~6 clean outs (pair a 7 or 4), roughly a 24% chance to improve by the river. There are no strong backdoor draws (only runner-runner possibilities), and Flash is an aggressive opponent who often bets for value on flops like this. Calling to ‘float’ is a possibility but is negative EV here given the math and the likelihood Flash continues; a check-raise bluff would be too risky with such a weak hand. I’ll fold, preserve my stack and wait for a better spot in position against this opponent.

I am on the button with 9c4c. In a heads-up situation, suited connectors or gappers are often playable from the button, but 94 suited is quite weak. However, I have a significant chip lead (27625 vs 12225) and have been aggressive pre-flop. Raising here to put pressure on the big blind is a standard play, but given the weakness of the hand, a fold is also acceptable. However, in this specific meta-game against Minni, I have found success with standard 3x raises. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot early.

I will call to 300. A♥4♥ is a strong heads‑up hand with good playability and suit for flush/backdoor combos; facing a button open to 300 I only need 200 more into a 400 pot (break‑even equity ≈33%), and against a wide CO/button opening range A4s easily meets that. Flash has position on later streets, so I prefer to defend and keep the pot controllable rather than 3‑bet a marginal kicker out of position. With deep stacks there are healthy implied odds to realize equity; plan is to proceed post‑flop (value on A/heart flops, pot control or fold to heavy aggression on dry boards). If Flash 3‑bets to a sizable sizing preflop I’ll re‑evaluate (fold or pot‑control) — but for this open, calling is the +EV play.

Flop: J♠ J♦ A♣. My A♥4♥ gives me two pair (Aces & Jacks) — a very strong heads‑up hand. Flash opened wide preflop so his range contains many worse hands (weaker A‑x, Kx, broadways, small pairs) but also the combos that beat me (any J in hand → trips; AJ or pocket Aces → full house). I want to extract value and deny free cards while keeping the pot controllable. Lead a value bet ~half‑pot to charge worse hands and fold out pure air. If Flash raises I’ll re‑evaluate by sizing (small raise likely thin/value or bluff → call; large shove/overbet is usually trips/full house and I’ll protect my stack by folding), if he calls I’ll proceed on the turn for more value or pot‑control depending on the card.

On a flop of Js-Jd-Ac, Minni has led out for 300 (a donk bet) after calling my pre-flop raise. My hand, 9c-4c, has virtually no equity on this board. While the board is good for my perceived range, her leading into the pre-flop aggressor often indicates either a strong hand (an Ace or a Jack) or an attempt to take the pot away with a bluff. Given my significant chip lead and the low probability of my hand improving, I will fold and wait for a better opportunity rather than engaging in a high-variance bluffing war.

Heads-up on the button (SB). 8♠2♣ is a garbage hand, but in heads-up play the button should open very wide to seize initiative and pressure the big blind. I’ll raise to the min (200 total, 2x BB) — small enough to avoid bloating the pot with a weak hand but large enough to take the lead and fold out some of BB’s weaker holdings. Plan: fold to a sizable 3-bet with this holding; if Flash calls I’ll play the flop in position, c-bet selectively on favorable textures and give up to strong resistance on obvious hits.

I am in the big blind with 6d 4s. The button, Minni, has raised to 200. This is a very weak hand with poor playability, and although the price to call is only 100 more into a 300 pot (offering 3:1 odds), I am out of position against an opponent who has been aggressive. I have a significant chip lead (27,275 vs 12,425) and there's no reason to defend with such a marginal hand.

In a heads-up situation, opening from the button with most hands is standard. Although 86o is a marginal hand, I will raise to 300 (3x the big blind) to maintain aggression and attempt to steal the blinds or play the pot in position against Minni, who has been folding to several of my pre-flop raises recently.

Flash opened to 300 from the button. Pot is 400 and I need to call 200 more (total committed 300 including my BB). Heads‑up, facing a 3x open, I should defend a wide range — the pot odds (~200 to win ~600 → ~25% equity) justify seeing a flop with a marginal but playable hand. 3♣5♦ is weak but has some post‑flop playability (can hit pair, two‑pair, or runout straights) and I don’t want to fold purely for fear of aggression. A 3‑bet would be overly ambitious with this holding. Plan: call, see flop, continue only if I improve or the board is very favorable; fold to heavy aggression when completely missed.

I completely missed this flop with 3♣5♦ on 8♠ J♥ 7♣ — no pair and no realistic draw (only a runner‑runner straight). Heads‑up the preflop raiser’s range contains lots of jacks, eights, broadways and connected cards that fare much better on this board, and I’m out of position. Betting first here would be a thin bluff that bloats the pot with almost no equity; better to keep the pot small and let the aggressor reveal strength. Plan: check and give up to significant pressure. I may call a very tiny probe if it’s a token stab that offers correct pot odds to see a cheap turn, but I’ll fold to a standard c‑bet (typical sizes ≥ ~1/3–1/2 pot).

On a flop of 8s-Jh-7c, I have middle pair (8s) with a 6 kicker. The board is quite coordinated, but middle pair is a relatively strong hand in a heads-up pot. Since Minni checked to me, I will place a continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) to realize my equity, protect against overcards, and see where I stand. If Minni calls or raises, I can re-evaluate on the turn.

I called preflop with 3♣5♦ as a defensive BB call, but I completely missed the flop (8♠ J♥ 7♣) — no pair and no realistic draw. I’m out of position and facing Flash’s 300 into a 900 pot (I must call 300 to win 1200 → ~25% equity required). My hand has negligible equity vs a realistic c‑betting range (Jx, 8x, broadways, connected cards) and I have no fold‑equity or realistic backdoor outs. The bet size is a standard/half‑pot c‑bet, so calling or raising would be a losing long‑term play. Fold and conserve stack for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button with J♣7♦. I’ve already posted the SB (50) and to call is only 50 more, but in heads-up standard strategy is to open-raise wide from the button to seize initiative and use position postflop. Flash (BB) is deep and aggressive but also defends a lot, so a small, standard raise to 200 total (2×BB) is the right play: it’s cheap relative to stacks, applies pressure to win the blinds, and gives me the lead and positional advantage if called. If Flash 3-bets big I’ll fold this marginal holding; if called I’ll play postflop in position — c-bet on dry, uncoordinated flops, control pot size on dangerous/connected boards, and continue only with clear equity (pairs, good draws, or well-timed bluffs). Raising also keeps my range wide and takes advantage of heads-up dynamics, so raise to 200.

I am holding 8h 3s in the Big Blind. Minni has raised to 200 (a 2x open). While the price to call is small (100 more), 83o is one of the weakest hands in poker and plays very poorly out of position. Given the stack depths and the fact that I am significantly ahead in the overall match, there is no reason to defend with such a marginal hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button in a heads-up situation with 9h 7c. This is a standard hand to open for a raise to maintain pressure and take control of the pot early. My standard opening raise in this session has been 300, and I will continue with that sizing to induce folds or see a flop in position.

Heads-up, I’m in the big blind with Q♦5♦ facing a button open to 300. Pot is 400 and I have 200 more to call — that gives me ~33% break-even equity. Q5♦s is a marginal hand but has decent playability heads-up (backdoor flush, Q-outs) and good equity vs a wide opening range from an aggressive button. I don’t want to 3-bet this hand OOP as a thin bluff (min-3bet to 500) because it creates difficult postflop decisions against a deep, loose opponent and I’ll often be dominated by other Qx hands. Calling keeps the pot controllable, leverages implied odds when I hit, and lets me fold to heavy pressure on dry boards. Plan: see the flop, continue with top pair/strong draws, fold otherwise to large bets, and only 3-bet/raise with stronger holdings or balanced bluffs in future spots.

Check. Heads-up versus Flash’s wide button open, Q♦5♦ is just queen-high on A♠ J♣ 6♦ — no pair, only a weak backdoor diamond (need both turn+river → ~4% to make a flush) and only 6 thin pair-outs (3 queens, 3 fives) that will frequently be second-best with an Ace on board. I called preflop to keep the pot small and planned to continue only with real hits or strong draws; this spot fits that plan. Checking keeps the pot controllable and lets me fold to significant pressure rather than bloating the pot OOP with a marginal holding. If Flash makes a small c-bet (≈≤1/3 pot) I will consider a float to exploit his wide range and see a turn, but I will fold to larger bets (≈≥1/2 pot) and wait for a clean improvement (Q/5 or backdoor runout) before getting aggressive.

I am the pre-flop aggressor on a board (As Jc 6d) that heavily favors my range over the defender's. Minni's check is standard. I will place a continuation bet of half the pot (300 chips) to represent an Ace or a Jack and capitalize on the fold equity I have against her non-connecting hands.

Situation: heads-up in the big blind with Q♦5♦ on J♣ 6♦ A♠. Pot is 900 and Flash has bet 300 (small c-bet ≈1/3 pot). Assessment: Q5♦ is a marginal holding — no pair, only two-runner diamond backdoor and a few pair-outs — but the bet size gives me ~25% break-even equity (300 to win ~1,200). Flash has shown a wide, aggressive opening/c-bet frequency in prior hands, so his flop-betting range will contain many bluffs and weaker holdings. Plan: call and float the flop to see the turn — this keeps the pot controlled OOP and lets me realize equity and exploit later streets if he continues with air. I will only continue aggressively if I pair the Q/5 or pick up a clear diamond runout; otherwise fold to substantial turn pressure (≥ ~1/2 pot) to avoid bloating the pot with a second-best hand. I will not 3-bet/raise OOP with this marginal holding.

Flash checked the turn after c-betting the flop. I hold Qd5d and missed — the 3♥ on the turn kills my backdoor diamond plan, so I only have ~6 clean outs (~13% to improve on the river) and essentially no showdown value. With one street left a small, exploitative stab is correct: a ~1/3‑pot bet (≈400) only needs about a 10–11% fold frequency to be +EV given my slim equity when called. Flash has shown a wide, aggressive range and will check with a lot of marginal hands, so a small bet should pick up the pot enough of the time to justify the risk. If he calls I can still hit a Q/5 on the river; if he check‑raises I will fold to avoid bloating the pot out of position. Bet 400 to either take the pot now or realize my (small) equity to the river and re-evaluate.

I am currently holding 9-high with no draw on a board of As-Jc-6d-3h. I attempted a continuation bet on the flop to represent the Ace, but Minni called and has now led into me on the turn. Since I have zero showdown value and Minni's lead indicates she likely has a piece of the board (a Jack, a Six, or an Ace) that she isn't willing to fold to further aggression, there is no reason to continue with this bluff. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button I want to take the initiative and use position. 2♣8♦ is a very weak hand, but folding loses the posted 50 and limp/call surrenders initiative and often leads to a tougher postflop spot against a wide BB defense. I’ll make a standard heads-up open to the minimum raise (to 200 total) to apply pressure, pick up the blind some of the time, and keep the pot manageable when called. If Flash 3-bets a significant amount I will fold this weak holding to avoid playing dominated/deep-stack spots; if he calls I’ll play postflop in position (c-bet when the flop favors my range, check/control or fold to heavy action when I miss). This raise balances exploitation and risk and maximizes long-run EV in heads-up play.

I am holding Queen-Jack offsuit in the Big Blind. This is a strong hand heads-up. Minni has raised to 200 from the Small Blind, which is a common 2x opening. I will put in a 3-bet to 700 to take initiative and extract value from weaker hands in Minni's opening range. If called, I have a hand that plays well on many flops.

I opened to 200 from the button with 2♣8♦ and Flash 3‑bet to 700. The pot is 900 and I must call 500 (or 1200 to min‑raise). 2‑8 offsuit is the absolute worst hand — heavily dominated by any reasonable 3‑bet range — and my equity vs a 3‑bet range is well below the ~35.7% break‑even threshold (500/(900+500)). While I have position postflop, the hand rarely fares well and a 4‑bet bluff here has poor blockers and high risk versus Flash’s deep stack and aggressive tendencies. Consistent with my preflop plan to fold large 3‑bets with very weak holdings, I’ll fold and preserve my stack for better spots.

I am on the button with 8s 6d. This is a standard hand where I can choose to either raise or fold. Given the 150-hand limit and my significant stack lead, I will maintain a consistent strategy of opening 3x from the button to put pressure on the big blind. If the opponent raises, I can easily fold this marginal holding.

I have A♥T♣ in the big blind facing a 3x open to 300 from the small blind. Heads‑up ATo is a strong defending hand and the caller investment is small: I must call 200 into a 400 pot (≈33% break‑even). I’m out of position, stacks are deep, and Flash’s open range from the SB appears fairly wide — 3‑betting OOP with ATo will build a bigger pot and put me in tough spots postflop; a minimum 3‑bet won’t fold enough worse hands to be profitable. I’ll call to see a flop and keep the pot manageable. Postflop plan: proceed aggressively when I hit top pair or strong draws; on dry boards I’ll often check and fold to heavy pressure, and on Ace/Ten boards I’ll extract value or control the pot based on texture and villain action.

I have A♥T♣ on Th-6s-Kh: top pair with the Ace blocker but I’m out of position versus the preflop raiser (Flash) who has a wide, often aggressive raising range that contains a lot of Kx and other hands that currently beat me. Betting OOP here risks getting check‑raised or bloating a pot when I’m likely behind to many of his value combos; checking controls the pot size and lets me realize equity against his bluffs and weaker holdings.

Plan: check and evaluate. If Flash leads a small‑to‑medium c‑bet (roughly ≤1/2 pot) I’ll call and keep the pot manageable, intending to extract more value on A/T turn cards or fold to large pressure on blanks. If he checks back I’ll re‑evaluate the turn and often bet for thin value on favorable runouts or pot‑control on dry cards. I’ll fold to a large check‑raise or oversized barreling line unless I improve, since those lines are consistent with Kx/sets that dominate me. Checking now maximizes long‑term EV by avoiding marginal OOP confrontations while preserving showdown value.

On this flop (Kh-Th-6s), I have bottom pair (sixes). This board is quite coordinated and favors the caller's range (Minni), which contains many broadway cards and hearts. While I have some showdown value, checking back allows me to control the size of the pot and see how the turn develops without being forced to fold to a check-raise. I am ahead of some bluffs but behind any King, Ten, or drawing hands that hit. Checking is a safe way to proceed with a marginal pair.

I’m first to act on the turn with A♣T♣? (actually A♥T♣) — I have top pair with the best possible kicker on Th‑6s‑Kh‑Qc. Flash is the preflop raiser and checked back the flop, so his range is wide and contains lots of hands I beat (weaker tens, some Ax, single‑pair broadways, floats) as well as Kx/Qx/sets that beat me. The turn Q is a little scary for parts of his range (it gives Qx combos), but it’s also a good card to thinly value‑bet because many worse hands will still call and some bluffs/floats will fold. I want to extract value while keeping the pot manageable out of position and I don’t want to give a free river. A half‑pot bet achieves that: it gets called by worse pairs/Tx and floats, charges any backdoor draws, and keeps a straightforward fold plan vs strong aggression. If Flash check‑raises the turn I’ll treat that as a strong line (Kx/sets/two‑pair) and fold rather than bloating the pot OOP with a marginal hand. If he just calls I’ll re‑evaluate river and either thin‑value bet or pot‑control depending on the card. Bet ~1/2 pot to extract value and define the hand.

The turn card is the Queen of Clubs, making the board King-Queen-Ten-Six. This board is extremely coordinated and hits a huge portion of the big blind's defending range. Almost any two broadway cards (J, Q, K, A) now beat my bottom pair of sixes. Any Jack has made a straight. Minni's lead for half the pot indicates she likely has a piece of this board or a strong draw. With only bottom pair on such a dangerous board, I have very little equity and should not continue. Folding is the safest and most mathematically sound play here.

Heads-up on the button with K♥5♥ — a playable, suited king with good blocker value. I want initiative and to pressure the big blind rather than limp; standard 3x-ish open is appropriate. I’ll raise to 300 total to steal the blind and play postflop in position. If Flash 3-bets large I’ll fold K5s; if called I’ll C‑bet favorable flops and control the pot using position.

I am in the Big Blind with Jd Ts. Minni, on the Button, has raised to 300. JTo is a solid hand to defend my big blind against a standard button open. The pot odds are favorable (calling 200 into a pot of 400), and while I will be out of position, the hand has enough post-flop playability to continue.

I missed the flop completely with Jack-Ten offsuit. The board contains an Ace, which is well within the pre-flop raiser's (Minni's) range. I will check and see how Minni acts, but I am prepared to fold to most bets given the lack of connectivity or equity with my hand.

I opened preflop and Flash just checked the A♣ 6♦ 3♣ flop. I have K♥5♥ — no pair and no club, but I have a King blocker and a tiny backdoor heart plan. As the preflop aggressor I should continue to apply pressure on this dry-ish A‑high board: many of Flash’s check-calling/marginal hands (missed broadways, small pairs, some floats) fold to a c‑bet, and a modest size also keeps the pot manageable if I’m called. I’ll make a small continuation bet (~1/3 pot = 200) to take it down now or gather info. If he folds I win the pot; if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn and plan to check behind most bricks or fold to strong aggression since I have very little showdown equity without improvement.

I have Jack-Ten offsuit and the flop came Ace-high. I have no pair and no real draws. Minni's small continuation bet of 200 into a 600 pot is standard for an Ace-high board, which heavily favors her pre-flop raising range. Since I have no equity or viable draws to continue, I will fold and wait for a better spot.
I am on the button with Queen-Ten suited (hearts), which is a very strong starting hand in heads-up play. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and leverage my positional advantage.

Opponent (Flash) opened to 300 from the button/SB. I’m in the big blind with 9♥5♠ and must call 200 more into a 400 pot, so I’m getting ~33% pot odds. Heads-up the button’s opening range is wide, so 9‑5o has enough equity vs a typical steal to justify a defend. I’m out of position which hurts postflop play, and 3‑betting with this weak offsuit hand is too thin and exploitative only rarely, so the correct play is to call and see a flop. Postflop plan: proceed cautiously OOP — check/fold to large, disciplined pressure on dry boards, float and realize equity on favorable runouts, and selectively continue when I pick up pair/backdoor draws or when villain shows weakness.

Out of position with a weak, unpaired hand (9♥5♠) on J♣ 8♦ 7♠. I have only a 4‑out gutshot to a straight (4 sixes) — ~8.5% on the turn, ~16% to hit by river — and no meaningful backdoor equity. Pot is 600 and there’s no bet; against a wide button opening range he will c‑bet often, but I don’t want to inflate the pot OOP with such thin equity. Check for pot control and to allow villain to bluff or show weakness. Plan: check‑call small c‑bets to realize equity, fold to large/committed bets or raises, and only barrel/raise later if I pick up a pair or a good draw or see a clear exploitable pattern.

I have two overcards and a gutshot straight draw (a 9 makes a straight). Since Minni checked to me, I will place a continuation bet of 300 to maintain aggression and potentially take down the pot now, or build it for when I improve on the turn.

Call. Flash bet 300 into a 600 pot (pot now 900); calling 300 gives me ~25% break‑even. With 9♥5♠ on J♣8♦7♠ I have ~10 clean outs (3 nines, 3 fives, 4 sixes) — roughly ~38% to improve by the river and reasonable equity versus a wide button c‑betting range. Out of position, a raise is too thin and would bloat the pot with marginal equity, so the +EV line is to realize equity and keep the pot controllable.
