I’ll call now, see the turn, and fold to large/committed pressure unless I pair or hit the straight (or pick up a clear read to bluff).

Recap: I defended the BB with 9♥5♠ vs a standard button open and called a small flop c‑bet on J♣8♦7♠. Turn K♣ checked to me; pot is 1,200 and there is no bet to call. Current hand: I have no pair and only 10 raw outs to improve to a pair or the straight (three 9s, three 5s, four 6s). From the turn to the river that’s ~10/46 ≈ 21.7% to hit. The K♣ also brings a second club on board, which increases the chance that some of my pair/straight outs are “dirty” (a club on the river may give the opponent a flush if they hold clubs).

Opponent read: Flash is a frequent raiser and c‑better (aggressive), but his check on the turn shows either pot‑control with a made hand or weakness in a wide range — not a clear limp to be bluffed every time. Betting OOP here would be a thin semi‑bluff: I’d risk bloating the pot with only ~22% equity and limited fold equity against a player who will call decent portions of his range (Kx, Jx, many pairs, some clubs). A small bet could fold out some hands, but will also get called by enough hands that I’d be playing a big pot OOP with marginal equity.

Decision: check. This keeps the pot under control, lets me realize my ~22% chance to improve on the river without committing chips unnecessarily, and preserves options — fold to turn/river aggression if I miss, or extract value if I pair/complete the straight. If Flash leads on the river and I haven’t improved I’ll fold most reasonable-sized bets; if I hit I’ll size for value according to the river texture and opponent’s line.

On the turn, the board now shows 8d-7s-Jc-Kc. I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a 9) and overcards to the 8 and 7, though the Jack and King are now on the board. Since Minni checked to me again, I have the option to check back and take a free card to realize my equity. Given the board texture and Minni's tendency to call flop bets, checking allows me to see the river without risking more chips on a semi-bluff that might get called by any Jack or King in her range.

Check. I have 9♥5♠ with no pair — the board is 8♦ 7♠ J♣ K♣ 8♠ (the eights are on board). I have zero showdown value and no meaningful blockers. Line summary: Flash opened and c-bet the flop (wide, aggressive range), I called; both checked the turn. That line is consistent with a wide range that includes missed draws, Jx, Kx, and infrequent 8x/sets. The river pairing to an 8 actually improves many parts of his betting range (trips, full houses, or hands willing to value-bet), and given Flash’s history of calling and aggression, a river bluff here has poor fold equity. With deep stacks and no blockers, betting is a thin, low-expected-value bluff that will get called too often; checking keeps the pot small and preserves stack. Plan: check and fold to any meaningful river bet; only consider a bluff in very specific reads that he checks back too often on this runout (not the case here).

I have Queen-high on a board of 8-7-J-K-8. While I missed my gutshot straight draw, Minni has checked three times. However, her call on the flop suggests she has at least a piece of the board, such as a Jack or a 7. After I checked back on the turn, my hand looks weak, and a river bluff might not be enough to fold out a pair. While Queen-high rarely wins at showdown, checking back is the safest play to preserve my chip lead, as betting into a 'sticky' opponent who has shown interest in the pot early on is risky.

Raise to 200 (2x BB). In heads-up on the button I should open very wide — 9♣4♥ is marginal but fine to attack with deep stacks and positional leverage. A 200 total raise preserves initiative, gives fold equity vs the big blind, and keeps post-flop play in my hands (I act last after the flop). If Flash 3-bets big I'll fold and avoid bloating the pot with a poor hand; if he calls I can continuation-bet favorable flops or pot-control on dry boards.

I am in the Big Blind with 8d 4s, which is a very weak hand. Minni has raised to 200 (a min-raise). While the pot odds are 3:1 to call, this hand plays extremely poorly post-flop and is generally outside the defensive range even in heads-up play. Given my significant stack lead and the fact that we are approaching the final hands of the match, there is no reason to take unnecessary risks or splash chips with junk hands. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Holding Ace-Deuce offsuit on the button, I have a standard opening hand. Given the stack depths and previous dynamics where I've been raising most of my buttons to 300, I will continue with this sizing to maintain pressure and potentially take the blinds or play a pot with a range advantage.

Facing a button open to 300 from the SB while I’m in the big blind with A♣6♠. Pot is 400 and I need 200 more (300 total) — about 33% pot odds. Flash opens a wide range from the button frequently, so A6o has sufficient equity to call and see a flop cheaply. I’m out of position so a small 3-bet to 500 won’t gain much fold equity and a larger 3-bet is unnecessary with a marginal hand. Plan: flat-call to evaluate the flop, continue with top pair or strong draws, and exercise pot control or fold to heavy aggression if I miss.

The flop J♠ T♠ Q♦ is very connected and heavily favors a preflop raiser’s range (AK = Broadway straight, K9, QJ/JT two-pair combos, sets, lots of pairs and strong draws). I hold A♣6♠ — just Ace-high with a single spade (only a remote backdoor flush needing two spades) and essentially no immediate showdown value. Betting OOP here would be a thin bluff into a range that hits this board very often; I don’t have enough fold equity or equity to continue aggresssively. Best plan is pot control and information: check and see how Flash reacts. If he checks back I can realize my equity cheaply; if he bets a small continuation bet I can call to see a turn (or call a reasonable-sized bet with fold-to-large action plan), but I will fold to heavy aggression unless I improve (ace or a turn spade that gives a real draw).

I have Ace-Deuce offsuit on a very coordinated board of Jack-Ten-Queen. While I have an overcard (the Ace), this board texture hits Minni's calling range extremely well (Broadway cards, pairs, straight draws). Checking back is the most prudent play here to realize my equity and avoid being check-raised on a board where I have very little connection.

Check. I hold A♣6♠ on J♠ T♠ Q♦ 5♠ — Ace-high plus a 4‑to‑a‑flush on the turn. The turn gives me ~19.6% to hit a spade on the river and ~6.5% to pair my Ace, but I’m still behind most of the preflop raiser’s believable range (pairs, two‑pair/sets, K9 straights, broadway combos). Flash checked the flop and checked the turn, so betting OOP here would bloat the pot and risk facing calls/raises from many hands that already beat me. I prefer pot control and to realize equity cheaply: check and call a small river bet with my draw, fold to heavy aggression, and only fire for value or bluff on the river if I actually improve (spade or pair) or the river texture is particularly favorable.

On the turn, the board has become even more dangerous with three spades appearing. I hold the Ace of spades, which gives me the nut flush draw. Minni has checked again. While I could bet as a semi-bluff, checking back allows me to realize my equity for free and potentially hit the nut flush on the river, or simply win at showdown if it goes check-check and my Ace high is good. Given the coordinated nature of the board, checking is a safer way to control the pot.

River: J♠ T♠ Q♦ 5♠ 5♦ — I hold A♣6♠, so I missed: I have only Ace‑high and the board is very connected and paired. Flash was the preflop raiser and checked through (flop/turn/river), which can be either a missed broadway or a medium/strong pair he’s pot‑controlling. Betting here as a bluff has very little fold equity against his calling tendencies and would be thin value at best (almost every Q/J/T/paired or better hand calls). Pot is 600 and my stack is deep enough to avoid unnecessary donations. Best line is to check and take the showdown — don’t bluff into a range that fares much better than my Ace‑high.

I have Ace-high with the Ace of spades. The board is highly coordinated (Js Ts Qd 5s 5d) and almost any pair in Minni's range beats me. While I hold the nut flush blocker (As), Minni has checked three streets, suggesting she might have a marginal made hand like a Ten or a Jack that she's willing to take to showdown in a small pot. Given the small pot size (600), it's not worth a large bluff, and my Ace-high has some (albeit very slim) showdown value against her total misses or lower Ace-highs. I will check and concede if beaten.

Heads-up from the button (I’m the SB) I should be very wide preflop. 5♥7♠ is marginal but perfectly playable in position — I want to seize initiative and keep the pot manageable. Raising to the minimum (200 total) applies pressure to the BB, preserves fold equity, and gives me positional advantage postflop. If Flash 3-bets big I’ll fold most of the time (hand has poor equity vs a strong 3-bet); if he calls I’ll c-bet thin on dry boards and proceed cautiously on wet/connected boards, using position to control pot size. Completing to 100 is a passive option but raising maximizes EV in this heads-up spot.

In a heads-up situation, Queen-Nine offsuit (Q9o) is a strong enough hand to defend the Big Blind against a button raise, especially when getting 3-to-1 pot odds (100 to call for a 300 total pot). I will call to see the flop and proceed based on board texture.

On the flop of 4s 9s Qd, I have top two pair (Queens and Nines). This is a very strong hand in a heads-up pot. The board is somewhat coordinated with two spades, so I need to protect my hand and extract value from worse hands like single Queens, Nines, or flush draws. I will check to the raiser, Minni, to give her a chance to continuation bet, which I can then check-raise or call depending on her bet size and the board development.

I was the preflop raiser with a wide heads-up opening range. Flop Qd 9s 4s: my 7s5h has no pair and only weak backdoor equity (needs runner-runner spades/straights), but as the aggressor in HU I should c-bet frequently to take advantage of many missed hands in the BB’s calling range. Flash checked, showing weakness or willingness to float — a half-pot continuation bet will fold out many unpaired broadways and small cards while keeping the pot manageable if called. I’ll bet 200 (half the pot) to apply pressure; plan is to fold to a significant check-raise since my hand has little showdown value, and if called proceed cautiously on the turn: only continue aggressively if I pair or pick up a spade, otherwise check and control pot size.

I have top two pair (Queens and Nines) on a board that contains a flush draw (two spades). This is a very strong hand in a heads-up pot. Minni has made a standard continuation bet. I will check-raise to build the pot and extract value from worse hands like a Queen with a lower kicker, straight draws (like J-T), or flush draws, while also protecting my hand against potential overcards or completed draws on the turn.

I c-bet this dry Q♦9♠4♠ flop as the preflop aggressor, exactly as planned. Flash check‑raised big (from my 200 to 600). My hand (7♠5♥) has no pair and only a runner‑runner spade/straight possibility — essentially no real equity vs a check‑raise range that includes Qx, 9x, sets, strong spade combos and some value Ax/Kx hands. Calling 400 into the 1,200 pot requires ≈25% equity; I’m far below that. Raising is spewy and unnecessary with no showdown value. Fold and preserve my stack for better spots.

I am on the button with 54o, which is a weak hand. However, we are playing heads-up at this stage of the game. Minni has been quite active, but my stack is significantly larger than hers (over 2:1). With only 10 hands left, I want to maintain my lead and avoid unnecessary risks with marginal hands. Folding 54o on the button is a standard tight play to avoid getting entangled in a pot with a very weak hand, although in some heads-up strategies, it could be a call or a raise. Given the current game state and my stack advantage, folding is the safest way to preserve my lead.

Heads-up on the button with 5♣6♦ is a playable, positionally-advantaged hand. I want to take the initiative preflop and deny the big blind a cheap flop — while keeping the pot manageable with deep stacks. I’ll open to a standard heads-up size (≈3x BB) to pressure Flash’s defending range. If he calls I’ll play postflop in position (c-bet many dry boards, slow down on coordinated/overcard boards); if he 3-bets to a large size I’ll fold 56o rather than get committed out of position. Raise to 300 keeps options flexible and exploits heads-up dynamics.

I am in the big blind with Jc6d and Minni has raised to 300 from the button/small blind. This hand is a bit weak to defend against a 3x open, especially as it doesn't flop particularly well and I am out of position. Given our current stack sizes and the stage of the game, I should prioritize higher quality starting hands to protect my lead.

I am on the button with a suited hand (Jd 5d) in a heads-up situation with a significant chip lead. I will put in a standard open raise to 300 to maintain pressure and take control of the hand in position.

Facing a button open to 300 heads-up. I’m in the big blind with K♥6♣ and ~127BB effective. K6o is a reasonable defend heads-up: it has decent showdown equity and can make top-pair hands that extract value. A 3-bet here would be thin and often dominated; folding is too tight. Pot is 400 and I need to add 200 (300 total committed) — calling to see a flop keeps the pot controllable and lets me play post-flop rather than commit with a marginal hand. Plan: proceed cautiously OOP — bet/extract on Kx, pursue draws selectively, fold to heavy aggression on dry boards.

Heads-up vs a wide button opening. Flop As 8s Kc and I hold K♥6♣ — top pair with a weak kicker but an Ace on board means villain has plenty of A‑x combos that beat me and some Kx that I can still extract value from. I want value/protection without bloating the pot OOP, so I’ll take the initiative with a half‑pot lead to charge worse kings and many broadway/medium pairs while denying free turn cards. Size = 300 (half the 600 pot). Plan: if Flash calls, proceed cautiously on the turn and value-bet safe cards; if he raises big I’ll give credit to combos with an Ace/better kicker and fold or re-evaluate based on sizing.

On a board of As 8s Kc, I have Jack-high with no direct draws. Minni has led out with a donk bet of 300 into the 600 pot. While this board generally favors my pre-flop raising range, her lead suggests she either has a piece of the board (like an 8 or a weak King) or is testing me. Given my massive chip lead and the fact that I have zero equity here, there is no reason to float or bluff-raise. I will fold and move on to the next hand.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the SB) with 8♦7♦ — a suited connector that plays very well in position. Pot is 150 and it’s only 50 to complete, but I want to take the initiative, build the pot when I have good postflop options, and apply fold equity. A standard 3x BB open to 300 accomplishes that: it’s large enough to fold out some of BB’s weakest holdings and keeps the pot controllable if I get called. If Flash 3-bets huge I’ll largely fold this speculative hand; versus a small 3-bet I’ll consider calling to play postflop in position and leverage my equity and IP. Raising to 300 maximizes my edge in this spot while preserving flexibility postflop.

I am holding Ace-King suited, which is a premium hand. Minni has raised to 300 from the small blind. Given the strength of my hand and my previous betting patterns where I consistently 3-bet to 1000 with strong linear ranges, I will put in a raise to build the pot and take the initiative in the hand.

I opened to 300 with 8♦7♦ (a suited connector) and Flash 3-bet to 1000. Heads‑up on the button with deep stacks this hand has excellent postflop playability and blockers to big Ax/Kx combos. The pot is 1300 and I have to call 700 (total 1000), which is about a 35% break‑even equity — reasonable versus Flash’s likely wide/aggressive 3‑betting range seen earlier. A 4‑bet here would be a thin, poorly‑blocked bluff and folding is too tight heads‑up. I’ll call to play the flop in position, c‑bet or float favorable flops and fold to overwhelming aggression when out of equity.

I have flopped top pair with the best possible kicker (TPTK) on a relatively dry King-high board. As the pre-flop 3-better, I have a significant range advantage here. I will lead out with a continuation bet for value, targeting weaker Kings, pocket pairs, or club flush draws in Minni's calling range.

Flop: Kc 5s 3c. I opened pre, Flash 3‑bet to 1000 and I called — now he bets 700 into a ~2700 pot. I have 8♦7♦: missed the flop but I’m in position with decent postflop playability. Calling 700 requires ~21% equity (700/(2700+700)≈20.6%), and versus Flash’s wide/aggressive 3‑bet/c‑bet tendencies I expect to have roughly that equity and good opportunities to float, realise equity, or pick up the pot on later streets. A raise as a blind bluff is thin (I don’t block K or club combinations and he’s sticky), and folding here gives up too much with favorable pot odds. Plan: call, see the turn in position; continue if I pick up a pair or meaningful draw or if he shows weakness, fold to excessive turn aggression if I remain totally blank.

The turn card is the 8 of clubs, which completes potential flush draws that Minni could have called with on the flop (like AcQc or JcTc). While I still have Top Pair Top Kicker (TPTK) with Ace-King, the board has become more coordinated. By checking here, I am exercising pot control. This allows me to see how Minni reacts to the scare card. If she checks back, I am likely still ahead of her King-Queen or King-Jack type hands that are also now wary of the flush. If she bets, I can decide whether to call based on the size of the bet, keeping the pot manageable rather than bloating it and potentially facing a difficult decision against a shove.

Turn: Kc 5s 3c 8c. I paired my 8 (second pair) and have position. Pot is ~3400 and Flash checked to me. Given my prior plan to continue when I improve, a modest value/protection bet is correct: it extracts from worse Kx/Ax, small pairs and bluffs, preserves fold equity, and keeps the pot manageable versus the parts of his 3‑bet range that beat me (overpairs, sets, or a made club flush). A ~1/3 pot sizing gives value while allowing an easy fold if he check‑raises big (which will usually represent a flush or very strong hand). I will bet 1200; if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate the river, if he check‑raises large I’ll fold most of the time.

I have Top Pair Top Kicker with Ace-King. Although the 8 of clubs completes the flush draw, Minni's bet of 1200 into the 3400 pot (making it 4600) is relatively small. I am getting approximately 3.8:1 odds to call. Minni could be betting a weaker King for value (like KQ or KJ), a lower pocket pair, or potentially semi-bluffing with a hand containing a single high club. Folding Top Pair Top Kicker to such a small bet would be overly tight and exploitable. I will call to see the river and re-evaluate.

I have Top Pair Top Kicker with Ace-King. The board contains three clubs, making a flush possible. I checked the turn to control the pot after the third club appeared, and called Minni's relatively small bet. The river is a 4 of hearts, which is a complete brick and does not change the board state. Since I am first to act post-flop in this structure, I will check again. This allows me to see how Minni reacts; if she has a King, she might check behind for a showdown, and if she was bluffing a missed draw, she might bet again. If she bets, I will likely call given the strength of my hand and the pot odds.

I have second pair (8♦7♦ with the board Kc 5s 3c 8c 4h). Flash 3‑bet pre, c‑bet on the flop, then called my modest turn bet — a line consistent with a range containing Kx, pocket pairs/sets, and club draws that could've made a flush on the turn. The river 4♥ changes almost nothing: it gives only unlikely pairs (a lone 4) and doesn’t remove the main hands that beat me (Kx, sets, or a made club flush).

Betting here as a bluff or thin value is unattractive. A bluff needs the villain to fold a fairly large fraction of his calling range; Flash has shown himself sticky and aggressive (likely to call small/medium river bets with Kx or flushes). A small river bet won’t get many worse hands to call and will still be called by most hands that beat me; a large bet requires a fold frequency I don’t trust. My pair of eights has decent showdown value versus missed hands and some floats, so the lowest-variance, highest-expected-value line is to check back and take it to showdown. If Flash now fires a small river bet, I’ll re-evaluate (call vs fold) based on sizing, but with no bet in front of me I’m not risking more chips.

Action: check and see showdown.

I am on the button with a strong suited connector (5c 6c). In heads-up play, this is a very profitable hand to open-raise with to seize the initiative and build a pot. Given my significant chip lead and the proximity to the end of the game, I will continue my aggressive opening strategy with a standard 3x raise to 300.

Heads-up in the BB vs a small‑blind open to 300. Pot is 400; I need to put in 200 more (total commit 300). Q♠5♦ is marginal and often dominated by my opponent’s button raising range, but vs a wide opening range it still has some showdown value. My break‑even equity to call is ~33% (200 into a 600 pot) — reasonable to defend one street from the BB and see a flop cheaply. I don’t want to 3‑bet Q5o as a light bluff (too frequently dominated and Flash is an aggressive player who often fights post‑flop), and folding now gives up good pot odds and I’d be too nitty heads‑up. Plan: call, see flop, proceed cautiously — continue only with clear equity (pair/strong draw) or a favorable read; fold to heavy action when I miss.

Check. I completely missed this monotone 7♥-6♥-4♥ flop with Q♠5♦ — no hearts and only an 8‑out straight draw (~32% to river, ~17% to turn). Flash is an aggressive button opener who will have many heart combos and will c‑bet this board often, so bluffing out here is too risky and will be called by many better hands. I’ll control the pot by checking: if Flash bets small (acceptable pot odds, e.g. ~200 or less) I can call to realize my equity and see a turn; if he fires a large, pot‑sized bet I’ll fold because I lack the equity to continue. If he checks back I get a free card to reevaluate. Amount: 0 (check).

On the flop, I have middle pair (sixes) with an open-ended straight draw (any 3, 5, or 8 completes a straight). However, the board is monotone (all hearts), and I do not have a heart in my hand. Minni has checked to me. I have a strong enough hand to bet for value and protection, but because of the flush possibility and the straight-heavy nature of the board, I want to keep the pot manageable and avoid getting check-raised by a heart. I will bet 300 to continue my aggression and see how Minni responds.

Heads‑up on a monotone 7♥‑6♥‑4♥ flop holding Q♠5♦. I checked and Flash c‑bets 300 into the current pot (pot shown 900); calling 300 gives 300/(900+300)=25% break‑even equity. Raw outs: 3 Qs + 3 fives + all 3s and 8s for the 4‑card straight (8) → ~14 raw outs, ~51% to hit by river. Some of those outs are “dirty” (heart cards that can give Flash a flush or that still lose to better pairs), so my realistic realizable equity vs a heart‑heavy c‑bet range is lower (roughly mid‑30s to low‑40s %), but still comfortably above the 25% threshold. Check‑raising as a bluff is unattractive on this texture (opener’s range contains many hearts and high cards), and folding gives up positive‑EV pot odds. Plan: call 300 to realize equity and see a turn; fold to large pot‑sized aggression if I miss, and play aggressively if I pair or hit a clean straight/strong improvement.

Check. I have Q♠5♦ on 7♥-6♥-4♥-4♣ — I missed hard and have no pair, no heart, only Q-high. Flash’s line (button open, c-bet flop and turn) is polarized toward hearts, pairs (7/6), or 4x/trips and will call a river bluff too often. The pot is 1,200; a pure bluff would need a high fold frequency (e.g. a 600-chip bluff requires ~33% folds) — given his line and aggro profile that’s unlikely. Betting risks getting called by better hands and creating a multi-street confrontation I’m not equipped to win. I’ll check and preserve stack; plan to fold to any meaningful river bet and only continue if he checks back or shows a clear “missed” bluff.

The turn card is the 4c, pairing the board. My hand is now two pair (sixes and fours with a seven kicker). However, the board remains very dangerous due to the three hearts present. Minni's check-call on the flop suggests she could be holding a flush draw, a seven, or perhaps even a four. By checking back, I control the size of the pot, avoid a potential check-raise, and get to see a free river card to potentially improve my hand to a straight or simply reach showdown with my current pair.

Board: 7♥ 6♥ 4♥ 4♣ 7♦ — two pair on board. I hold Q♠5♦ with no pair and no hearts, so I have essentially no showdown value vs a reasonable continuing range. Flash (button) c-bet the flop and I called; he then checked the turn. That line is consistent with strong heart draws/made flushes, single pairs (7x/6x/4x) or pot‑control with a made hand. A river bluff would need a high fold frequency (e.g. a 600 chip bluff needs ~33% folds) — given Flash’s aggression and range I’m unlikely to get that. Betting risks being called by any hand that beats me; checking preserves my stack and allows me to fold to any river bet. Plan: check the river and fold to a bet; if he checks back I’ll see showdown (likely lose but conserve chips).

On the river, the board has double-paired (Sevens and Fours). My hand is now two pair, Sevens and Fours, with a Six kicker (from the 6c in my hand). This means any hand Minni has with a card higher than a Six (like Ace-high, King-high, or an Eight) beats me. Given Minni's check-calls on the flop and her checks on the turn and river, she likely has a marginal hand or a missed draw. While many of those missed draws (like Ace-high or King-high hearts) actually beat my Six kicker, I don't believe a bluff will be consistently effective enough here to warrant the risk, especially since Ace-high will often call a small-to-medium bet on such a board. Checking back to reach showdown is the safest and most prudent play.

Heads-up on the button with pocket fives is a clear open — strong enough to raise and take advantage of position. I want to build the pot and get value from worse hands while folding out some hands that have decent equity. A standard 2x BB open to 200 total is fine here (I’ve already posted the 50 SB, so I’ll add 150). With ~95BB effective I can call or 4-bet as appropriate vs a 3-bet, and postflop I’ll c-bet most dry flops and control the pot on dangerous overcard boards.

Holding a pair of nines heads-up is a very strong position. Minni has opened with a min-raise, and I want to build the pot for value while also protecting my hand. A 3-bet to 700 is a standard sizing in this situation to take the initiative and potentially win the pot immediately or narrow Minni's range.

Heads-up, I opened to 200 and Flash 3-bet to 700. I have pocket 5s with ~93BB effective — a small pair is worth seeing a flop in position vs a likely wide 3-bet range. Pot is 900; it costs me 500 to call (700 total), leaving a pot of 1400 and giving ~35.7% immediate pot odds, but with deep stacks I get excellent implied odds to set-mine and can realize equity in position. A min 4-bet to 1200 is too thin: it won’t fold out enough of his 3-bet range and puts me in a poor spot if called. Plan: call to 700 total, play the flop in position — fold to extreme pressure on dry overcard flops, extract maximum if I hit a set or favorable texture.

On a flop of K-T-3, my pair of nines is now a marginal made hand. This board hits many hands in Minni's 3-bet calling range, such as Kx and Tx. As the pre-flop aggressor, checking here allows me to control the pot and see how Minni reacts. Betting might force out hands I beat while keeping in hands that have me crushed.

Flop is K♦ T♣ 3♣ — two overcards to my 5♠5♦ and a fairly dry texture. Flash checked from a wide 3‑betting range, so I can try to take the pot down now or get called by worse hands while keeping the pot small. I’ll make a small continuation bet (about 1/3 pot) to apply pressure to ace‑highs and other unpaired hands, retain fold equity, and avoid bloating the pot out of position if I’m against a K/T or a set. If he check‑raises or applies significant pressure I’ll fold this underpair; if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn and re-evaluate. Betting small preserves my ability to fold to aggression and still realize equity if I hit.

Minni's bet of 500 into a 1400 pot is quite small, roughly one-third of the pot. While the board has two overcards (King and Ten) to my pocket nines, Minni's calling range for a 3-bet pre-flop includes many hands that I still beat, such as lower pocket pairs or suited connectors that missed. Raising here would likely only be called by hands that have me beat (Kx or Tx), so calling is the most prudent action to control the pot size and see how the turn develops. I am getting good odds to see another card.

The turn 4d is a total blank. My pocket nines remain a marginal made hand, likely behind a King or a Ten but ahead of most of Minni's bluffs or draws. I check-called the flop, and checking the turn is the most consistent and safest way to proceed. It keeps the pot manageable and allows me to see how Minni responds to another blank card. Betting here doesn't accomplish much as I'm unlikely to be called by worse and will almost certainly be called by better.

Check. I have an underpair (5♠5♦) on K♦ T♣ 3♣ 4♦ and the turn is essentially a brick for my hand. Flash is a wide 3‑bettor who check‑called my small flop c‑bet, so his range contains Kx/Tx, some pocket pairs and club draws — many hands that beat me or will continue as callers. Betting the turn gains little value (called by draws/better) and risks bloating a pot I don’t want to commit to with only a pair of fives. With ~8.4k effective and a 2.4k pot, standard prudent play is pot control: check to see a river, preserve my equity to hit a set, and avoid facing a large turn raise. I’ll re-evaluate on the river depending on the card and his action (fold to heavy pressure, value bet if river pairs me or he shows clear weakness).

The river is the Jack of Clubs, which is another overcard to my pocket nines and completes a potential club flush. The board now reads K-J-T-4-3. My nines are essentially a bluff catcher now, as I am losing to any King, Jack, or Ten, as well as any flush or straight (AQ). Minni's check on the turn after betting the flop suggests she might have a marginal hand like a Ten or was drawing. Since I am unlikely to get better hands to fold or worse hands to call a bet, checking to see if I can get to a cheap showdown is the best strategy. If Minni bets, I will likely fold given how much of her range now connects with this board.

Line review: I called a 3‑bet preflop and c‑bet the K♦ T♣ 3♣ flop; Flash called. I checked the blank 4♦ turn and he checked back. River J♣ pairs the board and completes a three‑club texture — my 5s remain an underpair and haven’t improved. Flash’s line (wide 3‑better, flop call, checks turn and river) is heavily weighted toward Kx/TX/pocket pairs and some club draws; those hands either already beat me or will call a river bluff. A river bet has minimal fold equity vs the hands that matter and will simply get value‑called by better holdings, so this is a pure pot‑control spot. Action: check to showdown (and fold to any large raise).
I have a very strong starting hand (Ace-Nine suited) in a heads-up situation. I will open with a standard 3x raise to build the pot and take control of the hand from the button.

Heads-up vs a button open to 300 (3x). I have J♠3♦ in the big blind with ~82BB effective. J3o is marginal but defensible heads-up — it's often correct to defend rather than fold. 3-betting this particular hand OOP is too ambitious (dominated vs calling/4-bet ranges and creates difficult postflop decisions). Calling 200 more (300 total committed) keeps the pot manageable, lets me realize equity and see a flop where J-high or backdoor pickups can be playable; I'll fold to heavy aggression on dry flops and continue with top pair or good draws.

I check. Heads-up vs a standard 3x open I defended preflop with J♠3♦; the flop K♣–T♦–3♥ gives me only a single (bottom) pair with a mediocre kicker and limited improvement (roughly 5 outs, ~20% to river). Flash’s opening range contains a lot of Kx/Tx and broadway combos that dominate me, and I’m OOP with ~80BB — I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal holding. Checking controls the pot and lets me call small-to-medium continuation bets to realize equity (I’ll call roughly ≤1/2 pot ≈ 300) but fold to heavy aggression (pot-sized or larger). If Flash checks back I get to see a cheap turn; if I improve to two pair or better on the turn I’ll switch to value betting/raising.

I have Ace-high with a backdoor nut flush draw on a King-high board. As the pre-flop aggressor, I will place a standard continuation bet of half the pot to maintain pressure and potentially take the pot down immediately, or to set up a semi-bluff on future streets if I pick up more equity.

Call 300. I have J♠3♦ on Kc–Td–3h: bottom pair (a marginal but real hand) and this flop bet is small (~1/3 pot). Calling 300 into a 900 pot gives ~5:1 pot odds (≈16.7% required equity). I have ~5 clean outs (3 jacks + 2 threes) to make two pair or trips (~20% to improve by the river) and Flash’s wide button opening/c-bet range includes enough bluffs and dominated holdings to make a call + realization of equity profitable. I’m OOP with ~80BB effective so I don’t want to inflate the pot with a raise — calling keeps the pot controllable and preserves fold equity on later streets. Plan: call the flop, re-evaluate on the turn — if I pair up (J or 3) I will pursue value; if a blank comes and Flash barrels pot-sized or larger I’ll fold.

Check. I have J♠3♦ — bottom pair (3s) on 3♥ K♣ T♦ T♣. I called the small flop c-bet to realize equity, but the turn paired the Ten and didn’t improve me. Pot is 1,200 and I have only ~5 outs (~11%) to hit on the river. With one card to come a realistic turn bet (e.g. 300) requires ~16.7% equity to profitably call (300/(1200+600)), which is well above my raw equity — so calling normal bet sizes is -EV unless villain is bluffing heavily. Flash opens and c-bets wide but also has lots of Kx/Tx and broadway value that dominate my hand. I’ll check to control the pot and force Flash to show aggression; I will only call an extremely tiny bet (≈≤170) based purely on pot odds, otherwise I’ll fold to meaningful barrels. If he checks back I’ll reevaluate the river and extract value if I pair up (J or 3).

The turn pairs the Ten, which changes the board texture significantly. My Ace-high still has some showdown value against missed straight draws like QJ or J9. However, after Minni called the flop bet, she likely has a King, a Ten, or a pair. Continuing to bet here is risky as it's unlikely to fold out better hands, and I could face a check-raise. I will check behind to control the pot size and see if I can improve on the river or win a small pot at showdown.

I hold Js3d — bottom pair (pair of threes) on 3h–Kc–Td–Tc–8d. Villain opened pre, c‑bet the flop (small) and then checked the turn when the Ten paired the board. That line is consistent with Kx for pot control, Tx (now very strong) or a mixed set of missed broadway combos/bluffs. The river 8d is a pure blank for me — I didn’t improve and my hand is rarely ahead of Villain’s value range. Betting here would be a thin bluff that needs fold equity I don’t trust: Flash opens wide and has shown willingness to call or pot‑control rather than fold to small probes often. With ~82BB effective and a 1,200 pot, the lowest‑risk +EV line is to check and go to showdown; I’ll fold to any meaningful river raise or bet and only consider calling an extremely tiny probe that offers correct pot odds. Check and show down.

On the river board of 3h-Kc-Td-Tc-8d, I hold Ace-high. Minni checked on the turn and has now checked again on the river. My Ace-high has some showdown value against missed straight draws like QJ or J9. Betting here is unlikely to get a King or a Ten to fold, and I'm not getting called by any worse hands. Checking back is the most prudent play to see if my Ace-high is good enough to win the pot at showdown.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the SB) with 5h3h suited. Pot is 150 and it’s only 50 to complete; however in heads-up play with deep stacks and position I should be opening very wide. 5-3s is playable postflop and has blockers/flush potential. A min 2x raise to 200 (200 total) takes the initiative, denies the BB cheap equity, and lets me play the flop in position. Flash defends widely but is also aggressive — this size will pick up folds and isolate when I’m often ahead; if he 3-bets big I can fold and preserve chips. Calling 50 is too passive and folding is too tight. Raise to 200 to seize initiative and play position postflop.

I am in the Big Blind with 8s 6s, a decent suited connector. Minni has raised to 200 from the Button. Given the pot odds (calling 100 to win a total pot of 300) and the playability of my hand, this is a standard call to see a flop. I have a significant stack lead and want to play reasonably solid poker as we approach the final hands of the match.

On this flop of 5s As 6h, I have middle pair (sixes) and an 8-high flush draw. This is a very strong hand in a heads-up pot. Since I am out of position, I will start with a check to see how Minni reacts. My hand has significant equity and can comfortably call a bet or even consider a check-raise depending on sizing, but checking first is the standard approach to realize my equity or trap a bluff.

I have 5♥3♥ on A♠5♠6♥ — a second pair (pair of fives) with a weak kicker, in position. Flash checked after calling my preflop open; heads‑up this is a good spot to take the initiative with a value/protection c‑bet. Many of Flash’s calling hands (Kx/Qx/lower pairs and draws) will call a modest bet, while A‑x hands that beat me will often either check‑call or occasionally check‑raise. I want to extract value and deny free cards but keep the pot controllable because an Ace on board is a common dominator for my range. Betting about half the pot (200 into 400) achieves that balance: it gets called by worse hands and draws, folds pure air, and keeps me flexible — I can fold to a large check‑raise or proceed cautiously on the turn if he just calls.

I have middle pair (sixes) and an 8-high flush draw on an Ace-high board. This is a very strong hand in heads-up play with significant equity. I am choosing to check-raise here to put pressure on Minni's continuation betting range and to build the pot in case I improve to a flush or two pair/trips. Even if she holds an Ace, I have many outs to improve. Raising to 700 is a standard size that forces her to make a decision with her marginal hands.

I have second pair (5♥3♥ on A♠5♠6♥) but a very weak kicker and effectively only two clean outs to improve (the remaining fives) plus a remote runner‑runner heart possibility. I bet 200 and Flash check‑raised to 700 — a large, polarizing sizing that heavily favors value (Ax and sets) over bluffs. To call I must put 500 more into a 1300 pot, which requires ~28% equity (500/(1300+500)), and my real equity versus a value‑weighted check‑raise range is well below that. Raising myself would overcommit with an inferior hand and is likely to get me further exploited by A‑x or sets. Fold and preserve the stack for a better, clearer spot.

With only three hands remaining in the match and a substantial lead over Minni, my primary objective is to minimize risk and avoid unnecessary large pots. Although 8d 2d is suited and I have positional advantage as the button, it is a very weak hand. I will choose to fold and protect my lead, as I don't need to contest this pot to secure my overall standing.

8♣4♠ is a very weak offsuit hand — poor playability and little showdown value. The pot is 150 and I only need to call 50 more (making my total committed 100), so the price to see a flop is cheap (pot odds ~3:1). I could min-raise as a steal, but versus Flash’s deep, often-defending stack that’s a marginal bluff and likely to get called or 3-bet. I’ll call the 50 to see a flop cheaply, and plan to fold to significant preflop 3-bets or major postflop aggression unless I pair or pick up a strong draw. This keeps the pot small and preserves my stack for the last hand.

With a significant lead in the stack and only two hands remaining in the game, there is no strategic advantage to bloating the pot with a weak hand like Jack-Trey offsuit. Since Minni limped in from the small blind, checking allows me to see the flop for free while maintaining my chip advantage.

I am in the Big Blind with a weak hand (Jack-Trey offsuit) on a flop of Ten-Deuce-Five. Given that I have a massive chip lead and there are only two hands remaining in the match, my primary objective is to avoid unnecessary risk and preserve my stack. With no pair and no real draw, I will check and see if I can get to a cheap showdown or fold if facing significant aggression.

Flop: T♠ 2♥ 5♠. I hold 8♣4♠ — no pair and only a very weak backdoor spade (would need both turn+river spades). Flash checked, so his checked range is wide (many missed overcards, some small pairs, bluffs). Pot is 200 and the minimum bet is 100 (~½ pot). In heads‑up, in position, I have range advantage and can apply pressure to win the pot outright. A small c‑bet (100) buys the pot a large portion of the time, costs very little relative to my 8.5k stack, and keeps future options open: if called I still have position and can proceed cautiously on the turn; if I face a check‑raise I can fold and preserve my stack. Given the board texture and stack dynamics, a minimum-size bluff is the +EV, low-risk line here.

With only two hands remaining and a massive chip lead of over 20,000, my goal is to protect my stack and ensure I finish the session with the maximum possible lead. On this flop, I have only Jack-high with no pair and no real draws. Minni has bet 100 into the pot. While the bet is small, there is no strategic benefit to calling with such a weak hand when I can simply fold and maintain my dominant position without any risk. I will fold and move on to the final hand.

On the final hand of the game, I hold Jh 7h on the button. This is a strong enough hand to open for a raise in heads-up play. With a significant chip lead, I'll put in a standard raise to 300 to maintain pressure and try to take down the blinds or play a controlled pot.

Heads-up vs the button’s open to 300, K♦Q♥ is too good to muck. Calling 200 keeps us out of position on the flop against a likely wide, aggressive opening range. I prefer to take the initiative and 3‑bet for value and fold equity: KQ is ahead of most of Flash’s button range and a sized 3‑bet will fold out weaker broadways, small pairs, and some suited hands while still allowing post‑flop maneuvering if called. I’ll make it a standard-sized 3‑bet to 1,200 total — large enough to get meaningful folds and define the hand, but small enough relative to our 86bb stack to play the flop/draws comfortably if called. If Flash 4‑bets big I’ll re‑evaluate (likely fold to an oversized 4‑bet, call against moderate sizing).

On the final hand of the match, I hold Jh 7h and have already secured a massive chip lead. Minni has 3-bet to 1,200, which is a 4x raise over my initial open. While J7s is a playable hand in heads-up, the objective is to maximize the stack over 150 hands. Given this is the very last hand, there is no long-term strategic value in taking a high-variance spot against a large 3-bet. Folding ensures I protect my substantial winnings and conclude the game with a stack of over 30,000 chips.
